$$\delta = K \cdot \frac{L^2 \Delta\alpha \, \Delta T}{t}$$

where $K$ is a constant (≈ 0.75 for flat, ≈ 1.0 for helical).

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Trade-off:** To increase sensitivity, you can:
> - Increase $L$ (longer strip)
> - Use a material pair with **larger $\Delta\alpha$** (e.g., brass–invar)
> - Reduce $t$ (thinner strip)
> But thinner strips are mechanically fragile and have higher thermal lag.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Liquid-Filled | Vapour Pressure | Gas-Filled | Bimetallic | RTD (Pt100) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Output** | Column height | Pressure | Pressure | Displacement | Resistance (Ω) |
| **Scale** | Linear | **Non-linear** | Linear | Linear | Linear (approx.) |
| **Range (°C)** | −200 to +600 | −40 to +350 | −270 to +1500 | −50 to +500 | −200 to +850 |
| **Accuracy** | Moderate | Low–Moderate | High | Low–Moderate | **Very High** |
| **Remote Reading** | Not possible | Yes (Bourdon) | Yes (Bourdon) | Not possible | Yes (4-wire) |
| **Power Required** | None | None | None | None | **Yes (mA excitation)** |
| **Cost** | Very low | Low | Moderate | Low | **High** |
| **Industrial Use** | Lab/field | Process industries | High-temp furnaces | Thermostats, dial gauges | Standard in DCS/PLC |

    P4 --> P5[Output Stage Display mA Voltage DCS PLC]

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Every thermometer type in this module performs only the first two blocks (sensor + transduction). The remaining blocks (conditioning, output, control) are added by the *instrumentation engineer*. This is why **RTDs are preferred in modern DCS systems** — they integrate seamlessly with electronic signal conditioning.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Explain the working principle of a vapour pressure thermometer with a neat sketch. Mention two advantages and one disadvantage.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A vapour pressure thermometer is a filled-system thermometer in which the bulb is partially filled with a **volatile liquid** (e.g., methyl chloride, ether, or butane), and the remaining volume contains the **saturated vapour** of that liquid in equilibrium with it. The pressure of this saturated vapour is a **monotonically increasing function of temperature**, given approximately by the Clausius–Clapeyron relation:

$$\ln P = -\frac{L}{RT} + C$$

As the process temperature changes, the vapour pressure changes correspondingly, and this pressure is transmitted through a capillary to a **Bourdon tube**, which converts it into a mechanical pointer deflection. The scale is non-linear (exponential in $1/T$).

**Advantages:** (i) High sensitivity near saturation, (ii) Compact and low cost.
**Disadvantage:** Non-linear scale, requires individual calibration.

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Why is platinum preferred over copper and nickel for industrial RTDs?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Platinum is preferred for industrial RTDs because of:
1. **High chemical inertness** — does not oxidize or corrode, even at high temperatures.
2. **Excellent linearity** — the resistance–temperature curve is nearly linear over a wide range.
3. **High stability and reproducibility** — essential for use as a primary standard.
4. **Wide temperature range** (−200 °C to +850 °C) — the largest among common RTD metals.
5. **High resistivity** (10.6 μΩ·cm) — provides a good signal level.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write *only* "Pt is non-corrosive" and lose 1.5 marks. Always list at least 3 distinct reasons in a comparison question.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** With the help of a labelled diagram, explain the construction and working of a **bimetallic thermometer**. Derive the expression for its deflection.

**Model Solution:**

**Construction:**
A bimetallic thermometer consists of two strips of different metals (typically **Invar** with $\alpha_1 \approx 1.2 \times 10^{-6}\,/°C$ and **Brass** with $\alpha_2 \approx 19 \times 10^{-6}\,/°C$) firmly bonded together along their entire length. The composite strip is wound into a **helical coil** with the higher-expansion metal on the outer side. One end of the coil is rigidly fixed to the thermometer housing, while the free end is attached via a shaft to a pointer moving over a calibrated dial.

**Working:**
When the temperature of the strip rises, the brass expands more than the invar. Because the two metals are bonded, the strip cannot expand freely. Instead, it **bends** (or unwinds) towards the invar side. This rotation is transmitted via the shaft to the pointer, indicating the temperature on the dial.

**Derivation of Deflection:**

For a flat bimetallic strip of length $L$, thickness $t$, and equal layer thicknesses, the free-end deflection is:

$$\delta = \frac{3 L^2 (\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \Delta T}{16 t}$$

For a helical coil, the **angular deflection** $\theta$ (in radians) is:

$$\theta = \frac{360 L (\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \Delta T}{\pi t \cdot d}$$

where $d$ is the diameter of the helix. The corresponding **linear tip deflection** becomes:

$$\delta = K \cdot \frac{L^2 (\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \Delta T}{t}$$

with $K \approx 1$ for a well-designed helical coil.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Compare bimetallic thermometers with **liquid-in-glass thermometers** in terms of range, accuracy, and applications.

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | Bimetallic Thermometer | Liquid-in-Glass Thermometer |
|---|---|---|
| **Range (°C)** | −50 to +500 | −200 to +600 (mercury) |
| **Accuracy** | ±1 to ±5 % of full scale | ±0.1 to ±2 % of full scale |
| **Output** | Mechanical (pointer) | Mechanical (column height) |
| **Remote Reading** | Limited (can drive switches) | **Not possible** |
| **Robustness** | Excellent (no glass) | Fragile (glass envelope) |
| **Application** | Oven thermostats, dial gauges, circuit breakers | Laboratory thermometry, field calibration |
| **Cost** | Moderate | Very low |

Bimetallic thermometers are **preferred in industrial environments** (ovens, HVAC, hot water tanks) because of their robustness, while liquid-in-glass thermometers remain the **gold standard for laboratory reference** work.

### Question B: [KTU University Exam - May 2023] — (Internal Choice)

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Derive the **Callendar–Van Dusen equation** for an RTD and use it to find the resistance of a Pt100 sensor at **150 °C**.

**Model Solution:**

The resistance of a metal varies with temperature in a polynomial series. Truncating after the quadratic term for $T \geq 0\,°C$:

$$R_T = R_0 \left( 1 + \alpha T + \beta T^2 \right)$$

For a standard Pt100 RTD, the IEC 60751 coefficients are:

$$R_0 = 100\,\Omega, \quad \alpha = 3.85 \times 10^{-3}\,/°C, \quad \beta = -5.775 \times 10^{-7}\,/°C^2$$

**Step 1: Identify T.**

$$T = 150\,°C$$

**Step 2: Substitute into the formula.**

$$R_{150} = 100 \left[ 1 + (3.85 \times 10^{-3})(150) + (-5.775 \times 10^{-7})(150)^2 \right]$$

**Step 3: Compute the linear term.**

$$3.85 \times 10^{-3} \times 150 = 0.5775$$

**Step 4: Compute the quadratic term.**

$$-5.775 \times 10^{-7} \times 22500 = -0.01299$$

**Step 5: Sum the bracket terms.**

$$1 + 0.5775 - 0.01299 = 1.5645$$

**Step 6: Multiply by $R_0$.**

$$R_{150} = 100 \times 1.5645 = 156.45\,\Omega$$

**Final Answer:** $R_{150} \approx 156.45\,\Omega$ (Experimentally measured value is ≈ 157.31 Ω; the small error is due to truncation after the quadratic term).

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Explain the **3-wire and 4-wire** RTD connection methods. Why is 2-wire connection discouraged in industrial applications?

**Model Solution:**

**2-Wire Connection:**
The RTD is connected to the measuring instrument using two lead wires. The measured resistance is:

$$R_{measured} = R_{RTD} + 2 R_{lead}$$

The lead-wire resistance $R_{lead}$ is temperature-dependent (typically 0.4 Ω per metre for copper) and introduces a **systematic error**, especially for low-resistance RTDs (e.g., Pt100). This is **why 2-wire connection is discouraged** in industrial applications.

**3-Wire Connection (Most Common):**
A third wire is added in parallel with one of the leads, effectively compensating for the lead resistance. The bridge circuit measures:

$$R_{measured} = R_{RTD} + R_{lead} - R_{lead} = R_{RTD}$$

The 3-wire method assumes **identical lead resistance** in all three wires, which is reasonably true for equal-length copper leads. This is the **industry standard** for Pt100 installations.

**4-Wire Connection (Highest Accuracy):**
The RTD is excited by a constant current through two "force" leads, and the voltage drop is measured through two "sense" leads using a high-impedance voltmeter. Because the voltmeter draws negligible current, the voltage drop is **exactly** $I \cdot R_{RTD}$ with **no lead-resistance error**. This is used in **laboratory-grade and Class A** temperature measurements.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** In derivation problems like 3.1–3.3, students often skip the **assumptions** (e.g., "ideal gas behaviour", "uniform temperature", "constant mass"). Always state these explicitly — they carry **at least 1.5 marks** in the rubric.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Temperature** is governed by the **Zeroth Law of Thermodynamics**, not the First or Second.
- [x] All five thermometer types exploit different **physical properties** of matter (volume, pressure, displacement, resistance).
- [x] **Liquid-in-glass thermometers** require **stem correction** for partial immersion.
- [x] **Vapour pressure** thermometers use **saturated two-phase** systems — pressure depends ONLY on temperature, not bulb volume.
- [x] **Gas-filled** thermometers follow the **Ideal Gas Law** ($PV = nRT$) and have a **linear** scale.
- [x] **Bimetallic** thermometers depend on the **differential expansion** of two bonded metals; Invar + Brass is the most common pair.
- [x] **Pt100 RTD** has $R_0 = 100\,\Omega$ at 0 °C and nominal sensitivity of **0.385 Ω/°C**.
- [x] The **Callendar–Van Dusen equation** for $T \geq 0\,°C$: $R_T = R_0(1 + \alpha T + \beta T^2)$.
- [x] **Thermistors** are non-linear; **RTDs are linear** — this is the key distinction.
- [x] **2-wire RTD connection is discouraged**; use 3-wire (industrial) or 4-wire (laboratory) for lead compensation.
- [x] **ITS-90** is the international standard for temperature scale definition; triple point of water = **273.16 K**.
- [x] For KTU numerical problems, remember: $T(K) = T(°C) + 273.15$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** Temperature measurement is a **3-mark/14-mark dual-category topic**. Definitions (e.g., "what is a vapour pressure thermometer?") appear in Part A, while derivations (Clausius–Clapeyron, Callendar–Van Dusen, bimetallic deflection) and comparisons appear in Part B. Master the **formula sheet in Section 2.6** — it covers >80% of all KTU questions on this module.

---
