topics.title: Thermocouple Type: Thermoelectric Principles (Seebeck Effect, Peltier Effect, and Thomson Effect), Laws of Thermoelectricity, Thermocouple Output Measurement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT724 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Thermocouple Type: Thermoelectric Principles (Seebeck Effect, Peltier Effect, and Thomson Effect), Laws of Thermoelectricity, Thermocouple Output Measurement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Thermocouple — Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> A **thermocouple** is a temperature-measuring sensor consisting of two dissimilar metallic conductors joined at one end (the *measuring/hot junction*) and connected to a measuring instrument at the other end (the *reference/cold junction*). It operates on the principle of the **Seebeck Effect**, converting a temperature difference between the two junctions into a measurable electromotive force (EMF), which is then correlated to the unknown temperature using standard thermoelectric reference tables (ITS-90).

The fundamental temperature-sensing phenomenon relies on three interrelated thermoelectric effects, originally discovered between **1821 and 1856**, that govern the conversion between thermal and electrical energy in metallic circuits.

---

### 1.2 The Three Thermoelectric Effects — Intuitive Overview

#### **A) Seebeck Effect (1821 — T. J. Seebeck)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Statement:** *"When two dissimilar metals are joined to form a closed circuit, and the two junctions are maintained at different temperatures, an EMF (and consequently a current) is induced in the circuit. The magnitude of this EMF is a function of the temperature difference and the identity of the two metals."*

**Intuition (Plain English + Real-World Analogy):**
Imagine a closed loop made of two different wires — say, copper and constantan — joined at the two ends. Now warm one joint with a candle while the other sits in an ice bath. A voltmeter connected anywhere in the loop immediately deflects! Energy has been converted directly from heat → electricity with **no moving parts**.
Think of it like a *water wheel between two reservoirs at different heights* — the height difference (temperature difference) drives the flow (current). The thermocouple is the "heat-to-electricity transducer" of the instrumentation world.

* **Seebeck Coefficient (S):** $S = \dfrac{dE}{dT}$ in **μV/°C** — the *sensitivity* of the metal pair.
* **Sign Convention:** $S_{AB} = S_A - S_B$ is positive if current flows from A to B at the hot junction.

---

#### **B) Peltier Effect (1834 — J. C. A. Peltier)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Statement:** *"When an external EMF drives a current through a junction of two dissimilar metals, heat is either absorbed or liberated at the junction, depending on the direction of current flow. The rate of heat absorption/release is proportional to the current."*

**Intuition (Analogy):**
The Seebeck effect is *heat → electricity*. The Peltier effect is the *reverse* — *electricity → heat* (or cold!). When you push a current through a bismuth–antimony junction, one side gets hot and the other gets cold. This is the principle behind **thermoelectric refrigerators (Peltier coolers)** used in portable coolers, CPU coolers, and laboratory-grade temperature-controlled stages.
Think of it as a *one-way valve for heat* that you control with current direction.

* **Peltier Coefficient (π):** $Q = \pi_{AB} \cdot I$ (Watts) at a junction.
* **Units of π:** **Volts (V)** — though conceptually it represents heat per unit charge.

---

#### **C) Thomson Effect (1856 — Lord Kelvin / William Thomson)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Statement:** *"When an electric current flows through a single homogeneous conductor in which a temperature gradient exists, heat is either absorbed or evolved along the length of the conductor (in addition to I²R heating). The effect is reversible."*

**Intuition (Analogy):**
Even with **one single metal wire**, if it has a hot end and a cold end, and you push a current through it, the wire either absorbs extra heat (cools down a bit more) or releases extra heat (warms up) depending on the direction of the current relative to the heat flow. It is the "ghostly cousin" of the Seebeck and Peltier effects — you don't need two metals, just one metal with a temperature slope.

* **Thomson Coefficient (σ):** Heat absorbed per unit length per unit current per °C.
* **Units of σ:** **V/°C** (or μV/°C, same dimension as Seebeck coefficient).

---

### 1.3 Summary Snapshot — The Thermoelectric Trinity

| **Effect** | **Energy Conversion** | **Discovered By** | **Year** | **Driving Condition** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Seebeck | Heat → Electricity | T. J. Seebeck | **1821** | Temperature gradient at junction |
| Peltier | Electricity → Heat/Cold | J. C. A. Peltier | **1834** | Current through junction |
| Thomson | Current + Gradient → Heat | Lord Kelvin | **1856** | Current + gradient in one metal |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Trick:** *“SPT — Seebeck Produces Thermoelectricity, Peltier Pumps Temperature, Thomson Treats a single conductor.”*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Seebeck Effect — Operational Breakdown

1. **Two Dissimilar Metals (A & B)** are joined at the **Hot Junction (T_H)** and connected to a measuring instrument at the **Cold/Reference Junction (T_C)**.
2. Electrons at the hot end gain thermal kinetic energy and diffuse toward the cold end.
3. Because metals A and B have **different electron densities and Fermi levels**, electrons diffuse at **different rates** in the two wires.
4. This differential diffusion creates a **net potential difference** (EMF) between the open ends of the wires.
5. The induced EMF depends on:
   * The temperature difference $\Delta T = T_H - T_C$
   * The identity of metals A and B (material property — Seebeck coefficient)
   * The *integrity* of the junctions (law of intermediate metals — see §2.4)

**Key Physical Constants (K-Type Chromel–Alumel):**
* Sensitivity ≈ **40 μV/°C** at room temperature
* Useful range: **−200 °C to +1350 °C**
* Standardized under **ITS-90** (International Temperature Scale of 1990)

---

### 2.2 The Peltier Effect — Operational Breakdown

1. An **external DC source** drives a current $I$ through a junction of metals A and B.
2. Electrons crossing from A → B must either **gain energy** (from lattice vibrations = cooling effect) or **lose energy** (to lattice = heating effect).
3. The energy exchange per unit charge is the **Peltier coefficient π_AB** (units: V).
4. **Rate of heat absorbed/released:** $\dot{Q} = \pi_{AB} \cdot I$ (W)
5. **Sign:** $\pi_{AB} = \pi_A - \pi_B$; positive if heat is absorbed when current flows from A → B.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction:** Peltier heat is **linear in I** (not I²), which distinguishes it from the always-present Joule heating (I²R losses). Peltier is the *useful* reversible effect.

---

### 2.3 The Thomson Effect — Operational Breakdown

1. A current $I$ flows through a **single, homogeneous wire** with temperatures $T_1$ and $T_2$ at its ends.
2. Heat is absorbed (or released) along the wire at a rate per unit length proportional to:
   * The current $I$
   * The temperature gradient $\dfrac{dT}{dx}$
   * The Thomson coefficient $\sigma$ of the material
3. **Differential form of Thomson heat:** $dQ_T = \sigma \cdot I \cdot \left(\dfrac{dT}{dx}\right) \cdot dt$
4. **Sign of σ:** Positive when heat is absorbed if current flows from cold to hot end.

**Kelvin's Onsager Relation (links all three effects):**
$$S_{AB} = \frac{\pi_{AB}}{T}$$
$$\sigma_A = T \cdot \frac{dS_A}{dT}$$

These relations arise from **thermodynamic reversibility** (Onsager reciprocal relations, 1931) and confirm that all three thermoelectric effects are not independent.

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — these equations appear in nearly every KTU numerical on thermocouples.**

| **#** | **Equation** | **Meaning** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E = \int_{T_C}^{T_H} S_{AB}(T)\, dT$ | Total Seebeck EMF generated | mV |
| 2 | $S_{AB} = S_A - S_B$ | Relative Seebeck coefficient | μV/°C |
| 3 | $E \approx S_{AB} \cdot (T_H - T_C)$ | Approx. (linear) EMF for small $\Delta T$ | mV |
| 4 | $\dot{Q} = \pi_{AB} \cdot I$ | Rate of Peltier heat | W |
| 5 | $Q_P = \pi_{AB} \cdot I \cdot t$ | Total Peltier heat in time $t$ | J |
| 6 | $\pi_{AB} = T \cdot S_{AB}$ | Kelvin relation (1) | V |
| 7 | $dQ_T = \sigma \cdot I \cdot dT$ | Thomson heat (differential) | J |
| 8 | $E(T_1, T_3) = E(T_1, T_2) + E(T_2, T_3)$ | Law of Intermediate Temperatures | mV |
| 9 | $E_{ABC} = E_{AB}$ if $T_A = T_C$ | Law of Intermediate Metals | mV |

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **Industrial Furnaces & Boilers** (K-type, S-type, R-type) — process industries, steel plants.
* **Aircraft Engine Exhaust Gauges** (high-temperature chromel-alumel).
* **Cryogenics** — T-type (Cu–Constantan) and E-type for sub-zero measurements.
* **Medical Thermometry** — fine-wire thermocouples for tissue/catheter probes.
* **Energy Harvesting** — thermoelectric generators (TEGs) using Seebeck effect to power remote IoT sensors from waste heat.
* **Peltier Coolers** — DNA amplification (PCR machines), laser diode temperature stabilization, portable beverage coolers.
* **Thomson Effect** — used in precise thermoelectric refrigeration modeling and for material characterization (σ is small, but theoretically significant).

---

### 2.6 Laws of Thermoelectricity — Three Foundational Laws

#### **Law 1 — Law of Homogeneous Circuit**
> *“The net EMF developed in a thermocouple circuit depends only on the temperatures of the two junctions and is independent of the temperature distribution along the wires themselves.”*

In other words, a homogeneous wire of uniform composition cannot generate a thermoelectric EMF, no matter how its temperature is distributed. The EMF arises *only* at junctions of dissimilar materials.

#### **Law 2 — Law of Intermediate Metals**
> *“The introduction of a third metal (C) into a thermocouple circuit (A–B) does not alter the net EMF, provided that both ends of the inserted metal C are at the same temperature.”*

This is why you can solder a copper lead wire, attach terminal blocks, and use measuring instruments without disturbing the EMF. **Mathematically:** $E_{ABC} = E_{AB}$ when $T_C$ is the same at both junctions of C.

#### **Law 3 — Law of Intermediate (or Successive) Temperatures**
> *“The EMF generated by a thermocouple with junctions at $T_1$ and $T_3$ is the algebraic sum of the EMFs generated with junctions at $T_1$, $T_2$ and $T_2$, $T_3$.”*

$$E_{AB}(T_1, T_3) = E_{AB}(T_1, T_2) + E_{AB}(T_2, T_3)$$

This is why **standard reference tables** (ITS-90) are built assuming a cold-junction reference of 0 °C — you can then add corrections for any actual reference temperature.

---

### 2.7 Thermocouple Output Measurement — Practical Schemes

The raw Seebeck EMF is in **microvolts to millivolts**, so precision measurement requires care:

1. **High-Input-Impedance Voltmeter** (≥ 10 MΩ) — to avoid loading the high-source-impedance thermocouple.
2. **Cold-Junction Compensation (CJC)** — the cold junction is not at 0 °C in practice; its temperature is measured by a separate sensor (e.g., a silicon diode or RTD), and the corresponding EMF is added to the measured value using the Law of Intermediate Temperatures.
3. **Thermopile (Series Connection)** — multiple thermocouples connected in series multiply the EMF by N (useful for low-ΔT applications like infrared pyrometers and heat flux sensors).
4. **Break Protection & Shielding** — twisted shielded pairs to reject EMI, since the signal is very low-level.
5. **Linearization** — Seebeck EMF vs. temperature is *non-linear*; a polynomial or lookup-table correction is applied:
$$T = a_0 + a_1 E + a_2 E^2 + a_3 E^3 + \cdots + a_n E^n$$
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Seebeck EMF from a Polished Differential View

We start by defining the **absolute Seebeck coefficient** $S_A(T)$ of a material A as the local derivative of the electrochemical potential per unit temperature change:

$$S_A(T) = \frac{1}{q}\frac{d\mu_A}{dT}\bigg\vert_T$$

where $q$ is the electronic charge and $\mu_A$ is the electrochemical potential. The net EMF produced by a closed loop of two metals A and B with junctions at $T_H$ and $T_C$ is obtained by integrating the **local Seebeck field** along the loop:

$$E = \oint \vec{E}_S \cdot d\vec{l}$$

Since the Seebeck field exists only along the temperature gradient inside each conductor, we integrate from cold to hot in metal A and from hot to cold in metal B:

$$E = \int_{T_C}^{T_H} S_A(T)\, dT - \int_{T_C}^{T_H} S_B(T)\, dT$$

Rearranging:

$$E = \int_{T_C}^{T_H} \big[S_A(T) - S_B(T)\big]\, dT = \int_{T_C}^{T_H} S_{AB}(T)\, dT$$

**Step-by-Step Conversion Logic:**

1. The Seebeck field inside each wire is a **scalar** function of the local temperature, not a constant — this is critical because *S* depends on *T*.
2. The minus sign for the B-wire integration comes from the fact that current traversal in B is in the **opposite direction** along the loop.
3. The combined integrand $S_A - S_B$ is the **relative Seebeck coefficient** $S_{AB}(T)$, a material property of the pair.
4. For the simple case where $S_{AB}$ is approximately constant over the range (true for small $\Delta T$):

$$E \approx S_{AB} \cdot (T_H - T_C)$$

This is the **linear approximation** used in KTU exam numericals when no calibration data is provided.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Relationship Between Peltier and Seebeck Coefficients (Kelvin's First Relation)

The Seebeck and Peltier effects are coupled by **thermodynamics**. Consider an elementary thermocouple carrying current $I$ with junctions at $T$ and $T + dT$. The total entropy change in the system must be zero for a reversible process:

* **Heat absorbed at hot junction (Peltier):** $dQ_H = \pi(T + dT) \cdot I \cdot dt$
* **Heat released at cold junction (Peltier):** $dQ_C = \pi(T) \cdot I \cdot dt$
* **Heat absorbed along the wires (Thomson):** $dQ_T = \sigma \cdot I \cdot dT \cdot dt$

For the process to be **reversible**, the net entropy change must vanish:

$$\frac{dQ_H}{T + dT} - \frac{dQ_C}{T} + \int \frac{\sigma \cdot I \cdot dT}{T} = 0$$

Expanding the first two terms and simplifying yields:

$$\frac{\pi(T + dT) - \pi(T)}{T} = -\frac{\pi \cdot dT}{T^2} + \frac{\sigma \cdot dT}{T}$$

Taking the limit $dT \to 0$:

$$\frac{d\pi}{dT} - \frac{\pi}{T} = -\sigma$$

Multiplying through by $-1$ and rearranging:

$$\frac{\pi}{T} - \frac{d\pi}{dT} = \sigma$$

Now, since $S_{AB} = \pi_{AB}/T$ (one form of the Kelvin relation), we differentiate:

$$S_{AB} = \frac{\pi_{AB}}{T} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{dS_{AB}}{dT} = \frac{1}{T}\frac{d\pi_{AB}}{dT} - \frac{\pi_{AB}}{T^2}$$

Comparing with the previous equation:

$$\sigma = -T \cdot \frac{dS_{AB}}{dT}$$

This is **Kelvin's Second Relation**, linking the Thomson coefficient directly to the temperature sensitivity of the Seebeck coefficient. **Physical interpretation:** if the Seebeck coefficient changes rapidly with temperature, the Thomson effect is strong; if $S$ is constant, $\sigma = 0$.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example — KTU-Style Numerical

> **Problem:** A type-K thermocouple (Chromel–Alumel) has its hot junction inserted in a furnace and its cold junction held at $T_C = 25~^\circ\text{C}$. The voltmeter reads $E = 11.723~\text{mV}$. Given that the Seebeck coefficient for the K-type pair at this operating range is approximately $S_K = 41~\mu\text{V}/^\circ\text{C}$, determine the furnace temperature $T_H$.

**Step 1: Convert all units to a consistent base.**

$$E = 11.723~\text{mV} = 11723~\mu\text{V}$$

$$S_K = 41~\mu\text{V}/^\circ\text{C}$$

$$T_C = 25~^\circ\text{C}$$

**Step 2: Apply the linear approximation formula for the Seebeck EMF.**

$$E = S_K \cdot (T_H - T_C)$$

$$11723 = 41 \cdot (T_H - 25)$$

**Step 3: Solve for $T_H$.**

$$T_H - 25 = \frac{11723}{41}$$

$$T_H - 25 = 285.9268...$$

$$T_H = 285.9268 + 25 = 310.9268~^\circ\text{C}$$

**Step 4: Round to engineering significance.**

$$\boxed{T_H \approx 310.93~^\circ\text{C}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note for KTU Examiners:** In the actual exam, students are expected to consult **ITS-90 reference tables** and use the law of intermediate temperatures to find $E(T_C, 0) = E(25~^\circ\text{C}, 0~^\circ\text{C})$, then $E(T_H, 0) = E_{\text{measured}} + E(25, 0)$, and finally read off $T_H$ from the K-type column. The linear approximation is an *engineering estimate*, not the certified procedure.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Numerical Patterns

| **Problem Type** | **Given** | **Find** | **Governing Equation** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Temperature from EMF | $E$, $T_C$, type | $T_H$ | $E = S_{AB}(T_H - T_C)$ |
| EMF with CJC correction | $E_{\text{meas}}$, $T_C$, type | $T_H$ | $E_{T_H,0} = E_{\text{meas}} + E_{T_C,0}$ |
| Thermopile output | $N$ couples, $\Delta T$ | $E_{\text{total}}$ | $E_{\text{total}} = N \cdot S_{AB} \cdot \Delta T$ |
| Peltier heat | $I$, $t$, $\pi_{AB}$ | $Q_P$ | $Q_P = \pi_{AB} \cdot I \cdot t$ |
| Thomson heat | $I$, $\Delta T$, $\sigma$, $L$ | $Q_T$ | $Q_T = \sigma \cdot I \cdot \Delta T \cdot t$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Thermocouple Measurement Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Furnace Process T hot] --> B[Hot Junction A B]
    B --> C[Compensation Lead Wires]
    C --> D[Cold Reference Junction T cold]
    D --> E[Terminal Block Cold Junction Compensation]
    E --> F[Low Pass EMI Filter]
    F --> G[High Impedance Differential Amplifier Gain 1000]
    G --> H[ADC 16 bit Resolution]
    H --> I[Microcontroller Linearization ITS 90 Table]
    I --> J[Display Temperature in degree Celsius]
    E --> K[Reference Sensor RTD or Silicon Diode]
    K --> I
    H --> L[Output 4 to 20 mA or Digital RS485]
```

**Interpretation of the Flowchart:**

1. The **Hot Junction** senses the unknown furnace/process temperature.
2. **Compensation lead wires** carry the millivolt signal out of the hot zone, while a **Cold Reference Junction** (often at the instrument terminal block) is kept at a known reference temperature.
3. A separate **RTD or silicon diode sensor** measures the cold-junction temperature; its reading is fed into the linearization algorithm — this is the **Cold-Junction Compensation (CJC)** step.
4. The signal is **filtered** to reject 50/60 Hz mains pickup and **amplified** by a high-impedance differential amplifier (essential because thermocouple source impedance is non-negligible).
5. A high-resolution **16-bit ADC** digitizes the signal.
6. A **microcontroller** consults the **ITS-90 polynomial** for the appropriate thermocouple type (K, J, T, etc.) and converts the millivolt reading into a temperature in °C.
7. The final reading is displayed and/or transmitted as a **4–20 mA analog** or **RS-485 digital** output for the DCS/PLC.

---

### 4.2 Functional Architecture of the Three Thermoelectric Effects

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SEEBECK["Seebeck Effect 1821"]
        S1[Heat Source at T hot] --> S2[Junction of Two Diss dissimilar Metals A and B]
        S2 --> S3[Temperature Difference T hot minus T cold]
        S3 --> S4[Net EMF on Open Circuit]
        S4 --> S5[Voltmeter Deflection]
    end

    subgraph PELTIER["Peltier Effect 1834"]
        P1[External DC Source EMF] --> P2[Current I forced through Junction]
        P2 --> P3{Heat Absorbed or Released}
        P3 --> P4[Cooling side Junction 1]
        P3 --> P5[Heating side Junction 2]
    end

    subgraph THOMSON["Thomson Effect 1856"]
        T1[Single Homogeneous Conductor] --> T2[Temperature Gradient along Length]
        T2 --> T3[Current I flows through wire]
        T3 --> T4[Reversible Heat evolved or absorbed]
        T4 --> T5[Thomson Coefficient sigma]
    end
```

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Flowchart — Application of the Three Laws of Thermoelectricity

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Thermocouple Circuit A and B] --> B{Law 1 Homogeneous}
    B -->|No EMF if one metal| C[EMF depends only on Junction Temperatures]
    A --> D{Law 2 Intermediate Metal}
    D -->|Insert Metal C at uniform T| E[EMF unchanged E ABC equals E AB]
    A --> F{Law 3 Intermediate Temperatures}
    F --> G[Sum of EMFs across sub ranges]
    G --> H[E T1 T3 equals E T1 T2 plus E T2 T3]
    H --> I[Used for Reference Table Calibration and Cold Junction Compensation]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading Tip for KTU Students:** Law 2 is what allows you to insert a voltmeter, solder joints, and terminal blocks into the circuit *without* affecting accuracy — as long as both ends of the inserted metal sit at the same temperature. Law 3 is the foundation of **all standard thermocouple tables** and of **cold-junction compensation algorithms**.

---

### 4.4 ASCII Schematic of a Practical Thermocouple Probe

```
        PROCESS (T_H)
            |
        =[HOT JUNCTION]=
            |          |
        [Metal A]    [Metal B]
            |          |
            |----------|
            |          |
        ---+---SHIELD---+--- (Twisted, shielded pair)
            |          |
        [Terminal Block @ T_C]
            |          |
        =[COLD JUNCTION]=
            |          |
        [Cu Lead A]  [Cu Lead B]
            |          |
        [Reference Sensor — RTD]
            |          |
        [Differential Amplifier] --> [ADC] --> [Display °C]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer / Conceptual)**

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> **Q1.** *Define the Seebeck effect and state the mathematical relationship between the Seebeck coefficient of a metal pair and the EMF generated.*
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The Seebeck effect is the phenomenon in which an electromotive force (EMF) is induced in a closed circuit comprising two dissimilar metals when their junctions are maintained at different temperatures. The Seebeck coefficient of a pair, $S_{AB}$, is defined as the change in EMF per unit change in temperature:
> $$S_{AB} = \frac{dE}{dT} \quad \text{(μV/°C)}$$
> The total EMF is given by the integral:
> $$E = \int_{T_C}^{T_H} S_{AB}(T)\, dT$$
> which for small temperature ranges reduces to $E \approx S_{AB}(T_H - T_C)$.
> **[Marks distribution: Definition 1m, formula 1m, integrated form / linear approximation 1m]**

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> **Q2.** *Distinguish between the Peltier effect and the Thomson effect. Mention the units of their respective coefficients.*
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> | Feature | Peltier Effect | Thomson Effect |
> |---|---|---|
> | Requirement | Junction of two dissimilar metals | Single homogeneous conductor |
> | Driving force | Current through junction | Current + temperature gradient |
> | Coefficient (π / σ) | π_AB in **Volts** | σ in **V/°C** (or μV/°C) |
> | Heat expression | Q = π · I · t | Q = σ · I · ΔT · t |
> | Reversibility | Reversible | Reversible |
> **[Marks distribution: 1m Peltier, 1m Thomson, 1m units/table]**

---

### **PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)**

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 — Module 1]**
> **Question A (14 Marks):**
> (a) Derive the relationship between the Peltier coefficient and the Seebeck coefficient using thermodynamic arguments. **(7 marks)**
> (b) A copper–constantan (Type T) thermocouple is used to measure the temperature of a cryogenic bath. The cold junction is at $T_C = 0~^\circ\text{C}$ and the measured EMF is $E = 5.604~\text{mV}$. Given $S_{Cu} = 1.83~\mu\text{V}/^\circ\text{C}$ and $S_{Co} = -38.11~\mu\text{V}/^\circ\text{C}$ (Co = constantan), determine the bath temperature $T_H$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation — Kelvin's First Relation (7 marks):**

1. **Reversibility & entropy balance** (2 marks):
   Consider a reversible thermocouple carrying charge $dq$ with hot junction at $T+dT$ and cold junction at $T$. The entropy change must vanish:
   $$\frac{\pi(T+dT)}{T+dT} - \frac{\pi(T)}{T} + \sigma \cdot \frac{dT}{T} = 0$$

2. **Expand and simplify** (2 marks):
   $$\frac{d\pi}{T} - \frac{\pi}{T^2}dT + \frac{\sigma}{T}dT = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{d\pi}{dT} = \frac{\pi}{T} - \sigma$$

3. **Differentiate the assumed form $S = \pi/T$** (1 mark):
   $$\frac{dS}{dT} = \frac{1}{T}\frac{d\pi}{dT} - \frac{\pi}{T^2} = -\frac{\sigma}{T}$$

4. **Conclude Kelvin's first relation** (2 marks):
   $$\boxed{S_{AB} = \frac{\pi_{AB}}{T} \quad \text{and} \quad \sigma = -T\frac{dS_{AB}}{dT}}$$

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

1. Compute relative Seebeck coefficient (1 mark):
   $$S_{AB} = S_{Cu} - S_{Co} = 1.83 - (-38.11) = 39.94~\mu\text{V}/^\circ\text{C}$$

2. Convert EMF to microvolts (1 mark):
   $$E = 5.604~\text{mV} = 5604~\mu\text{V}$$

3. Apply linear approximation formula (2 marks):
   $$E = S_{AB}(T_H - T_C) \quad \Rightarrow \quad 5604 = 39.94(T_H - 0)$$

4. Solve (1 mark):
   $$T_H = \frac{5604}{39.94} = 140.31~^\circ\text{C}$$

5. Note for the examiner (1 mark): Type T is valid down to about −200 °C; for cryogenic work, look up ITS-90 reference tables rather than rely on the linear approximation.
   $$\boxed{T_H \approx 140.31~^\circ\text{C}}$$

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**
> (a) State and explain the three laws of thermoelectricity. Show how the law of intermediate temperatures is used in cold-junction compensation. **(7 marks)**
> (b) A thermopile consisting of 20 chromel–alumel (K-type) thermocouples is connected in series to detect a small temperature difference. If each couple has a Seebeck coefficient of $41~\mu\text{V}/^\circ\text{C}$ and the hot/cold junction temperatures are $T_H = 52~^\circ\text{C}$ and $T_C = 28~^\circ\text{C}$, compute: (i) the EMF per couple, and (ii) the total thermopile output. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Three Laws of Thermoelectricity (7 marks):**

1. **Law of Homogeneous Circuit (2 marks):**
   The EMF in a thermocouple depends only on the materials and the temperatures of the two junctions; it does not depend on the *distribution* of temperature along the wires. A homogeneous wire cannot produce EMF regardless of its temperature profile.
   $$E_{AB}(T_1, T_2) \neq f(\text{distribution})$$

2. **Law of Intermediate Metals (2 marks):**
   If a third metal C is inserted into the A–B circuit, and both ends of C are at the same temperature, the net EMF is unchanged.
   $$E_{ABC}(T_H, T_C) = E_{AB}(T_H, T_C)$$

3. **Law of Intermediate Temperatures (3 marks):**
   The EMF for junctions at $T_1$ and $T_3$ is the algebraic sum of EMFs at $T_1, T_2$ and $T_2, T_3$:
   $$E_{AB}(T_1, T_3) = E_{AB}(T_1, T_2) + E_{AB}(T_2, T_3)$$
   **Application to Cold-Junction Compensation:**
   The voltmeter reads $E(T_H, T_C)$. The standard tables give $E(T_H, 0)$ and $E(T_C, 0)$. We compute:
   $$E(T_H, T_C) = E(T_H, 0) - E(T_C, 0)$$
   So:
   $$E(T_H, 0) = E(T_H, T_C) + E(T_C, 0)$$
   Since $T_C$ is measured by a separate sensor, $E(T_C, 0)$ is looked up from the table, added to the meter reading, and the corrected EMF is then converted back to $T_H$.

**(b) Thermopile Numerical (7 marks):**

1. Given: $S_K = 41~\mu\text{V}/^\circ\text{C}$, $\Delta T = 52 - 28 = 24~^\circ\text{C}$, $N = 20$ couples.

2. EMF per couple (2 marks):
   $$E_1 = S_K \cdot \Delta T = 41 \times 24 = 984~\mu\text{V} = 0.984~\text{mV}$$

3. Total thermopile EMF (2 marks):
   $$E_{\text{total}} = N \cdot E_1 = 20 \times 0.984 = 19.68~\text{mV}$$

4. Concluding remark (1 mark): Series connection multiplies EMF, but **all couples must share the same $\Delta T$**; otherwise, the signals add vectorially with sign. Useful for low-ΔT detectors like heat-flux sensors and infrared thermopiles.

   $$\boxed{E_1 = 0.984~\text{mV};\quad E_{\text{total}} = 19.68~\text{mV}}$$

---

### **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks in This Topic:**
> 1. **Unit Confusion:** Mixing mV with μV. Always convert before plugging into equations ($1~\text{mV} = 1000~\mu\text{V}$).
> 2. **Sign Error in Seebeck Coefficient:** $S_{AB} = S_A - S_B$, *not* $S_B - S_A$. A wrong sign can flip a temperature from positive to negative.
> 3. **Peltier vs Joule Heat:** Students often confuse $\pi \cdot I$ (linear, reversible) with $I^2 R$ (quadratic, irreversible). They coexist; only the Peltier part is *useful* for cooling.
> 4. **Linear Approximation Caveat:** The formula $E = S(T_H - T_C)$ is valid only for small $\Delta T$ where $S$ is roughly constant. For high temperatures (>500 °C), always use **ITS-90 polynomial coefficients** — examiners award zero marks for naive use of constant-S in K-type at 1000 °C.
> 5. **Forgetting Law of Intermediate Metals:** Students often claim that attaching a copper lead to a chromel wire will "spoil" the EMF. It will not — *provided the two ends of the copper are at the same temperature*. Cite this law explicitly to earn full marks.
> 6. **Thermopile vs Thermopile Misuse:** Series connection multiplies EMF only if the temperature gradient is common to all couples. Series–parallel hybrid configurations are *not* simply additive.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read this 5 minutes before entering the exam hall.**

- [x] **Seebeck Effect** = Heat → EMF (2 dissimilar metals, 2 junctions at different T).
- [x] **Peltier Effect** = Current → Heat (or cold) at a single junction.
- [x] **Thomson Effect** = Current in one metal with a temperature gradient → reversible heat.
- [x] **Seebeck coefficient** $S_{AB} = S_A - S_B$ in **μV/°C**.
- [x] **Peltier coefficient** $\pi_{AB}$ in **Volts**; heat rate $\dot{Q} = \pi \cdot I$.
- [x] **Thomson coefficient** $\sigma$ in **V/°C**; heat $Q = \sigma I \Delta T t$.
- [x] **Kelvin Relation #1:** $S_{AB} = \dfrac{\pi_{AB}}{T}$.
- [x] **Kelvin Relation #2:** $\sigma_A = -T \dfrac{dS_A}{dT}$.
- [x] **Law 1 (Homogeneous):** One metal cannot generate EMF no matter how heated.
- [x] **Law 2 (Intermediate Metals):** Inserting a third metal (uniform T) → no change in EMF.
- [x] **Law 3 (Intermediate Temperatures):** $E(T_1, T_3) = E(T_1, T_2) + E(T_2, T_3)$.
- [x] **CJC formula:** $E(T_H, 0) = E_{\text{meas}}(T_H, T_C) + E_{\text{lookup}}(T_C, 0)$.
- [x] **Thermopile output:** $E_{\text{total}} = N \cdot S_{AB} \cdot \Delta T$.
- [x] **Linear approximation valid only for small $\Delta T$; use ITS-90 for high accuracy.**
- [x] **ITS-90** is the current international standard for thermocouple reference tables (since 1990).
- [x] **Common thermocouple types to remember:** K (Chromel–Alumel), T (Cu–Constantan), J (Fe–Constantan), E (Chromel–Constantan), S/R (Pt–Pt/Rh), N (Nicrosil–Nisil), B (Pt/Rh–Pt/Rh).

> [!TIP]
> **Final Memory Aid:**
> *"Seebeck Sees, Peltier Pushes, Thomson Treats a single wire."*
> *"Three Laws: Homogeneous, Intermediate Metal, Intermediate Temperature."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Radiation Methods: Radiation and Optical Pyrometry
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT724 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Radiation Methods: Radiation and Optical Pyrometry

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Radiation Pyrometry — Formal Definition

**Radiation Pyrometry** is a non-contact method of temperature measurement that exploits the principle of thermal radiation emitted by every body whose temperature lies above absolute zero (0 K). In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, it is classified under **radiation methods of temperature measurement** and includes *Total Radiation Pyrometers*, *Optical Pyrometers (Disappearing Filament Type)*, *Photoelectric Pyrometers*, and *Two-Color (Ratio) Pyrometers*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (OECHT724, Module 1):** Radiation methods of temperature measurement form a high-weightage area. Mastery of blackbody laws, pyrometer construction, and emissivity correction is mandatory for both Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks) questions.

### 1.2 Thermal Radiation — The Underlying Physics

Every body with a temperature above **0 K** emits electromagnetic radiation across a continuous spectrum. The intensity and spectral distribution of this emitted radiation depend purely on the body's temperature and the nature of its surface.

**Classification of bodies with respect to radiation:**

| Body Type | Definition | Practical Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Blackbody** | Perfect absorber & emitter (absorptivity α = 1, emissivity ε = 1) | Hypothetical cavity with small hole |
| **Grey body** | Emissivity ε = constant < 1 (independent of λ) | Most industrial metals |
| **Real body** | ε varies with λ and temperature | Polished surfaces, oxidized metals |

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Glowing Iron Rod"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine heating an iron rod in a forge.
> - At low heat, it gives off **invisible infrared** (you sense warmth without seeing glow).
> - As it heats further, it glows **dull red**, then **orange**, then **white-hot**.
>
> The color and intensity of the glow are **direct indicators of temperature**. A radiation pyrometer essentially mimics the human eye (and beyond) to "see" this thermal glow and convert it into a temperature reading — **without ever touching the hot body**. This is critical for measuring furnace interiors, molten metals, and turbine blades where contact sensors would melt.

### 1.4 Fundamental Radiation Quantities

- **Radiant Emittance (E):** Total radiant energy emitted per unit area per unit time (W/m²)
- **Monochromatic Emittance (E_λ):** Energy emitted per unit area per unit time per unit wavelength interval
- **Emissivity (ε):** Ratio of radiation emitted by a real body to that of a blackbody at the same temperature: **ε = E / E_b**
- **Absorptivity (α):** Fraction of incident radiation absorbed by the body
- **Reflectivity (ρ)** and **Transmissivity (τ):** Fractions reflected and transmitted
- **Conservation Identity:** α + ρ + τ = **1** (opaque body ⇒ τ = 0 ⇒ α + ρ = 1)

### 1.5 Key Physical Constants

- **Stefan-Boltzmann constant:** σ = **5.6697 × 10⁻⁸ W/(m²·K⁴)**
- **Wien's displacement constant:** b = **2.898 × 10⁻³ m·K**
- **Speed of light:** c = **2.998 × 10⁸ m/s**
- **Planck's constant:** h = **6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s**
- **Boltzmann constant:** k = **1.381 × 10⁻²³ J/K**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Pillars of Blackbody Radiation

These four laws are **the most heavily tested theoretical content** in KTU exams for radiation pyrometry.

#### 2.1.1 Planck's Radiation Law (Quantum Theory — 1900)

Planck postulated that energy is quantized in packets called *quanta* of magnitude **E = hν**. The spectral distribution of blackbody radiation per unit wavelength is:

The derived spectral emittance is:

$$E_{\lambda,b} = \frac{2\pi h c^{2}}{\lambda^{5} \, \left(e^{hc/\lambda kT} - 1\right)}$$

- **Why it matters:** Planck's law is the *parent equation*. Wien's and Rayleigh-Jeans are limiting cases.
- **High-frequency limit (λ small):** Reduces to **Wien's distribution.**
- **Low-frequency limit (λ large):** Reduces to **Rayleigh-Jeans distribution.**

#### 2.1.2 Wien's Displacement Law (1893)

The wavelength at which the blackbody emission is *maximum* shifts toward shorter wavelengths as temperature rises:

$$\lambda_{m} \cdot T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m·K}$$

- **Intuition:** "Hotter objects glow bluer" — this is why a furnace at 1500 K glows red, while a hotter one (3000 K) glows white-blue.
- **Engineering utility:** Basis of *two-color pyrometers* and *spectral ratio pyrometers*.

#### 2.1.3 Stefan-Boltzmann Law (1879/1884)

Total energy radiated per unit area by a blackbody over ALL wavelengths:

$$E_{b} = \sigma T^{4} = 5.6697 \times 10^{-8} \cdot T^{4} \text{ W/m}^{2}$$

For a **real (grey) body:**

$$E = \varepsilon \sigma T^{4}$$

- **Intuition:** Energy output is *extremely* sensitive to temperature — doubling T gives **16× the energy.**
- **Engineering utility:** Forms the working principle of **Total Radiation Pyrometers** (e.g., the *Féry* telescope pyrometer).

#### 2.1.4 Kirchhoff's Law (1860)

A body that is a good absorber is also a good emitter. In thermal equilibrium:

$$\varepsilon_{\lambda} = \alpha_{\lambda}$$

- **Consequence for pyrometers:** A pyrometer calibrated for a blackbody will read *lower* than true temperature for real bodies because real bodies emit less (ε < 1).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE — Memorize all entries before the exam.**

| # | Law / Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Key Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Stefan-Boltzmann (blackbody) | $E_{b} = \sigma T^{4}$ | E\_b: W/m², σ: W/(m²·K⁴), T: K | Ideal blackbody |
| 2 | Stefan-Boltzmann (real body) | $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^{4}$ | ε: emissivity (0 to 1) | Grey body |
| 3 | Wien's Displacement | $\lambda_{m} \cdot T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ | λ\_m: m, T: K | Peak wavelength |
| 4 | Planck's Law | $E_{\lambda,b} = \frac{2\pi h c^{2}}{\lambda^{5}(e^{hc/\lambda kT}-1)}$ | E\_(λ,b): W/m³ | Full spectrum |
| 5 | Monochromatic Emittance (Wien approx.) | $E_{\lambda,b} = C_{1} \lambda^{-5} e^{-C_{2}/\lambda T}$ | $C_{1}, C_{2}$ are constants | $hc \gg \lambda k T$ |
| 6 | Brightness Temperature | $\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_{b}} = \frac{\lambda}{C_{2}} \ln \varepsilon_{\lambda}$ | T: true, T\_b: brightness | Optical pyrometer |
| 7 | Total Radiation Pyrometer | $T = \left( \frac{E}{\varepsilon \sigma} \right)^{1/4}$ | E: measured flux | Corrected for ε |
| 8 | Energy Conservation | $\alpha + \rho + \tau = 1$ | All dimensionless | Opaque ⇒ τ = 0 |
| 9 | Effective Emissivity (two-color) | $\varepsilon_{eff} = \varepsilon_{1}/\varepsilon_{2}$ | At two wavelengths | Ratio pyrometer |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Steel & Glass Industry:** Continuous monitoring of molten metal temperature (1500–3000 K) in furnaces and ladles.
- **Aerospace:** Turbine blade temperature in jet engines (use two-color pyrometers due to unknown emissivity).
- **Semiconductor:** Rapid Thermal Processing (RTP) — wafer temperature control during annealing.
- **Cement & Ceramics:** Kiln temperature profiling.
- **Power Plants:** Boiler tube and combustion gas temperature measurement.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Stefan-Boltzmann Law from Planck's Law

Planck's law gives the *spectral* emittance. The *total* emittance is the integral of E\_(λ,b) over all wavelengths from 0 to ∞:

$$E_{b}(T) = \int_{0}^{\infty} E_{\lambda,b} \, d\lambda = \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{2\pi h c^{2}}{\lambda^{5} \left(e^{hc/\lambda kT} - 1\right)} \, d\lambda$$

**Step 1 — Substitution to simplify the integral:**

Let $x = \frac{hc}{\lambda k T}$, which means $\lambda = \frac{hc}{x k T}$ and $d\lambda = -\frac{hc}{x^{2} k T} dx$.

When λ → 0, x → ∞; when λ → ∞, x → 0.

**Step 2 — Rewrite the integrand:**

$$E_{b}(T) = \frac{2\pi h c^{2}}{\left(\frac{hc}{x k T}\right)^{5}} \cdot \left(-\frac{hc}{x^{2} k T}\right) \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{x^{5}}{e^{x} - 1} \, dx$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the constants:**

$$\frac{2\pi h c^{2} \cdot x^{5} (kT)^{5}}{(hc)^{5}} \cdot \left(\frac{hc}{x^{2} kT}\right) = \frac{2\pi (kT)^{4}}{h^{3} c^{2}} \cdot x^{3} \cdot hc = \frac{2\pi k^{4} T^{4}}{h^{3} c} \cdot x^{3}$$

**Step 4 — Pull out constants from the integral:**

$$E_{b}(T) = \frac{2\pi k^{4} T^{4}}{h^{3} c} \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{x^{3}}{e^{x} - 1} \, dx$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the standard integral:**

$$\int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{x^{3}}{e^{x} - 1} \, dx = \frac{\pi^{4}}{15}$$

(provable via Riemann zeta function: $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} 1/n^{4} = \pi^{4}/90$, multiplied by 6 for the $x^3$ integral.)

**Step 6 — Substitute back:**

$$E_{b}(T) = \frac{2\pi k^{4} T^{4}}{h^{3} c} \cdot \frac{\pi^{4}}{15} = \frac{2\pi^{5} k^{4}}{15 h^{3} c} \cdot T^{4}$$

**Step 7 — Define Stefan-Boltzmann constant:**

$$\sigma = \frac{2\pi^{5} k^{4}}{15 h^{3} c} = 5.6697 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W/(m}^{2}\text{·K}^{4}\text{)}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{E_{b}(T) = \sigma T^{4}}$$

This derivation reveals why $\sigma$ has its peculiar value — it emerges directly from fundamental constants.

### 3.2 Derivation of Wien's Displacement Law

We seek the wavelength λ\_m that *maximizes* the spectral emittance E\_(λ,b). Differentiate with respect to λ and set to zero:

$$\frac{\partial E_{\lambda,b}}{\partial \lambda} = 0$$

**Step 1 — Differentiate Planck's law** using quotient rule (the algebra is lengthy; we present the condensed result):

$$\frac{\partial E_{\lambda,b}}{\partial \lambda} = \frac{2\pi h c^{2}}{\lambda^{6}(e^{hc/\lambda kT} - 1)} \left[ \frac{hc/\lambda kT \cdot e^{hc/\lambda kT}}{e^{hc/\lambda kT} - 1} - 5 \right] = 0$$

**Step 2 — Solve the transcendental equation:**

Let $y = \frac{hc}{\lambda_{m} k T}$. The condition reduces to:

$$\frac{y \, e^{y}}{e^{y} - 1} = 5$$

**Step 3 — Numerical solution:** $y \approx 4.9651$

**Step 4 — Solve for λ\_m:**

$$\lambda_{m} = \frac{hc}{y k T} = \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 2.998 \times 10^{8}}{4.9651 \times 1.381 \times 10^{-23} \times T}$$

$$\lambda_{m} = \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3}}{T} \text{ meters}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\lambda_{m} T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m·K}}$$

### 3.3 Correction for Emissivity — Optical Pyrometer

The optical pyrometer compares the brightness of an unknown source with that of an internal calibrated filament. It essentially measures the **brightness temperature** T\_b, defined as the temperature at which a blackbody would emit the same monochromatic intensity at wavelength λ as the real body.

**Starting relation (Wien's approximation valid for visible/IR at T > 1000 K):**

$$E_{\lambda}(\text{real}) = \varepsilon_{\lambda} \, C_{1} \lambda^{-5} e^{-C_{2}/\lambda T}$$

$$E_{\lambda}(\text{blackbody at } T_b) = C_{1} \lambda^{-5} e^{-C_{2}/\lambda T_b}$$

**Step 1 — Set them equal (same measured brightness):**

$$\varepsilon_{\lambda} \, C_{1} \lambda^{-5} e^{-C_{2}/\lambda T} = C_{1} \lambda^{-5} e^{-C_{2}/\lambda T_b}$$

**Step 2 — Cancel common terms:**

$$\varepsilon_{\lambda} \, e^{-C_{2}/\lambda T} = e^{-C_{2}/\lambda T_b}$$

**Step 3 — Take natural logarithm of both sides:**

$$\ln \varepsilon_{\lambda} - \frac{C_{2}}{\lambda T} = -\frac{C_{2}}{\lambda T_b}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for true temperature T:**

$$\boxed{\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_b} = \frac{\lambda}{C_{2}} \ln \varepsilon_{\lambda}}$$

Where $C_{2} = \frac{hc}{k} = 1.4388 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m·K}$.

**Engineering significance:** If emissivity ε\_λ < 1, the *brightness* temperature T\_b is *less than* the *true* temperature T. This equation quantifies the correction.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix of Pyrometer Types

| Pyrometer Type | Detected Radiation | Typical Range | Emissivity Sensitivity | Best Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Total Radiation (Féry) | All wavelengths | 700–3500 K | High (needs ε) | Furnace interiors |
| Optical (Disappearing Filament) | Monochromatic (λ ≈ 0.65 μm) | 1300–10000 K | Moderate (correction needed) | Molten metal |
| Photoelectric | Narrow band, one λ | 700–3000 K | Moderate | Industrial automation |
| Two-Color (Ratio) | Two wavelengths | 1000–5000 K | Very low (ε cancels) | Dusty/smoky furnaces, turbines |
| Infrared Thermometer | Wide IR band | 200–2000 K | High | Building, medical |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Total Radiation Pyrometer — Block Functional Architecture

This diagram maps the *signal path* and *optical train* of a classic **Féry Total Radiation Pyrometer**, a high-yield KTU topic.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Hot Furnace Target T] --> B[Radiation Emitted epsilon sigma T4]
    B --> C[Concave Mirror Optics]
    C --> D[Diaphragm Aperture]
    D --> E[Blackbody Reference Cavity]
    E --> F[Thermopile Detector]
    F --> G[EMF Signal Microvolts]
    G --> H[Cold Junction Compensation]
    H --> I[Amplifier Stage]
    I --> J[Linearization Circuit T4 to T]
    J --> K[Display Indicator Celsius]
    K --> L[Recorder Output 4 to 20 mA]

    style A fill:#FFD580,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#B8860B
    style C fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4
    style F fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C
    style J fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22
    style K fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B
    style L fill:#87CEFA,stroke:#1E90FF
```

**Operating principle summary:**
1. Thermal radiation from the target is collected by a concave mirror.
2. The mirror focuses radiation onto a thermopile (series of thermocouples) — generates millivolts proportional to incident flux.
3. Since E ∝ T⁴, the signal is linearized electronically before display.
4. Calibration is done against an internal blackbody cavity.

### 4.2 Optical Pyrometer (Disappearing Filament) — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Target Object T_true] --> P2[Telescope Objective Lens]
    P3[Internal Reference Filament Lamp] --> P4[Eyepiece]
    P2 --> P5[Red Filter lambda approx 0.65 um]
    P5 --> P6[Filament Plane Superimposed Image]
    P6 --> P4
    P4 --> P7[Observer Eye or Photo Detector]
    P7 --> P8[Adjust Filament Current]
    P8 --> P9[Rheostat]
    P9 --> P3
    P8 --> P10[Meter Reading Brightness T_b]
    P10 --> P11[Emissivity Correction Equation]
    P11 --> P12[True Temperature T_displayed]

    style P1 fill:#FFD580,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px
    style P3 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C
    style P5 fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4
    style P7 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22
    style P10 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B
    style P12 fill:#87CEFA,stroke:#1E90FF
```

**Disappearing Filament Principle:**
- The operator superimposes the image of an internal tungsten filament onto the image of the target.
- Current through the filament is varied until the filament *disappears* into the background glow.
- At that moment, filament brightness = target brightness at the red filter wavelength (≈ 0.65 μm).
- The current is calibrated directly in terms of **brightness temperature T_b**.

### 4.3 Two-Color (Ratio) Pyrometer — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q1[Target T] --> Q2[Beam Splitter Optic]
    Q2 --> Q3[Filter lambda 1]
    Q2 --> Q4[Filter lambda 2]
    Q3 --> Q5[Detector D1]
    Q4 --> Q6[Detector D2]
    Q5 --> Q7[Logarithmic Amplifier ln D1]
    Q6 --> Q8[Logarithmic Amplifier ln D2]
    Q7 --> Q9[Subtractor ln D1 minus ln D2]
    Q8 --> Q9
    Q9 --> Q10[Ratio R of E_lambda1 by E_lambda2]
    Q10 --> Q11[Function Generator T of R]
    Q11 --> Q12[Temperature Display]

    style Q1 fill:#FFD580,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px
    style Q2 fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4
    style Q10 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C
    style Q11 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22
    style Q12 fill:#87CEFA,stroke:#1E90FF
```

**Why ratio pyrometers work in dust/smoke:**
- Smoke attenuates both wavelengths roughly equally.
- Taking the ratio cancels the attenuation factor, giving robust readings.
- If ε₁ ≈ ε₂, emissivity dependence is *minimized* — the ratio depends primarily on temperature.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. Define emissivity and absorptivity. State Kirchhoff's law of thermal radiation.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
- **Emissivity (ε):** Ratio of the radiant energy emitted by a real body to that emitted by a blackbody at the same temperature. $\varepsilon = E / E_b$, where $0 \le \varepsilon \le 1$.
- **Absorptivity (α):** Fraction of incident radiation absorbed by a body. $0 \le \alpha \le 1$.
- **Kirchhoff's Law:** At thermal equilibrium, the emissivity of a body equals its absorptivity at every wavelength and temperature: $\varepsilon_\lambda = \alpha_\lambda$.

---

#### **Q2. State and explain Wien's displacement law.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer:**
Wien's displacement law states that the wavelength at which the spectral emissive power of a blackbody is maximum is inversely proportional to its absolute temperature:

$$\lambda_m \cdot T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m·K}$$

**Explanation:** As temperature rises, the peak of the spectral distribution shifts toward shorter wavelengths — hence hotter objects appear bluer. This principle is used in two-color pyrometers.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert:**
> - When deriving Stefan-Boltzmann from Planck's law, students **lose 2–3 marks** by skipping the integral substitution step. Show $x = hc/(\lambda kT)$ explicitly.
> - In optical pyrometer problems, **always state which temperature** is being calculated (true vs. brightness) — examiners deduct 1 mark if confused.
> - In numericals, **carry forward units** (μm vs. m) for $C_2$; otherwise the final answer is marked wrong.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the working principle and construction of a **Total Radiation Pyrometer (Féry type)** with a neat block diagram. Discuss its advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Working Principle:**
The Féry total radiation pyrometer collects total thermal radiation emitted by a hot body across the entire wavelength spectrum and focuses it onto a thermopile detector using a concave mirror. The output EMF of the thermopile is proportional to the fourth power of the absolute temperature of the target (E ∝ T⁴). Since E = ε σ T⁴, the reading must be corrected for emissivity.

**Construction (refer to Section 4.1 block diagram):**
1. **Concave Mirror:** Collects radiation from the target; usually gold-plated for high reflectivity.
2. **Diaphragm/Adjustable Aperture:** Sets the field of view; protects eye/detector from excessive flux.
3. **Blackbody Reference Cavity:** Internal calibrated reference for cold-junction compensation.
4. **Thermopile:** Series-connected thermocouples (often 8–16) producing EMF in microvolts.
5. **Cold Junction:** Held at a known reference temperature for compensation.
6. **Display & Recorder:** Calibrated in °C or K, with optional 4–20 mA output.

**Advantages:**
- Non-contact; suitable for very high temperatures (700–3500 K).
- Fast response (milliseconds).
- No need for physical access to the target.
- Can be sighted through small openings.

**Limitations:**
- Requires prior knowledge of emissivity ε for accurate readings.
- Affected by dust, smoke, and atmospheric absorption (CO₂, H₂O vapor).
- Lens/mirror contamination causes drift.
- Less accurate than thermocouples for lower temperatures.

**(b)** A total radiation pyrometer is sighted on a hot body and gives a temperature reading of **1356 K**. If the body has an emissivity of **0.6**, find the **true temperature** of the body. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Apparent (radiation) temperature: $T_r = 1356$ K
- Emissivity: $\varepsilon = 0.6$

**Step 1 — Relate measured to true temperature:**

For a total radiation pyrometer, the radiation received from the real body equals that which would be received from a blackbody at the apparent temperature:

$$\varepsilon \sigma T^{4} = \sigma T_r^{4}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for true T:**

$$\varepsilon T^{4} = T_r^{4}$$

$$T = \frac{T_r}{\varepsilon^{1/4}}$$

**Step 3 — Compute:**

$$T = \frac{1356}{(0.6)^{0.25}} = \frac{1356}{0.8801}$$

$$\boxed{T \approx 1541 \text{ K}}$$

The true temperature (1541 K) is higher than the apparent reading, confirming that low emissivity makes the pyrometer *under-read.*

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** With a functional block diagram, explain the construction and working of a **Disappearing Filament Optical Pyrometer**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Principle:**
The optical pyrometer is a device that measures temperature by visually comparing the brightness of an image of the hot target with that of an internally calibrated reference filament, at a specific wavelength (typically red, λ ≈ 0.65 μm).

**Construction (refer to Section 4.2 diagram):**
1. **Objective Lens:** Forms image of the target.
2. **Red Filter:** Selects monochromatic wavelength for comparison.
3. **Reference Filament:** Tungsten lamp inside the instrument; brightness varied by adjusting current.
4. **Eyepiece:** Allows the observer to view the superimposed images of target and filament.
5. **Rheostat & Ammeter:** Adjusts filament current; calibrated directly in °C (brightness temperature).
6. **Power Supply:** Stable DC source for the filament.

**Working:**
- The image of the hot target is focused on the plane of the reference filament.
- Both images are viewed simultaneously through the eyepiece against a red filter.
- The rheostat is adjusted until the filament's tip just *disappears* into the background of the target image — meaning equal brightness at the chosen wavelength.
- The ammeter reading corresponds to the **brightness temperature T_b.**
- True temperature T is obtained using the emissivity correction equation:
$$\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_b} = \frac{\lambda}{C_{2}} \ln \varepsilon_{\lambda}$$

**Advantages:**
- High accuracy (within ±5 °C at 1500 °C).
- Suitable for very high temperatures (1300–10000 K).
- Subjective but reliable with trained operator.

**Limitations:**
- Manual; requires skilled operator (or photoelectric automation).
- Measures brightness temperature, not true temperature — needs emissivity data.

**(b)** An optical pyrometer with a red filter of wavelength **λ = 0.65 μm** reads a brightness temperature of **1323 K** for a hot body. If the body's spectral emissivity at this wavelength is **0.4**, determine the **true temperature** of the body. Given $C_2 = 1.4388 \times 10^{-2}$ m·K. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Wavelength: $\lambda = 0.65 \, \mu m = 0.65 \times 10^{-6}$ m
- Brightness temperature: $T_b = 1323$ K
- Spectral emissivity: $\varepsilon_\lambda = 0.4$
- Second radiation constant: $C_2 = 1.4388 \times 10^{-2}$ m·K

**Step 1 — Write the correction formula:**

$$\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_b} = \frac{\lambda}{C_{2}} \ln \varepsilon_{\lambda}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the right-hand side:**

$$\frac{\lambda}{C_{2}} = \frac{0.65 \times 10^{-6}}{1.4388 \times 10^{-2}} = 4.5177 \times 10^{-5} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

$$\ln \varepsilon_\lambda = \ln(0.4) = -0.9163$$

$$\frac{\lambda}{C_{2}} \ln \varepsilon_\lambda = (4.5177 \times 10^{-5}) \times (-0.9163) = -4.139 \times 10^{-5}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and solve for 1/T:**

$$\frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{T_b} - 4.139 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$\frac{1}{T_b} = \frac{1}{1323} = 7.5586 \times 10^{-4} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

$$\frac{1}{T} = 7.5586 \times 10^{-4} - 4.139 \times 10^{-5} = 7.1447 \times 10^{-4} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — Compute true temperature:**

$$T = \frac{1}{7.1447 \times 10^{-4}} = 1399.6 \text{ K}$$

$$\boxed{T \approx 1400 \text{ K}}$$

The true temperature (1400 K) is higher than the brightness temperature (1323 K), as expected since ε < 1.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Radiation & Optical Pyrometry**

**🔹 Blackbody Radiation Laws (Must Memorize):**
- ☐ Stefan-Boltzmann: $E_b = \sigma T^4$ → Total radiation pyrometer
- ☐ Wien's Displacement: $\lambda_m T = 2898 \, \mu m \cdot K$ → Peak wavelength
- ☐ Planck's Law: Quantum-correct spectral distribution
- ☐ Kirchhoff's Law: $\varepsilon_\lambda = \alpha_\lambda$ → Emissivity = Absorptivity

**🔹 Pyrometer Types:**
- ☐ **Total Radiation (Féry):** Thermopile, measures total flux, needs ε
- ☐ **Optical (Disappearing Filament):** Visual brightness match at λ ≈ 0.65 μm, reads T_b
- ☐ **Photoelectric:** Automated optical, faster, photo-detector replaces eye
- ☐ **Two-Color (Ratio):** Ratio of intensities at two λ's, robust against dust/emissivity

**🔹 Key Equations for Numericals:**
- ☐ True T from radiation pyrometer: $T = T_r / \varepsilon^{1/4}$
- ☐ True T from optical pyrometer: $\dfrac{1}{T} - \dfrac{1}{T_b} = \dfrac{\lambda}{C_2} \ln \varepsilon_\lambda$
- ☐ Constants: $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$, $C_2 = 1.4388 \times 10^{-2}$ m·K, $b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ m·K

**🔹 Exam Strategy:**
- ☐ Always state whether you are computing **true T** or **brightness T\_b**
- ☐ In derivations, show substitution step explicitly (Planck → Stefan-Boltzmann)
- ☐ When asked to "explain with diagram," include labeled block diagram (not just theory)
- ☐ For ε < 1: **True T > Brightness T > Apparent Radiation T**

**🔹 Common Pitfalls:**
- ☐ Confusing **emissivity** with **absorptivity** in opaque vs. transparent bodies
- ☐ Forgetting the $T^4$ dependence when comparing radiation levels
- ☐ Using wrong units for λ (μm vs. m) in Wien's law and $C_2$ equation
- ☐ Treating **two-color pyrometer** as sensitive to ε — it is largely *insensitive* if ε₁ ≈ ε₂
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermistors: Resistance Characteristics and Their Application in Temperature Measurement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT724 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Thermistors: Resistance Characteristics and Their Application in Temperature Measurement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thermistor (Thermal Resistor):** A thermistor is a *non-linear*, *passive*, *solid-state* temperature-sensitive semiconductor device whose electrical resistance varies predictably and strongly with its absolute body temperature. The word **thermistor** is a portmanteau of **THERM**ally sensitive res**ISTOR**.

Thermistors are fabricated from sintered mixtures of metallic oxides such as **manganese (Mn)**, **nickel (Ni)**, **cobalt (Co)**, **copper (Cu)**, and **iron (Fe)** oxides, doped and compressed into beads, discs, rods, or probe-type packages. The conduction mechanism is governed by **semiconductor hopping/tunneling**, which is fundamentally different from the metallic conduction found in RTDs.

### 1.2 Primary Classification

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Expectation:** Examiners frequently expect the three-fold classification. Memorize the mnemonic **"N-C-C"** — *Negative, Critical, (Positive) Coefficient*.

| Type | Full Form | Behaviour of R as T ↑ | Typical β (K) |
|------|-----------|------------------------|---------------|
| **NTC** | Negative Temperature Coefficient | Resistance **decreases exponentially** | 3000 – 5000 |
| **PTC** | Positive Temperature Coefficient | Resistance **increases sharply** above a *Curie point* | (Step-type, switch-like) |
| **CTR** | Critical Temperature Resistor | Abrupt resistance jump at a critical T | — |

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — "The Garden Hose of Electrons":** Imagine a narrow garden hose through which water (electrons) flows. If you heat the hose, the inner lining swells and pinches the flow → **NTC (resistance falls as temperature rises because more charge carriers are thermally excited across the semiconductor band-gap, NOT because of physical swelling — a more accurate mental model).** For a **PTC**, the hose abruptly kinks shut beyond a certain temperature due to a phase change in the crystalline structure, choking the flow.

The key intuitive takeaways for a first-time reader:
- **NTC thermistor** = Heat **frees** more charge carriers → more current flows → resistance drops.
- **PTC thermistor** = Heat **re-organizes** the crystal lattice above the Curie temperature → flow is blocked → resistance shoots up.
- Compared to a Platinum RTD, an NTC thermistor is approximately **10× more sensitive** per degree Celsius.

### 1.4 Standard Reference Metrics (KTU-Highlighted)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Reference Quantities — Memorize These Constants:**
> - **Reference Temperature:** $T_0 = 298.15 \, \text{K}$ (i.e., $25 \, ^\circ\text{C}$)
> - **Standard Resistance:** $R_0$ — resistance measured at $T_0$
> - **Beta Constant (β or B):** Material-specific, units = **Kelvin**
> - **Dissipation Constant (δ):** Power (mW) required to raise the thermistor by 1 °C in still air
> - **Thermal Time Constant (τ):** Time to reach 63.2 % of final value, typically **1 s – 60 s**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Theory — Why Does Resistance Change?

The electrical conduction in a thermistor is governed by the **semiconductor band-gap mechanism**. As temperature rises:
1. More electrons in the valence band gain sufficient thermal energy ($k_B T$, where $k_B$ is **Boltzmann's constant = $1.380 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K**) to jump into the conduction band.
2. The increased carrier density $n$ follows an **Arrhenius-type** exponential law.
3. Because conductivity $\sigma \propto n$, and resistance $R \propto 1/\sigma$, the resistance drops exponentially.

For a **PTC** (typically Barium Titanate, BaTiO₃ based):
- Below Curie temperature → behaves like an NTC.
- Above Curie temperature → the ferroelectric domains collapse, grain-boundary barriers rise sharply, and resistance jumps by **3–6 orders of magnitude**.

## 2.2 The Master Equation — B-Parameter Model

The canonical resistance-temperature characteristic is:

$$R_T = R_0 \, \exp\!\left[\, B \left( \dfrac{1}{T} - \dfrac{1}{T_0} \right) \,\right]$$

where:
- $R_T$ = resistance at absolute temperature $T$ (Kelvin)
- $R_0$ = resistance at reference temperature $T_0$ (typically 298.15 K)
- $B$ = **beta constant** in Kelvin (given on every thermistor datasheet)
- All temperatures MUST be in **Kelvin** — a common KTU mistake.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates **every equation you need** for thermistor-related KTU problems. Mastering these gives ~80 % coverage of any Module-1 numerical/derivation.

| # | Equation | Physical Meaning | Boundary / Note |
|---|----------|-------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | $R_T = R_0 \, \exp\!\left[B\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T} - \dfrac{1}{T_0}\right)\right]$ | Master B-model | $T, T_0$ in **Kelvin** |
| 2 | $\ln R_T = \ln R_0 + B\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T} - \dfrac{1}{T_0}\right)$ | Linear in $1/T$ | Used for graphical β extraction |
| 3 | $\dfrac{1}{T} = \dfrac{1}{T_0} + \dfrac{1}{B}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{R_T}{R_0}\right)$ | Inverse for temperature | Solve for unknown $T$ |
| 4 | $\alpha_T = \dfrac{1}{R_T}\dfrac{dR_T}{dT} = -\dfrac{B}{T^2}$ | Temperature coefficient (NTC) | Negative sign indicates NTC |
| 5 | $\dfrac{1}{T} = A + B_1 \ln R + B_3 (\ln R)^3$ | **Steinhart–Hart** (3-param) | High-precision, ±0.01 °C |
| 6 | $V_{\text{out}} = V_s \dfrac{R_T}{R_s + R_T}$ | Voltage-divider readout | $R_s$ = series resistor |
| 7 | $V_o = V_s \left[\dfrac{R_T}{R_T+R_1} - \dfrac{R_3}{R_2+R_3}\right]$ | Unbalanced Wheatstone output | Use for sensitivity analysis |
| 8 | $P = \delta \, (T_{\text{therm}} - T_{\text{amb}})$ | Self-heating power eqn. | $\delta$ in mW/°C |
| 9 | $\Delta R \, (\%) \approx -B \, \Delta T \, / \, T^2$ | Small-signal linear approx. | Valid for $\Delta T \ll T$ |
| 10 | $\tau = \dfrac{m \, c}{\delta}$ | Thermal time constant | $m$ = mass, $c$ = sp. heat |

### 2.4 Why These Formulas Matter in Engineering

- **Formula 1–3** are used by every thermistor manufacturer to produce calibration curves and lookup tables.
- **Formula 4** lets a designer predict sensor sensitivity — essential for instrumentation amplifier gain selection.
- **Formula 5 (Steinhart-Hart)** is the *gold-standard* model used in scientific thermometry, NASA probes, and medical-grade thermometers.
- **Formulas 6–7** determine the **interface electronics** choice: voltage divider (low-cost) vs. active bridge (high-precision).
- **Formulas 8–9** govern **self-heating error analysis** — a classic KTU 14-mark problem.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Anti-Skip Mandate:** Every algebraic transition is shown explicitly. No "it can be shown that" shortcuts. This is how a KTU topper (full marks on derivations) writes.

## 3.1 Derivation of the Temperature Coefficient α

**Starting Point:** Master equation

$$R_T = R_0 \, \exp\!\left[ B \left( \dfrac{1}{T} - \dfrac{1}{T_0} \right) \right]$$

**Step 1 — Differentiate $R_T$ with respect to $T$.**  
Apply the chain rule $\dfrac{d}{dT}[e^{u(T)}] = e^{u}\cdot\dfrac{du}{dT}$ with $u(T) = B\left(\dfrac{1}{T} - \dfrac{1}{T_0}\right)$.

Note that $\dfrac{1}{T_0}$ is a constant (does NOT depend on $T$), so:

$$\dfrac{du}{dT} = B \cdot \dfrac{d}{dT}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T}\right) = B \cdot \left(-\dfrac{1}{T^2}\right) = -\dfrac{B}{T^2}$$

**Step 2 — Write the derivative.**

$$\dfrac{dR_T}{dT} = R_0 \, \exp\!\left[ B \left( \dfrac{1}{T} - \dfrac{1}{T_0} \right) \right] \cdot \left(-\dfrac{B}{T^2}\right) = -\dfrac{B \, R_T}{T^2}$$

**Step 3 — Recognize that $R_0 \exp[\,\cdot\,]$ is simply $R_T$**, so substitute back:

$$\dfrac{dR_T}{dT} = -\dfrac{B \, R_T}{T^2}$$

**Step 4 — Define and compute the temperature coefficient $\alpha_T$** as the fractional change in resistance per kelvin:

$$\alpha_T \equiv \dfrac{1}{R_T} \cdot \dfrac{dR_T}{dT}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the result from Step 3.**

$$\alpha_T = \dfrac{1}{R_T} \cdot \left(-\dfrac{B \, R_T}{T^2}\right) = -\dfrac{B}{T^2}$$

**Step 6 — Final simplified result.**

$$\boxed{\;\alpha_T = -\dfrac{B}{T^2}\;}\quad \text{(units: K}^{-1}\text{)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** At $T = 298 \, \text{K}$ with $B = 4000 \, \text{K}$, $\alpha_T \approx -0.045 \, \text{K}^{-1}$, i.e., $\mathbf{-4.5\%/K}$. This is **roughly 12× larger in magnitude** than a Pt100 RTD ($\alpha \approx +0.385\%/\text{K}$). The negative sign confirms NTC behaviour.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Linearized (Small-Signal) Resistance Equation

For small temperature excursions $\Delta T = T - T_0 \ll T_0$, we can Taylor-expand $R_T$.

**Step 1 — Start from the logarithmic form.**

$$\ln R_T = \ln R_0 + B\left(\dfrac{1}{T} - \dfrac{1}{T_0}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Express $1/T$ in terms of $\Delta T$.**  
Write $T = T_0 + \Delta T$ and use the geometric-series expansion $\dfrac{1}{T_0 + \Delta T} = \dfrac{1}{T_0}\left(1 + \dfrac{\Delta T}{T_0}\right)^{-1}$. For $\Delta T \ll T_0$:

$$\dfrac{1}{T} \approx \dfrac{1}{T_0}\left(1 - \dfrac{\Delta T}{T_0}\right) = \dfrac{1}{T_0} - \dfrac{\Delta T}{T_0^2}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute back into Step 1.**

$$\ln R_T = \ln R_0 + B\left[\left(\dfrac{1}{T_0} - \dfrac{\Delta T}{T_0^2}\right) - \dfrac{1}{T_0}\right] = \ln R_0 - \dfrac{B \, \Delta T}{T_0^2}$$

**Step 4 — Exponentiate.**

$$R_T = R_0 \, \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{B \, \Delta T}{T_0^2}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Apply $e^x \approx 1 + x$ for small $x$.**

$$R_T \approx R_0 \left(1 - \dfrac{B \, \Delta T}{T_0^2}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Identify $\alpha_T$ from Section 3.1** ($\alpha_{T_0} = -B/T_0^2$) so $-B/T_0^2 = \alpha_{T_0}$:

$$\boxed{\;R_T \approx R_0 \left( 1 + \alpha_{T_0} \, \Delta T \right)\;}$$

This is **the same form as an RTD!** The linearization is valid within $\pm 5 \, ^\circ\text{C}$ of $T_0$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Steinhart–Hart Equation (Conceptual)

The B-model uses one parameter ($B$). Real devices deviate from a single exponential because the activation energy itself varies slightly with temperature. The **Steinhart–Hart** model uses three constants $A$, $B_1$, $B_3$ (do not confuse with the β of Section 3.1):

$$\dfrac{1}{T} = A + B_1 \ln R + B_3 (\ln R)^3$$

**The three constants are obtained by calibrating at three known temperatures** $T_1, T_2, T_3$ and solving the resulting 3×3 linear system in $(A, B_1, B_3)$. This is the **gold-standard model used in clinical/medical-grade thermistors.**

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — KTU Board Style Numerical

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (KTU-style, 7 marks):** An NTC thermistor has $R_0 = 10 \, \text{k}\Omega$ at $T_0 = 25 \, ^\circ\text{C}$ and $B = 4000 \, \text{K}$. Find (a) its resistance at $T = 100 \, ^\circ\text{C}$, (b) its temperature coefficient at 25 °C, and (c) verify the small-signal linearized result.

**Given:** $R_0 = 10\,000 \, \Omega$, $T_0 = 298.15 \, \text{K}$, $T = 373.15 \, \text{K}$, $B = 4000 \, \text{K}$.

### Part (a) — Resistance at 100 °C

**Step 1 — Convert to Kelvin.**

$$T_0 = 25 + 273.15 = 298.15 \, \text{K}, \quad T = 100 + 273.15 = 373.15 \, \text{K}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the bracketed term.**

$$\dfrac{1}{T} - \dfrac{1}{T_0} = \dfrac{1}{373.15} - \dfrac{1}{298.15}$$

$$\dfrac{1}{373.15} = 2.6799 \times 10^{-3}, \quad \dfrac{1}{298.15} = 3.3540 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\dfrac{1}{T} - \dfrac{1}{T_0} = (2.6799 - 3.3540) \times 10^{-3} = -6.7410 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{K}^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by B.**

$$B\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T} - \dfrac{1}{T_0}\right) = 4000 \times (-6.7410 \times 10^{-4}) = -2.6964$$

**Step 4 — Exponentiate.**

$$\exp(-2.6964) = 0.06748$$

**Step 5 — Compute $R_T$.**

$$R_T = 10\,000 \times 0.06748 = \boxed{674.8 \, \Omega}$$

> **Sanity check:** Temperature rises by 75 °C → resistance should *drop sharply* ✓.

### Part (b) — Temperature Coefficient at 25 °C

$$\alpha_T = -\dfrac{B}{T_0^2} = -\dfrac{4000}{(298.15)^2} = -\dfrac{4000}{88\,893.4}$$

$$\boxed{\;\alpha_T \approx -0.04500 \, \text{K}^{-1} = -4.5\%/\text{K}\;}$$

### Part (c) — Small-Signal Linearized Estimate

$\Delta T = 75 \, \text{K}$, so:

$$R_T^{\text{lin}} = R_0(1 + \alpha_{T_0}\Delta T) = 10\,000[1 + (-0.045)(75)]$$

$$R_T^{\text{lin}} = 10\,000[1 - 3.375] = 10\,000 \times (-2.375)$$

This is **negative** — confirming that the small-signal model is **invalid for $\Delta T = 75 \, \text{K}$** (linearization requires $\Delta T \ll T_0$, roughly $\Delta T < 15 \, \text{K}$).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Functional Architecture — Thermistor-Based Temperature Measurement Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[PHYSICAL TEMPERATURE T in degree C] --> B[THERMISTOR SENSOR NTC or PTC]
    B --> C[RESISTANCE CHANGE R of T]
    C --> D[CONDITIONING CIRCUIT Bridge or Voltage Divider]
    D --> E[AMPLIFIER STAGE Instrumentation Amp]
    E --> F[ADC AND MICROCONTROLLER]
    F --> G[DISPLAY OR PROCESS OUTPUT degree C value]
    B -. Self-heating effect .-> H[CALIBRATION AND COMPENSATION BLOCK]
    H --> F
    style A fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style B fill:#FF9AA2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style D fill:#B5EAD7,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style F fill:#C7CEEA,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style G fill:#E2F0CB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Wheatstone-Bridge Thermistor Interface (Block-Level Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    VS[Excitation Source Vs] --> N1[Node A top of bridge]
    N1 --> R1[R1 fixed resistor]
    N1 --> RT[RT Thermistor arm]
    R1 --> N2[Node B bottom left]
    RT --> N3[Node C bottom right]
    N2 --> R2[R2 fixed resistor]
    N3 --> R3[R3 fixed resistor]
    R2 --> N0[Ground reference node]
    R3 --> N0
    N2 --> OA[Instrumentation Amplifier IN +]
    N3 --> OB[Instrumentation Amplifier IN -]
    OA --> SUMM[Summing and Gain Stage]
    OB --> SUMM
    SUMM --> OUT[Output Vo proportional to delta T]
    style VS fill:#FFE082,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style RT fill:#FF8A80,stroke:#B71C1C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style OUT fill:#A5D6A7,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### Reading the schematic

- The four arms of the bridge are $R_1$ (top-left), $R_T$ (top-right), $R_2$ (bottom-left), $R_3$ (bottom-right).
- $V_s$ energizes the top node (A); the bottom node (D) is grounded.
- The differential output is taken between nodes B and C and fed into an instrumentation amplifier.
- **Balance condition:** $R_T \cdot R_2 = R_1 \cdot R_3$ → $V_o = 0$.
- When temperature changes, $R_T$ deviates → small differential voltage $\rightarrow V_o$.

## 4.3 Resistance-vs-Temperature Characteristic Curves (Qualitative)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1 [Resistance versus Temperature Curves]
    direction LR
    NTCCURVE["NTC CURVE: R falls EXPONENTIALLY as T rises"] -- exponential decay --> X1[Temperature axis]
    PTCCURVE["PTC CURVE: R is flat then JUMPS at Curie point"] -- step rise --> X1
    CTRCURVE["CTR CURVE: R has sharp NEGATIVE jump"] -- step fall --> X1
    end
    style NTCCURVE fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style PTCCURVE fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style CTRCURVE fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize the shapes for KTU diagrams:** NTC ↘️ exponential, PTC → flat ↗️ step, CTR ↘️ step. The examiner may ask you to "sketch and label" — these are the three sketches you must draw.

---
