For buoyancy corrections, the *buoyancy of air* on both spheres must be subtracted (a small but non-negligible effect of about 0.1 %).

#### 2.4.2 Modern Implementation — The Effusion-Type Densitometer

Modern systems use an **effusion principle**: the time taken for a fixed mass of gas to escape through a calibrated orifice is proportional to $\sqrt{M}$ (molar mass), and hence to $\sqrt{\rho}$. This avoids moving parts altogether and provides excellent long-term stability.

---

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in nearly every KTU Part-B solution on density measurement.**

| # | Equation | Description / When to Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\rho = \dfrac{m}{V}$ | Definition of density; used when mass and volume are known directly. | kg/m³ |
| 2 | $F_b = \rho \cdot g \cdot V_0$ | Archimedes' buoyancy; constant-volume hydrometer principle. | N |
| 3 | $\rho = \dfrac{F_b}{g \cdot V_0}$ | Density from measured buoyancy force. | kg/m³ |
| 4 | $\Delta P = (\rho_s - \rho_a) \cdot g \cdot h$ | Air pressure balance — sample density from differential pressure. | Pa |
| 5 | $\rho_s = \rho_a + \dfrac{\Delta P}{g \cdot h}$ | Solved form for sample density. | kg/m³ |
| 6 | $\rho = \dfrac{P \cdot M}{R \cdot T}$ | Ideal-gas law form; temperature correction of gas density. | kg/m³ |
| 7 | $SG = \dfrac{\rho_{gas}}{\rho_{air}}$ | Specific gravity definition (dimensionless). | — |
| 8 | $SG = \dfrac{M_{gas}}{M_{air}}$ | Since $SG = \rho_{gas}/\rho_{air} = M_{gas}/M_{air}$ (at same $P, T$). | — |
| 9 | $\rho_{air,STP} = 1.225 \,\text{kg/m}^3$ | Standard reference value at $T = 288.15\,\text{K}$, $P = 101.325\,\text{kPa}$. | kg/m³ |
| 10 | $R = 8.314 \,\text{kJ/(kmol·K)}$ | Universal gas constant — used in all ideal-gas density calculations. | kJ/(kmol·K) |

> [!TIP]
> **Note:** In the table above, subscripts $s$ = sample, $a$ = air, $b$ = buoyancy, $g$ = gas. The vertical bar notation $\vert x \vert$ has been avoided to preserve table syntax — KTU examiners recommend $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\mid x \mid$ in written exams.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Constant Volume Hydrometer Output

**Statement to prove:** The force measured at the load cell is linearly proportional to the process-fluid density.

**Starting point:** A plummet of fixed volume $V_0$ is fully immersed in the liquid. The total weight of the plummet in air is $W_0 = m_p \cdot g$, where $m_p$ is the plummet mass.

When immersed, the plummet experiences a buoyant force $F_b$ from the fluid. The **apparent weight** measured by the load cell is:

$$W_{app} = W_0 - F_b$$

Substituting Archimedes' principle:

$$W_{app} = m_p \cdot g - \rho \cdot g \cdot V_0$$

Factor $g$:

$$W_{app} = g \cdot \left( m_p - \rho \cdot V_0 \right)$$

Since $m_p$ and $V_0$ are constants, a change in apparent weight depends *only* on a change in $\rho$:

$$\Delta W_{app} = - g \cdot V_0 \cdot \Delta \rho$$

Rearrange to obtain $\rho$ in terms of the measured $W_{app}$:

$$\rho = \dfrac{m_p \cdot g - W_{app}}{g \cdot V_0} = \dfrac{m_p}{V_0} - \dfrac{W_{app}}{g \cdot V_0}$$

Because the plummet's own density $\rho_p = m_p / V_0$ is known, we get a clean, **linear** calibration equation:

$$\boxed{\rho = \rho_p - \dfrac{W_{app}}{g \cdot V_0}}$$

**Interpretation:** The output is linear in density, with slope $-1/(g V_0)$ and intercept equal to the plummet's own density. This is why the instrument is preferred over variable-volume hydrometers (which give a non-linear $\rho \propto 1/h^3$ relation).

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Air Pressure Balance Sensitivity

**Statement to prove:** A small change in sample density produces a measurable change in differential pressure.

Consider two vertical columns, each of height $h$, sealed at the top with a common pressure $P_0$. Column 1 is filled with air (density $\rho_a$); Column 2 is filled with sample gas (density $\rho_s$).

The hydrostatic pressure at the bottom of each column is:

$$P_1 = P_0 + \rho_a \cdot g \cdot h$$
$$P_2 = P_0 + \rho_s \cdot g \cdot h$$

The differential pressure at the bottom (read by a manometer) is:

$$\Delta P = P_2 - P_1 = \rho_s \cdot g \cdot h - \rho_a \cdot g \cdot h$$

Factor:

$$\Delta P = (\rho_s - \rho_a) \cdot g \cdot h$$

Solving for $\rho_s$:

$$\rho_s = \rho_a + \dfrac{\Delta P}{g \cdot h}$$

The **sensitivity** $\mathcal{S}$ of the system is defined as the change in $\Delta P$ per unit change in $\rho_s$:

$$\mathcal{S} = \dfrac{d(\Delta P)}{d\rho_s} = g \cdot h$$

For $h = 1\,\text{m}$ and $g = 9.81\,\text{m/s}^2$:

$$\mathcal{S} = 9.81 \cdot 1 = 9.81 \,\text{Pa per (kg/m}^3\text{)}$$

A density change of $1 \,\text{kg/m}^3$ produces a measurable pressure of $9.81 \,\text{Pa}$. By using taller columns ($h = 5\,\text{m}$ typical), sensitivity rises to about $49 \,\text{Pa/(kg/m}^3\text{)}$ — sufficient for natural gas (ρ ≈ 0.7–1.2 kg/m³) measurement to an accuracy of ±0.1 %.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Gas Density Detector Response

The GDD is essentially a **pneumatic Wheatstone bridge**. Let the reference and sample cavities each have a laminar flow resistance $R$. The pressure drop across each cavity at common inlet pressure $P_{in}$ is:

$$\Delta P_{ref} = P_{in} - P_{out} = \dfrac{8 \mu_{ref} L Q}{\pi r^4}$$

$$\Delta P_{sample} = P_{in} - P_{out} = \dfrac{8 \mu_{sample} L Q}{\pi r^4}$$

The differential signal across the sensing diaphragm is:

$$\delta P = \Delta P_{sample} - \Delta P_{ref} = \dfrac{8 L Q}{\pi r^4} (\mu_{sample} - \mu_{ref})$$

For gases at low pressure, **viscosity is essentially independent of density**, so this direct differential on viscosity gives nearly zero signal. The actual operation relies on the fact that **mass flow** through the cavities is the same (set by the upstream regulator), but the *volumetric flow* differs:

$$Q_{sample} = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{\rho_{sample}}, \quad Q_{ref} = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{\rho_{ref}}$$

The detector output diaphragm deflection $d$ is proportional to the volumetric-flow mismatch:

$$d \propto (Q_{sample} - Q_{ref}) = \dot{m} \left( \dfrac{1}{\rho_{sample}} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_{ref}} \right)$$

At constant $\dot{m}$, this is a **non-linear** function of $\rho_{sample}$, but for small deviations around a reference point it can be linearized to:

$$d \approx \dfrac{K \cdot \dot{m}}{\rho_{ref}^2} \cdot (\rho_{ref} - \rho_{sample})$$

Inverting for the sample density:

$$\boxed{\rho_{sample} \approx \rho_{ref} - \dfrac{\rho_{ref}^2 \cdot d}{K \cdot \dot{m}}}$$

where $K$ is the geometric-mechanical constant of the diaphragm.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Specific Gravity from Balance Beam

Let the two spheres each have volume $V_0$. Sphere 1 contains air (density $\rho_a$, mass $m_a = \rho_a V_0$). Sphere 2 contains the sample gas (density $\rho_g$, mass $m_g = \rho_g V_0$).

Neglecting the buoyancy of the surrounding air on the spheres themselves, the net downward force on the sample side is:

$$F_{net} = (m_g - m_a) \cdot g = (\rho_g - \rho_a) \cdot V_0 \cdot g$$

The balance beam applies a counter-force $F_r$ to restore equilibrium, so:

$$F_r = (\rho_g - \rho_a) \cdot V_0 \cdot g$$

Divide both sides by $\rho_a \cdot V_0 \cdot g$ to non-dimensionalize:

$$\dfrac{F_r}{\rho_a V_0 g} = \dfrac{\rho_g - \rho_a}{\rho_a} = SG - 1$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{SG = 1 + \dfrac{F_r}{\rho_a \cdot V_0 \cdot g}}$$

For natural gas ($M = 16$ to 20 kg/kmol), the expected SG is around 0.55 to 0.70, so $F_r$ is *negative* (buoyancy direction), confirming that natural gas is **lighter than air**.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Side-by-Side Comparison

| Parameter | Const. Volume Hydrometer | Air Pressure Balance | Gas Density Detector | SG Measuring System |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Media** | Liquids | Liquids & Gases | Gases (mainly) | Gases |
| **Primary Sensor** | Strain gauge / load cell | DPT (differential pressure transmitter) | Pneumatic bridge + diaphragm | Balance beam + force feedback |
| **Output** | Force / electrical signal | Differential pressure | Differential pressure | Restoring force |
| **Calibration** | Linear in ρ | Linear in (ρs − ρa) | Non-linear (1/ρ) | Linear in (SG − 1) |
| **Reference** | Plummet geometry V₀ | Dry air column | Carrier gas (He/H₂) | Dry air sphere |
| **Accuracy (typical)** | ±0.1 % to ±0.5 % | ±0.05 % to ±0.2 % | ±1 % to ±2 % | ±0.02 % (primary standard) |
| **Process Environment** | Open tanks, sumps | Pipelines, custody transfer | Lab chromatographs | Custody transfer labs |
| **KTU Marks Weight** | High | Very High | Medium | High |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Constant Volume Hydrometer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ProcessTank[Process Liquid Tank]
        fluidA[("Process Liquid\nDensity rho")]
    end

    subgraph PlummetAssembly[Plummet Assembly]
        plmA["Plummet\nVolume V0"]
        wireA["Suspension Wire"]
    end

    subgraph TransducerUnit[Force Transducer]
        bridgeA["Strain Gauge Bridge\nWheatstone"]
        ampA["Amplifier"]
        cpuA["Signal Conditioner\nrho = mp - Wapp / gV0"]
    end

    dispA["Display /\nRecorder\nkg per m3"]

    fluidA --> plmA
    plmA --> wireA
    wireA --> bridgeA
    bridgeA --> ampA
    ampA --> cpuA
    cpuA --> dispA
```

> **Interpretation:** The plummet is fully submerged in the process fluid; the buoyancy force it experiences is sensed by a Wheatstone strain-gauge bridge, amplified, and converted to a linear density reading via the calibration equation.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Air Pressure Balance Method

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PressureSource[Common Pressure Source P0]
        psrc["Pressure Regulator\nSealed Top"]
    end

    subgraph LeftColumn[Reference Column]
        airC["Dry Air Column\nHeight h"]
        rhoAir["rho_air = 1.225 kg/m3"]
    end

    subgraph RightColumn[Sample Column]
        gasC["Sample Gas Column\nHeight h"]
        rhoSample["rho_sample unknown"]
    end

    subgraph DifferentialManometer[Bottom Manometer]
        dpt["Differential Pressure\nTransmitter"]
        eq1["Delta P = rho_s - rho_a x g x h"]
    end

    subgraph OutputStage[Output]
        calc["rho_s = rho_a + Delta P / gh"]
        ctrl["PID Controller /\nCustody Meter"]
    end

    psrc --> airC
    psrc --> gasC
    airC --> dpt
    gasC --> dpt
    dpt --> eq1
    eq1 --> calc
    calc --> ctrl
```

> **Interpretation:** A common pressure $P_0$ pushes down on both columns. The heavier column (sample) produces a higher bottom pressure; the differential pressure $\Delta P$ is the *signature* of the unknown density.

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Gas Density Detector (Pneumatic Bridge)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CarrierGasInlet[Carrier Gas Inlet]
        carrier["Reference Gas\nHelium or H2\nrho_ref known"]
    end

    subgraph FlowSplitter[Flow Splitter]
        split1["50 percent to\nReference Cavity"]
        split2["50 percent to\nSample Cavity"]
    end

    subgraph ReferenceSide[Reference Side]
        refCav["Reference Cavity\nResistance R1"]
    end

    subgraph SampleSide[Sample Side]
        sampCav["Sample Cavity\nFlushed by Process Gas"]
    end

    subgraph DiaphragmSensing[Sensing Diaphragm]
        diaphragm["Pressure Differential\nDetection"]
        sigOut["Signal E_out"]
    end

    carrier --> split1
    carrier --> split2
    split1 --> refCav
    split2 --> sampCav
    refCav --> diaphragm
    sampCav --> diaphragm
    diaphragm --> sigOut
```

> **Interpretation:** Carrier gas flows in two parallel paths — one through a sealed reference cavity, one through a sample cavity flushed by the unknown gas. Any density difference creates a pressure imbalance, deflecting the diaphragm to produce the output signal.

---

### 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Gas Specific Gravity Measuring System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ReferenceSphere[Reference Sphere]
        airSphere["Filled with Dry Air\nrho_a = 1.225 kg/m3"]
        vRef["Volume V0"]
    end

    subgraph SampleSphere[Sample Sphere]
        gasSphere["Filled with Process Gas\nrho_g unknown"]
        vSamp["Volume V0"]
    end

    subgraph BalanceBeam[Torsion Balance Beam]
        beam["Pivot Point\nPneumatic / EM Restoring"]
        frOut["Restoring Force Fr"]
    end

    subgraph TempControl[Temperature Bath]
        bath["Constant T Bath\nWater or Oil"]
        sensor["RTD / Pt100"]
    end

    subgraph Computation[Solver]
        sgCalc["SG = 1 + Fr / rho_a V0 g"]
        sgDisp["Specific Gravity\nDisplay"]
    end

    airSphere --> beam
    gasSphere --> beam
    beam --> frOut
    beam --> bath
    sensor --> sgCalc
    frOut --> sgCalc
    sgCalc --> sgDisp
```

> **Interpretation:** Both spheres hang on a balance beam in a temperature-controlled bath. Any difference in mass tilts the beam; the restoring force is a direct linear measure of the specific gravity $SG = \rho_g / \rho_a$.

---

### 4.5 Functional Architecture — Full Density Measurement System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SensorLayer[Primary Sensing Layer]
        s1["Constant Volume\nHydrometer"]
        s2["Air Pressure\nBalance"]
        s3["Gas Density\nDetector"]
        s4["Specific Gravity\nBalance"]
    end

    subgraph TransducerLayer[Signal Conversion]
        t1["Strain Gauge\nVoltage Signal"]
        t2["DPT Output\n4 to 20 mA"]
        t3["Diaphragm\nDeflection mV"]
        t4["EM Restoring\nForce mA"]
    end

    subgraph ProcessingLayer[Signal Processing]
        cpu["Microprocessor /\nDCS Controller"]
        tComp["Temperature\nCompensation"]
        pComp["Pressure\nCompensation"]
        linCal["Linearisation\nLookup Table"]
    end

    subgraph OutputLayer[Output and HMI]
        hmi["HMI Display\nkg per m3 or SG"]
        valve["Control Valve\nFeedback"]
        log["Data Historian\nCustody Records"]
    end

    s1 --> t1 --> cpu
    s2 --> t2 --> cpu
    s3 --> t3 --> cpu
    s4 --> t4 --> cpu
    cpu --> tComp
    cpu --> pComp
    cpu --> linCal
    cpu --> hmi
    cpu --> valve
    cpu --> log
```

> **Interpretation:** This integrated architecture shows how all four sensing principles converge into a common digital processing layer with temperature/pressure compensation and linearisation, before being displayed, recorded, or used for closed-loop control.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. Define density and specific gravity. State their SI units. (3 Marks)**
> `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]`

**Model Answer:**

- **Density (ρ):** The mass per unit volume of a substance. $$\rho = \dfrac{m}{V} \quad [\text{kg/m}^3]$$
- **Specific Gravity (SG):** The dimensionless ratio of the density of a substance to that of a reference substance (usually water for liquids, air for gases). $$SG = \dfrac{\rho_{substance}}{\rho_{reference}}$$
- **SI Units:** Density → kg/m³ (or g/cm³); Specific Gravity → dimensionless.

---

> **Q2. With a neat sketch, explain the working of a constant volume hydrometer. (3 Marks)**
> `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]`

**Model Answer:**

A constant volume hydrometer consists of a sealed metallic plummet of fixed, precisely known volume $V_0$ suspended from a force-measuring element (strain gauge / load cell). When fully immersed in the liquid, the plummet experiences a buoyancy force $F_b = \rho \cdot g \cdot V_0$. The apparent weight $W_{app} = W_0 - F_b$ is read by the strain gauge. Since $V_0$ is constant, $W_{app}$ is linearly related to $\rho$. The instrument is calibrated to give a direct density reading. The diagram is shown in Section 4.1 above.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note on KTU pattern:** Each Part-B carries 14 marks and is split into two sub-parts of 7 marks each. The student is given internal choice — either **Question A** or **Question B** must be answered.

---

#### QUESTION A — (14 Marks)

> **Q A(a).** Describe with a neat diagram the principle, construction, and working of a **Constant Volume Hydrometer** for the measurement of liquid density. Derive the relationship between the measured force and the liquid density. **(7 Marks)**
>
> `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Module 2, 14 Mark]`

**Model Solution:**

**Principle:** A plummet of fixed volume $V_0$ is immersed in the liquid. The buoyancy force on it, given by Archimedes' principle, is:

$$F_b = \rho_l \cdot g \cdot V_0$$

The apparent weight loss of the plummet when transferred from air to the liquid is:

$$W_{app} = m_p \cdot g - \rho_l \cdot g \cdot V_0$$

Solving for $\rho_l$:

$$\rho_l = \dfrac{m_p \cdot g - W_{app}}{g \cdot V_0}$$

**Construction:**

- Plummet — sealed hollow metal cylinder/sphere, $V_0$ typically 50–500 cm³, made of stainless steel or Hastelloy for corrosive liquids.
- Suspension wire — fine, non-corrosive, negligible buoyancy contribution.
- Force transducer — strain-gauge bridge glued to a flexure; output proportional to apparent weight.
- Reference mass — built-in for in-situ self-calibration.
- Thermometer / RTD — for temperature compensation (since ρ is temperature-dependent).

**Working:**

1. Plummet is lowered into the process liquid until fully immersed.
2. Buoyancy reduces the apparent weight of the plummet.
3. The strain gauge bridge produces an output voltage proportional to the apparent weight loss.
4. The signal conditioner computes $\rho_l$ using the calibration equation above.
5. Output is displayed in kg/m³ and may be transmitted as 4–20 mA for DCS integration.

---

> **Q A(b).** With a block diagram, explain the **Air Pressure Balance Method** of density measurement. Derive an expression for the sensitivity of the system. **(7 Marks)**
>
> `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Module 2, 14 Mark]`

**Model Solution:**

**Principle (Block diagram shown in Section 4.2):** Two identical vertical columns of height $h$ are connected at the top to a common pressure source $P_0$ and at the bottom to a differential pressure transmitter. One column contains dry air (density $\rho_a$); the other contains the sample gas/liquid (density $\rho_s$).

**Derivation:**

At the bottom of the air column:

$$P_1 = P_0 + \rho_a \cdot g \cdot h$$

At the bottom of the sample column:

$$P_2 = P_0 + \rho_s \cdot g \cdot h$$

Differential pressure:

$$\Delta P = P_2 - P_1 = (\rho_s - \rho_a) \cdot g \cdot h$$

Solving for $\rho_s$:

$$\rho_s = \rho_a + \dfrac{\Delta P}{g \cdot h}$$

**Sensitivity:**

$$\mathcal{S} = \dfrac{d(\Delta P)}{d\rho_s} = g \cdot h \quad [\text{Pa per kg/m}^3]$$

For $h = 1\,\text{m}$: $\mathcal{S} = 9.81\,\text{Pa/(kg/m}^3\text{)}$.

**Procedure:**

1. Pressurize both columns from a stable, common source.
2. Measure the differential pressure at the bottom manometer.
3. Substitute into the equation to obtain $\rho_s$.
4. Output to DCS / recorder / flow computer (for custody transfer).

---

#### QUESTION B — (14 Marks, Alternative to Q.A)

> **Q B(a).** Explain with a neat sketch the working of a **Gas Density Detector** used in gas chromatography. What are its advantages and limitations? **(7 Marks)**
>
> `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Module 2, 14 Mark Alternative]`

**Model Solution:**

**Working Principle (diagram in Section 4.3):** The GDD is a pneumatic Wheatstone bridge. Carrier gas (typically Helium or Hydrogen) flows from a common inlet and is split equally into two parallel cavities: a sealed reference cavity and a sample cavity continuously flushed by the process gas. A pressure-sensing diaphragm at the common outlet deflects in proportion to the difference in volumetric flow rates, which is inversely proportional to the gas density. The output signal is proportional to $(\rho_{sample} - \rho_{ref})$.

**Advantages:**

- *Universal response*: Sensitivity depends only on molecular weight, not on chemical nature. Detects permanent gases (H₂, O₂, N₂, CO, CO₂, CH₄, noble gases) that FID/TCD cannot easily quantify.
- *Non-destructive*: Sample is not consumed; can be recovered for further analysis.
- *Linear response* in many ranges (with appropriate calibration).
- *Stable, predictable* — no flame or hot filament required.

**Limitations:**

- *Sensitive to pressure and temperature fluctuations*: Both must be precisely controlled.
- *Lower sensitivity than FID* for hydrocarbons.
- *Calibration depends on carrier gas choice*: Switching from He to H₂ requires re-calibration.
- *Slow response* compared to FID (typical response time ~1 s).

---

> **Q B(b).** With a neat diagram, describe the working of a **Gas Specific Gravity Measuring System**. How is the specific gravity of natural gas measured using this principle? **(7 Marks)**
>
> `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Module 2, 14 Mark Alternative]`

**Model Solution:**

**Principle (diagram in Section 4.4):** Two sealed spheres of identical volume $V_0$ are mounted on opposite ends of a sensitive balance beam. One sphere contains dry air (reference); the other is evacuated and filled with the process gas. Both spheres are immersed in a temperature-controlled water bath to eliminate temperature drift.

The net downward force on the sample side is:

$$F_{net} = (\rho_g - \rho_a) \cdot V_0 \cdot g$$

An electromagnetic or pneumatic restoring force $F_r$ balances this torque:

$$F_r = (\rho_g - \rho_a) \cdot V_0 \cdot g$$

Dividing by $\rho_a V_0 g$:

$$SG = \dfrac{\rho_g}{\rho_a} = 1 + \dfrac{F_r}{\rho_a \cdot V_0 \cdot g}$$

**For Natural Gas:** Natural gas (methane-rich) has $M \approx 16$ to $20$ kg/kmol versus air's $M = 28.97$ kg/kmol. Therefore, $\rho_g < \rho_a$, the sample sphere experiences net *buoyancy*, the beam tilts the opposite way, and the restoring force is **negative** ($F_r < 0$). The measured SG is typically in the range **0.55 to 0.70**, indicating that natural gas is lighter than air and will rise if released — a critical safety consideration in LNG plant design.

**Components:**

- Twin balanced spheres ($V_0 = 100$ to $500 \,\text{cm}^3$).
- Precision torsion balance with photoelectric null detector.
- Electromagnetic feedback coil for restoring force.
- Pt-100 RTD for temperature monitoring.
- Microprocessor for SG computation and pressure/temperature compensation.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE marks on density measurement questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units in the final answer.** Always write "$\rho = 950 \,\text{kg/m}^3$", not "$\rho = 950$". KTU examiners deduct 1–2 marks specifically for missing/wrong units.
>
> 2. **Confusing SG with density.** SG is dimensionless; density has units. Mixing these up costs full marks on definition questions.
>
> 3. **Sign convention errors in Air Pressure Balance.** If the sample gas is *lighter* than air (e.g., natural gas), $\Delta P$ is *negative*. Many students write it as positive without justification.
>
> 4. **Ignoring temperature compensation.** Gas density varies by ~0.3 % per °C. Always mention $\rho = PM/(RT)$ correction in your answer — KTU specifically rewards this.
>
> 5. **Skipping the diagram.** A "neat sketch" carries **2 marks** in any 7-mark sub-question. Always include a labelled block diagram, even a rough one.
>
> 6. **Mixing Constant Volume with Variable Volume hydrometers.** Variable volume hydrometers (floating-glass type) follow $\rho \propto 1/h^3$; constant volume follows $\rho \propto F_b$. Examiners test this distinction deliberately.
>
> 7. **Using $\rho_{water} = 1000 \,\text{kg/m}^3$ for gas SG problems.** For gases, the reference is air ($\rho_{air} = 1.225 \,\text{kg/m}^3$), not water. Using water gives an absurdly large SG.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap — High-Density Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Fire Revision: 20 Critical Points to Remember**

- ☐ **Density** = mass / volume; SI unit **kg/m³**.
- ☐ **Specific Gravity** = ratio of densities; **dimensionless**.
- ☐ **Reference for liquid SG** = water at 4 °C ($\rho_w = 1000 \,\text{kg/m}^3$).
- ☐ **Reference for gas SG** = dry air at STP ($\rho_a = 1.225 \,\text{kg/m}^3$, $M_{air} = 28.97 \,\text{kg/kmol}$).
- ☐ **Archimedes' principle**: $F_b = \rho \cdot g \cdot V_0$ is the foundation of every buoyancy-based densitometer.
- ☐ **Constant Volume Hydrometer** → linear in ρ; uses strain-gauge load cell.
- ☐ **Variable Volume Hydrometer** → non-linear ($\rho \propto 1/h^3$); traditional floating-glass type.
- ☐ **Air Pressure Balance** → measures $\Delta P = (\rho_s - \rho_a) \cdot g \cdot h$.
- ☐ **Sensitivity of Air Pressure Balance** = $g \cdot h$ in Pa/(kg/m³).
- ☐ **Gas Density Detector** → pneumatic Wheatstone bridge; universal in chromatography.
- ☐ **GDD output** $\propto \rho_{sample} - \rho_{ref}$; carrier gas = He or H₂.
- ☐ **Gas Specific Gravity** = $\rho_{gas} / \rho_{air} = M_{gas} / M_{air}$ (at same $P$, $T$).
- ☐ **Natural gas** $M \approx 16$–20 kg/kmol → $SG \approx 0.55$–0.70 (lighter than air, rises).
- ☐ **LPG / propane** $M \approx 44$ kg/kmol → $SG > 1$ (heavier than air, pools at ground).
- ☐ **Temperature compensation** is mandatory because $\rho = PM/(RT)$.
- ☐ **Universal gas constant** $R = 8.314 \,\text{kJ/(kmol·K)}$.
- ☐ **Ideal-gas density** $\rho = PM/(RT)$ is valid for low-pressure process gases.
- ☐ **Calibration of constant-volume hydrometer**: $\rho = \rho_p - W_{app}/(g V_0)$.
- ☐ **Custody transfer accuracy** → SG measuring system (±0.02 %).
- ☐ **Real-world applications**: petroleum pipelines, dairy, brewing, paper pulp, natural-gas distribution, LNG processing.

> [!TIP]
> **One-Line Memory Hook (Exam Hall Mnemonic):**
> **"Buoyancy for liquids, Pressure-Balance for gases, Detector for chromatographs, Balance Beam for SG."** — This sentence alone can fetch you 4 out of 7 marks on any Part-B question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flow Measurement Using Head Type Flowmeters Based on Differential Pressure: Orifice Meter, Venturi Meter, Flow Nozzle, and Pitot Tube
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECHT724 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Flow Measurement Using Head Type Flowmeters Based on Differential Pressure: Orifice Meter, Venturi Meter, Flow Nozzle, and Pitot Tube # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Head-type flowmeters** are a class of inferential flow measurement devices that determine the volumetric or mass flow rate of a fluid by inducing a measurable **differential pressure head** through a deliberate constriction (primary element) in the flow stream. The governing physical law is the **Bernoulli principle** combined with the **continuity equation**. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Definition:** "A head flowmeter measures flow rate by creating a pressure differential through a known obstruction, converting kinetic energy into a measurable pressure head that can be correlated to flow velocity." The four principal devices under this classification are: | # | Flowmeter | Distinctive Primary Element | |---|-----------|------------------------------| | 1 | **Orifice Meter** | Thin concentric sharp-edged plate | | 2 | **Venturi Meter** | Convergent → Throat → Divergent profile | | 3 | **Flow Nozzle** | Smooth bell-shaped convergent nozzle | | 4 | **Pitot Tube** | Open-ended tube sensing stagnation pressure | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition) Imagine you are standing in a wide, slow-moving river. Now imagine placing a large boulder in the middle of the river. What happens? - The water **speeds up** as it squeezes past the boulder. - The water **rises higher** against the boulder (because it pushes harder), but **drops lower** immediately behind it. This is exactly what happens inside a pipe with a restriction. The fluid speeds up (kinetic energy ↑), the pressure falls (potential energy ↓), and if we measure the pressure drop between two points, we can mathematically back-calculate the original flow rate. **Every head-type flowmeter exploits this single principle** — they only differ in *how* the restriction is shaped. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight:** A bigger pressure drop ⇒ faster flow ⇒ higher flow rate. The relationship is **square root** in nature — doubling the differential pressure does **not** double the flow rate; it increases it by only **√2 ≈ 1.414 times**. ## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics - **Acceleration due to gravity, g = 9.81 m/s²** - **Density of water, ρ_water = 1000 kg/m³** - **Specific gravity of mercury, S_Hg = 13.6** - **Standard temperature, T = 25 °C (298.15 K)** for calibration - **Pipe diameters** are commonly specified in **mm** or **inches** - **Differential pressure (ΔP)** is commonly expressed in **kPa, mbar, or inches of water column (inH₂O)** > [!TIP] > **KTU Board Tip:** Examiner's love asking for the **range of coefficient of discharge (Cd)** for each meter. Memorize: > - Orifice: **0.60 – 0.65** > - Venturi: **0.95 – 0.98** > - Flow Nozzle: **0.93 – 0.98** # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Underlying Physics — The Two Governing Laws ### Law 1: Bernoulli's Equation (Energy Conservation) For an ideal, incompressible, steady, frictionless flow along a streamline: $$P_1 + \frac{1}{2}\rho V_1^2 + \rho g z_1 = P_2 + \frac{1}{2}\rho V_2^2 + \rho g z_2$$ For a **horizontal pipe** (z₁ = z₂), this simplifies to: $$P_1 - P_2 = \frac{\rho}{2}\left(V_2^2 - V_1^2\right)$$ The pressure drop **ΔP = P₁ − P₂** is generated purely by the velocity increase. ### Law 2: Continuity Equation (Mass Conservation) For an incompressible fluid: $$A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$$ This means when the area shrinks, velocity rises in inverse proportion. ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Head Flowmeter Operation The head flowmeter works in five logical stages: - **Stage 1 — Approach:** Fluid enters the flowmeter in the upstream section with cross-section A₁, pressure P₁, velocity V₁. - **Stage 2 — Acceleration:** Fluid is forced through a constriction of cross-section A₂ (< A₁). Due to continuity, velocity rises to V₂ > V₁. - **Stage 3 — Pressure Drop:** Due to Bernoulli, the higher velocity V₂ corresponds to a lower static pressure P₂. - **Stage 4 — Sensing:** Two pressure tappings (upstream and downstream) feed a **differential pressure transducer** or manometer which records Δh or ΔP. - **Stage 5 — Computation:** ΔP is converted to flow rate Q using the **discharge equation** involving the coefficient of discharge C_d. ## 2.3 The Universal Discharge Equation All four head-type flowmeters are governed by the same equation — only **C_d** and geometry differ: $$Q = \frac{C_d \, A_2 \, \sqrt{2 \, \Delta P / \rho}}{\sqrt{1 - \left(A_2/A_1\right)^2}}$$ Equivalently, in terms of differential head h (meters of fluid): $$Q = \frac{C_d \, A_2 \, \sqrt{2 g h}}{\sqrt{1 - \left(D_2/D_1\right)^4}}$$ Mass flow rate form: $$\dot{m} = \frac{C_d \, A_2 \, \sqrt{2 \, \rho \, \Delta P}}{\sqrt{1 - \left(D_2/D_1\right)^4}}$$ ## 2.4 Velocity of Approach Factor (K) Because the upstream velocity V₁ is non-zero, a correction factor is introduced: $$K = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 - \left(D_2/D_1\right)^4}}$$ This factor multiplies the theoretical discharge to account for the kinetic energy already present in the upstream fluid. ## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Equation | Description / Application | |---|----------|----------------------------| | 1 | $P_1 + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_1^2 = P_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_2^2$ | Bernoulli (horizontal pipe) | | 2 | $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$ | Continuity equation | | 3 | $h = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\rho g}$ | Differential pressure head | | 4 | $Q = \dfrac{C_d A_2 \sqrt{2 \Delta P / \rho}}{\sqrt{1 - \left(D_2/D_1\right)^4}}$ | Volume flow rate (universal) | | 5 | $Q = \dfrac{C_d A_2 \sqrt{2 g h}}{\sqrt{1 - \left(D_2/D_1\right)^4}}$ | Q in terms of head h | | 6 | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{C_d A_2 \sqrt{2 \rho \Delta P}}{\sqrt{1 - \left(D_2/D_1\right)^4}}$ | Mass flow rate form | | 7 | $K = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 - \left(D_2/D_1\right)^4}}$ | Velocity of approach factor | | 8 | $h_{\text{water}} = h_{\text{Hg}} \left(\dfrac{S_{\text{Hg}}}{S_{\text{water}}} - 1\right)$ | Manometer reading conversion | | 9 | $C_d = \dfrac{Q_{\text{actual}}}{Q_{\text{theoretical}}}$ | Definition of Cd | | 10 | $C_c = \dfrac{A_c}{A_2}, \quad C_v = \dfrac{V_{\text{actual}}}{V_{\text{theoretical}}}$ | Coefficient of contraction & velocity | ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Orifice meter:** Standard in oil & gas custody transfer, steam lines, and chemical process plants — cheap and easy to install. - **Venturi meter:** Used where low permanent pressure loss is critical — large water mains, HVAC, penstocks of hydroelectric plants. - **Flow nozzle:** Preferred for **high-velocity steam** in power stations; ASME-approved. - **Pitot tube:** Used in aircraft **airspeed indicators**, boiler flue gas analysis, and open-channel flow estimation. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Detailed Working for Each Flowmeter ### 3.1.1 Orifice Meter **Construction:** A thin stainless-steel plate (typically **1.5 mm to 6 mm thick**) with a precisely machined **concentric circular hole** whose upstream edge is sharp and square. Pressure is tapped either via **flange taps, corner taps, or D–D/2 taps**. **Working — full derivation from Bernoulli's equation:** Let section (1) = upstream of the orifice, section (2) = at the **vena contracta** (the point just downstream where the jet is narrowest). Apply Bernoulli's equation between (1) and (2) for a horizontal pipe: $$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g}$$ Rearranging for the head difference h = (P₁ − P₂)/(ρg): $$\frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g} = h = \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2g}$$ Therefore: $$V_2^2 - V_1^2 = 2 g h$$ From continuity equation: $$V_1 = \frac{A_2}{A_1} V_2 = \left(\frac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^2 V_2$$ Substituting V₁ in terms of V₂: $$\begin{aligned} V_2^2 - \left(\frac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^4 V_2^2 &= 2 g h \\ V_2^2 \left[1 - \left(\frac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^4\right] &= 2 g h \end{aligned}$$ Solving for V₂: $$V_2 = \frac{\sqrt{2 g h}}{\sqrt{1 - \left(D_2/D_1\right)^4}}$$ Theoretical discharge (without losses): $$Q_{\text{th}} = A_2 V_2 = \frac{A_2 \sqrt{2 g h}}{\sqrt{1 - \left(D_2/D_1\right)^4}}$$ Introducing coefficient of discharge C_d (accounts for contraction and viscous effects): $$\boxed{\,Q = \frac{C_d \, A_2 \, \sqrt{2 g h}}{\sqrt{1 - \left(D_2/D_1\right)^4}}\,}$$ In terms of ΔP (using h = ΔP/ρg): $$Q = C_d \, A_2 \sqrt{\frac{2 \, \Delta P}{\rho}} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 - \left(D_2/D_1\right)^4}}$$ ### 3.1.2 Venturi Meter **Construction:** Three sections in series — an **entrance cone** (convergent, half-angle ≈ 20°–21°), a **cylindrical throat** of diameter D₂, and an **exit cone** (divergent, half-angle ≈ 5°–7°). **Working:** The smoothly diverging recovery cone recovers about **85%–90%** of the kinetic energy as pressure, making the venturi highly efficient. The same Bernoulli equation (between inlet and throat) applies identically, but because of the streamlined geometry, **C_d is much higher (≈ 0.95–0.98)** than for an orifice plate. $$Q_{\text{venturi}} = \frac{C_d \, A_2 \, \sqrt{2 \, \Delta P / \rho}}{\sqrt{1 - \left(D_2/D_1\right)^4}}$$ ### 3.1.3 Flow Nozzle **Construction:** A streamlined nozzle with a converging bell-shape (ASME long-radius nozzle is the standard). **No divergent section** is present — the flow exits directly into the pipe. **Working:** Because the contraction is smoother than an orifice, C_d ≈ 0.95–0.98. Particularly suited for **high-velocity steam** and **superheated fluids** where cavitation or erosion would damage an orifice plate. Permanent pressure loss is intermediate (≈ 30%–40%). $$Q_{\text{nozzle}} = \frac{C_d \, A_2 \, \sqrt{2 \, \Delta P / \rho}}{\sqrt{1 - \left(D_2/D_1\right)^4}}$$ ### 3.1.4 Pitot Tube **Construction:** An L-shaped tube with an **open end facing the flow** (stagnation opening) and a **side opening perpendicular to flow** (static opening). Sometimes two concentric tubes form the **Pitot-static tube**. **Working:** The forward-facing opening senses the **stagnation (total) pressure** P_t = P_static + ½ρV². The side opening senses only **static pressure** P_s. Their difference equals the **dynamic pressure**: $$\Delta P = P_t - P_s = \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2$$ Solving for velocity at the measurement point: $$V = \sqrt{\frac{2 \, \Delta P}{\rho}}$$ The **point velocity** V at the tip location is found, then average velocity V̄ = K_pitot × V (where K_pitot ≈ 0.85 for turbulent flow in pipes). **Note:** The Pitot tube measures **local velocity**, not total flow rate directly. To get flow, integrate across the pipe cross-section or assume a velocity profile. ## 3.2 Boundary Conditions and Validity | Condition | Applicability | |-----------|---------------| | Reynolds number Re > 10⁴ (turbulent flow) | Discharge equation valid | | Incompressible fluid (liquids, low-Mach gases) | Bernoulli applies directly | | Fully developed upstream profile (straight pipe ≥ 10D₁ before meter) | Required for accuracy | | D₂/D₁ ≥ 0.2 (avoid excessive vena contracta) | Geometric constraint | | Steady flow (∂V/∂t = 0) | Bernoulli applicable | ## 3.3 Comparison Matrix of Head-Type Flowmeters | Parameter | Orifice | Venturi | Flow Nozzle | Pitot Tube | |-----------|---------|---------|-------------|------------| | C_d (typical) | 0.60–0.65 | 0.95–0.98 | 0.95–0.98 | 0.98–1.00 (Pt) | | Permanent pressure loss | 40%–60% | 10%–15% | 30%–40% | Negligible | | Construction cost | **Low** | **High** | Moderate | **Low** | | Accuracy | ±1%–2% | ±0.5%–1% | ±1% | ±2%–3% | | Best suited for | General process | Water mains, large flows | High-velocity steam | Point velocity | | Erosion resistance | Poor | Excellent | Good | Excellent | | Installation space | Minimal | Long | Moderate | Minimal | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Functional Architecture (Mermaid) ## 4.1 Universal Operating Principle Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Inlet Flow
Pressure P1, Velocity V1] --> B[Primary Element
Restriction Creates Acceleration] B --> C[Constricted Throat
Higher Velocity V2, Lower Pressure P2] C --> D[Differential Pressure
DeltaP = P1 minus P2] D --> E[DP Transducer or Manometer] E --> F[Signal Conditioner and Square Root Extractor] F --> G[Flow Rate Display
Q in cubic meter per hour or L per s] H[Bernoulli Equation] -.applies to.-> B I[Continuity Equation] -.applies to.-> B ``` ## 4.2 Orifice Meter — Functional Architecture Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[Upstream Pipe
Diameter D1
Pressure P1
Velocity V1] --> A2[Sharp Edged Concentric Orifice Plate
Hole Diameter D2] A2 --> A3[Vena Contracta Region
Smallest Stream Area Ac
Maximum Velocity V2] A3 --> A4[Downstream Recovery Zone
Turbulent Eddy Zone
Permanent Pressure Loss] T1[Upstream Pressure Tap] --- A2 T2[Downstream Pressure Tap] --- A3 T1 --> M1[Differential Manometer or Transmitter] T2 --> M1 M1 --> M2[Read DeltaP or Differential Head h] M2 --> CALC[Apply Discharge Equation with Cd] CALC --> OUT[Volumetric Flow Q and Mass Flow m dot] ``` ## 4.3 Venturi Meter — Functional Architecture Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR V1[Inlet Cylinder
Diameter D1] --> V2[Convergent Cone
Half Angle 20 to 21 deg] V2 --> V3[Throat Cylinder
Diameter D2
Length equals 1 to 2 times D2] V3 --> V4[Divergent Cone
Half Angle 5 to 7 deg
Pressure Recovery Zone] V4 --> V5[Outlet Cylinder
Diameter D1] PT1[Pressure Tap at Inlet P1] --- V1 PT2[Pressure Tap at Throat P2] --- V3 PT1 --> DP[Differential Pressure Measurement] PT2 --> DP DP --> Q[Apply High Cd Discharge Equation
Cd about 0.98] Q --> OUTF[Smooth High Recovery Flow Rate Output] ``` ## 4.4 Pitot Tube — Functional Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR FL[Fluid Stream
Local Velocity V] --> ST[Stagnation Opening
Faces Flow Direction] FL --> SS[Static Side Opening
Perpendicular to Flow] ST --> PT1[Total Pressure Pt] SS --> PT2[Static Pressure Ps] PT1 --> DIFF[Differential Element] PT2 --> DIFF DIFF --> DP[Dynamic Pressure DeltaP] DP --> EQ[Velocity Equation V equals sqrt of 2 DeltaP over rho] EQ --> VP[Point Velocity at Tip Location] VP --> AVG[Apply Pitot Coefficient Kp about 0.85] AVG --> VA[Average Pipe Velocity] VA --> QF[Compute Flow via Q equals A times Vavg] ``` ## 4.5 Comparative Decision Tree (Which Flowmeter to Select?) ```mermaid flowchart TD START[Need Flow Measurement] --> Q1{Budget Tight?} Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{High Accuracy Critical?} Q1 -- No --> Q3{Long Straight Pipe Available?} Q2 -- Yes --> Q4{Need for Steam or High Temp?} Q2 -- No --> OFM[Use ORIFICE METER
Cheap and Easy to Install] Q4 -- Yes --> FN[Use FLOW NOZZLE
Handles High Velocity Steam] Q4 -- No --> OFM2[Use ORIFICE METER] Q3 -- Yes --> VM[Use VENTURI METER
Lowest Permanent Loss] Q3 -- No --> FN2[Use FLOW NOZZLE
Compact and Durable] START -.alternative.-> PT[Need Point Velocity Only?
Use PITOT TUBE] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📘 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define the term "coefficient of discharge" for a head-type flowmeter. State its typical value range for (i) orifice meter and (ii) venturi meter.** **Model Answer:** The **coefficient of discharge (C_d)** is defined as the ratio of the **actual flow rate** to the **theoretical flow rate** that would occur in an ideal, frictionless fluid. $$C_d = \frac{Q_{\text{actual}}}{Q_{\text{theoretical}}}$$ It is a dimensionless number less than 1 and accounts for two real-fluid effects: - **Vena contracta** — the jet contracts beyond the physical orifice. - **Viscous and turbulent losses** — friction along the walls. Typical values: | Flowmeter | Typical C_d | |-----------|-------------| | Orifice Meter | **0.60 – 0.65** | | Venturi Meter | **0.95 – 0.98** | --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Differentiate between a Venturi meter and an Orifice meter on the basis of (i) construction, (ii) coefficient of discharge, and (iii) permanent pressure loss.** **Model Answer:** | Basis | Venturi Meter | Orifice Meter | |-------|---------------|---------------| | Construction | Convergent cone + cylindrical throat + divergent cone | Simple thin plate with concentric sharp-edged hole | | Coefficient of discharge | 0.95 – 0.98 | 0.60 – 0.65 | | Permanent pressure loss | 10% – 15% | 40% – 60% | | Cost | High | Low | | Space required | Long (≈ 4D to 6D) | Short (≈ D) | The Venturi recovers most of its kinetic energy into pressure via its long divergent cone, whereas the orifice dissipates this energy as turbulent eddies downstream. --- ## 📕 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Provided) --- ### ✏️ Option A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]` #### (a) Derive the general discharge equation for an orifice meter starting from Bernoulli's equation. State the assumptions clearly. **[7 Marks]** **Model Solution:** **Assumptions:** 1. Fluid is **incompressible** (ρ = constant). 2. Flow is **steady** (∂V/∂t = 0). 3. Flow is along a **horizontal** pipe (z₁ = z₂). 4. Fluid is **ideal** at the application point (losses absorbed by C_d). 5. Pressure tappings are at the upstream section and at the **vena contracta**. **Derivation:** Apply Bernoulli's equation between section (1) — upstream, and section (2) — vena contracta: $$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g}$$ Rearranging: $$\frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g} = h = \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2g}$$ $$\therefore \; V_2^2 - V_1^2 = 2 g h$$ From continuity equation: $$A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 \;\Rightarrow\; V_1 = \frac{A_2}{A_1} V_2 = \left(\frac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^2 V_2$$ Substituting: $$\begin{aligned} V_2^2 - \left(\frac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^4 V_2^2 &= 2 g h \\ V_2^2 \left[1 - \left(\frac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^4\right] &= 2 g h \\ V_2 &= \frac{\sqrt{2 g h}}{\sqrt{1 - \left(D_2/D_1\right)^4}} \end{aligned}$$ Theoretical discharge (ideal): $$Q_{\text{th}} = A_2 V_2 = \frac{A_2 \sqrt{2 g h}}{\sqrt{1 - \left(D_2/D_1\right)^4}}$$ Actual discharge (using C_d): $$\boxed{\;Q = \frac{C_d \, A_2 \sqrt{2 g h}}{\sqrt{1 - \left(D_2/D_1\right)^4}}\;}$$ Or in terms of pressure difference (using h = ΔP/ρg): $$Q = C_d \, A_2 \, \sqrt{\frac{2 \, \Delta P}{\rho}} \, \bigg/ \sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^4}$$ --- #### (b) An orifice meter of diameter **50 mm** is fitted in a pipe of diameter **100 mm**. If the coefficient of discharge is **0.62** and the differential pressure is **10 kPa**, calculate the (i) volume flow rate, (ii) mass flow rate, and (iii) velocity of approach factor for water flow. **[7 Marks]** **Given:** - D₁ = 100 mm = 0.1 m - D₂ = 50 mm = 0.05 m - C_d = 0.62 - ΔP = 10 kPa = 10,000 Pa - ρ = 1000 kg/m³ (water) **Step 1: Cross-sectional area of the orifice** $$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4} D_2^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.05)^2 = 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 2: Velocity of approach factor (iii)** $$\left(\frac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^4 = (0.5)^4 = 0.0625$$ $$K = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 - 0.0625}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{0.9375}} = \frac{1}{0.9683} = 1.0328$$ $$\boxed{K = 1.0328}$$ **Step 3: Volume flow rate (i)** $$Q = \frac{C_d \, A_2 \, \sqrt{2 \Delta P / \rho}}{\sqrt{1 - (D_2/D_1)^4}}$$ $$\sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 10{,}000}{1000}} = \sqrt{20} = 4.4721 \text{ m/s}$$ $$Q = \frac{0.62 \times 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \times 4.4721}{0.9683}$$ $$Q = \frac{0.62 \times 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \times 4.4721}{0.9683} = \frac{5.441 \times 10^{-3}}{0.9683}$$ $$\boxed{Q = 5.62 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 5.62 \text{ L/s}}$$ **Step 4: Mass flow rate (ii)** $$\dot{m} = \rho Q = 1000 \times 5.62 \times 10^{-3}$$ $$\boxed{\dot{m} = 5.62 \text{ kg/s}}$$ --- ### ✏️ Option B `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Modified]` #### (a) With the help of a functional diagram, explain the construction and working of a Venturi meter. Compare its performance with an orifice meter. **[7 Marks]** **Model Solution:** **Construction:** A Venturi meter consists of three sections in series: 1. **Inlet section** — a cylindrical portion of diameter D₁. 2. **Convergent cone (entry cone)** — the cross-section smoothly reduces from D₁ to D₂ over a half-angle of approximately **20° to 21°**. 3. **Throat** — a short cylindrical section of constant reduced diameter D₂ (length ≈ 1 to 2 × D₂). 4. **Divergent cone (exit cone)** — the cross-section smoothly expands back to D₁ over a much smaller half-angle of **5° to 7°**. 5. **Pressure tappings** — one at the inlet (P₁) and one at the throat (P₂), connected to a differential manometer or DP transmitter. **Working:** As the fluid enters the convergent cone, its velocity gradually rises (by continuity) and its static pressure falls (by Bernoulli). At the throat, the velocity reaches its maximum and pressure its minimum. The differential pressure (P₁ − P₂) is measured. Because the geometry is streamlined: - There is **no vena contracta** — the flow follows the walls smoothly. - Viscous losses are very small. - C_d is therefore very high (0.95 – 0.98). The discharge equation is the same form as the orifice meter: $$Q = \frac{C_d \, A_2 \sqrt{2 g h}}{\sqrt{1 - (D_2/D_1)^4}}$$ **Comparison with Orifice Meter:** | Parameter | Venturi | Orifice | |-----------|---------|---------| | Profile | Smooth convergent-throat-divergent | Sharp-edged thin plate | | C_d | 0.95 – 0.98 | 0.60 – 0.65 | | Permanent pressure loss | 10% – 15% | 40% – 60% | | Length required | 4D to 6D | ≈ 1D | | Cost | High | Low | | Maintenance | Self-cleaning | Erosion-prone | The divergent cone of the Venturi gradually decelerates the fluid, recovering most of the kinetic energy as pressure — explaining its low permanent loss and high accuracy. --- #### (b) A venturi meter is fitted to a horizontal pipe of diameter **200 mm**. The throat diameter is **100 mm**. The differential pressure shown by a mercury-water manometer is **50 cm of mercury**. Calculate the discharge of water through the meter. Take **C_d = 0.98**. **[7 Marks]** **Given:** - D₁ = 200 mm = 0.2 m - D₂ (throat) = 100 mm = 0.1 m - h_Hg = 50 cm = 0.5 m - C_d = 0.98 - S_Hg = 13.6, S_water = 1.0 **Step 1: Equivalent head of water** $$h_{\text{water}} = h_{\text{Hg}} \left(\frac{S_{\text{Hg}}}{S_{\text{water}}} - 1\right) = 0.5 \times (13.6 - 1) = 0.5 \times 12.6 = 6.3 \text{ m of water}$$ **Step 2: Throat area** $$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4} D_2^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.1)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 3: Diameter ratio term** $$\left(\frac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^4 = (0.5)^4 = 0.0625$$ $$\sqrt{1 - 0.0625} = \sqrt{0.9375} = 0.9683$$ **Step 4: Apply discharge equation** $$Q = \frac{C_d \, A_2 \sqrt{2 g h_{\text{water}}}}{\sqrt{1 - (D_2/D_1)^4}}$$ $$\sqrt{2 g h} = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 6.3} = \sqrt{123.606} = 11.118 \text{ m/s}$$ $$Q = \frac{0.98 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 11.118}{0.9683}$$ $$Q = \frac{0.98 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 11.118}{0.9683}$$ Numerator: 0.98 × 7.854 × 10⁻³ × 11.118 = 0.08558 $$Q = \frac{0.08558}{0.9683} = 0.0884 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$ $$\boxed{Q = 0.0884 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \approx 88.4 \text{ L/s}}$$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Forgetting the velocity of approach factor** — Many students compute Q using only A₂√(2gh) without dividing by √(1 − (D₂/D₁)⁴). This typically costs **2 marks**. > 2. **Mixing up the manometer conversion** — When mercury over water is given, use h_water = h_Hg × (S_Hg/S_water − 1) = 12.6 × h_Hg, **not** simply 13.6 × h_Hg. The "minus 1" step is often missed. > 3. **Forgetting ρ when mass flow is asked** — ṁ = ρ × Q. A surprising number of students report Q as mass flow rate. > 4. **Writing C_d = 1** — For an orifice meter, always use C_d ≈ 0.62; for venturi, ≈ 0.98. Using C_d = 1 is a conceptual error. > 5. **Units mismatch** — Ensure ΔP is in Pa (not kPa) and D in metres when using the SI form of the equation. Always state final answer in both m³/s and L/s for clarity. > 6. **Not stating assumptions** — In derivations, examiners award marks for explicitly listing the assumptions (steady, incompressible, horizontal, etc.). --- ## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ All four head-type flowmeters operate on **Bernoulli's principle + continuity equation**. - ✅ The **universal discharge equation** is the same for all — only **C_d** and geometry differ. - ✅ **C_d (Orifice)** ≈ **0.60–0.65**; **C_d (Venturi/Nozzle)** ≈ **0.95–0.98**. - ✅ **Vena contracta** is the narrowest point of the jet downstream of an orifice — pressure is sensed here. - ✅ The **velocity of approach factor** K = 1/√(1 − (D₂/D₁)⁴) corrects for upstream kinetic energy. - ✅ Venturi has the **lowest permanent pressure loss** (10–15%) because of its divergent recovery cone. - ✅ Flow nozzle is preferred for **high-velocity steam**; venturi for **water mains**; orifice for **general process**; pitot tube for **point velocity** measurement. - ✅ For manometer with mercury over water: **h_water = h_Hg × (13.6 − 1) = 12.6 × h_Hg**. - ✅ Mass flow rate equation: **ṁ = C_d A₂ √(2 ρ ΔP) / √(1 − (D₂/D₁)⁴)**. - ✅ Pitot tube measures **stagnation − static = dynamic pressure**, giving **local velocity only**. - ✅ For accurate measurement, ensure **≥ 10D straight pipe length upstream** and **≥ 5D downstream** of the primary element. - ✅ Typical exam marks distribution: Part A (3 × 2 = 6 marks), Part B (14 marks with internal choice). - ✅ Memorize the **permanent pressure loss hierarchy**: Venturi < Flow Nozzle < Orifice.

topics.title: Open Channel Meters: Weirs and Flumes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT724 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Open Channel Meters: Weirs and Flumes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

An **Open Channel Flow Meter** is a primary flow measurement device used to determine the volumetric flow rate of liquids (most commonly water, wastewater, irrigation flow, and industrial effluents) flowing under gravity in a channel with a free liquid surface exposed to atmospheric pressure. Unlike closed-pipe flow meters, open channel flow is fundamentally characterized by a **variable hydraulic head** that must be measured and converted into flow rate using an established hydraulic relationship.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** Open channel flow measurement is the quantification of flow in conduits where the liquid surface is at atmospheric pressure and the driving force is gravity. The two principal classes of primary elements studied under the OECHT724 Module 3 syllabus are **Weirs** and **Flumes**.

### 1.2 Classification of Open Channel Meters

| Class | Physical Form | Governing Principle | Typical Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Weirs** | Overflow obstruction (plate / notch) | Head-over-crest measurement | Irrigation canals, small plant effluents |
| **Flumes** | Constricted channel section | Critical depth at throat | Sewage plants, large industrial drains |
| **Other** | Dilution, ultrasonic, float | Level $\to$ flow conversion | Smart water networks |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Picture:** Imagine a kitchen sink with the tap running. If you place a small **book (a weir)** partially across the drain, water will pile up behind the book and spill over its edge. The **higher the water rises behind the book, the more water is flowing in**. By simply measuring how tall that water column is, you can mathematically determine the flow rate. A **flume** works similarly, but instead of an obstruction, you create a **narrowing in the channel** (think of pinching a garden hose with your thumb) — the water speeds up and the surface drops at the throat, and that surface profile tells you the flow.

### 1.4 Key Terminology (KTU High-Yield Vocabulary)

- **Weir Plate** — A vertical obstruction with a precisely cut notch placed perpendicular to the flow direction.
- **Nappe** — The sheet of water that flows over the crest of the weir.
- **Crest / Sill** — The lowest edge of the weir notch over which water spills.
- **Head (H)** — The vertical distance from the crest to the upstream water surface, measured at a point sufficiently far upstream (typically **4H to 5H**) where the surface is undisturbed. Measured in **metres (m)**.
- **Pond / Approach Channel** — The still water body upstream of the weir.
- **Suppressed Weir** — A weir whose notch width equals the full width of the channel (no side contractions).
- **Contracted Weir** — A weir whose notch width is smaller than the channel width (end + side contractions occur).
- **Flume Throat** — The narrowest cross-section of a flume where flow becomes critical ($Fr = 1$).
- **Critical Flow** — The condition at which specific energy is minimum for a given discharge; flow is governed by gravity and inertial forces alone.
- **End contractions (n)** — The number of sides where the weir plate causes lateral contraction of the nappe (maximum $n = 2$).
- **Coefficient of Discharge ($C_d$)** — A dimensionless empirical factor accounting for real-fluid losses (viscous, turbulence, nappe shape) relative to ideal flow; typical range **0.61 – 0.66**.

> [!NOTE]
> **SI Unit Mandate:** KTU board answers must use $Q$ in $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$, $H$ in **metres**, $L$ in **metres**, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$, and $C_d$ as **dimensionless**. Always carry units through the calculation for full credit.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic of a Weir (Step-by-Step)

1. **Flow arrives** in the approach channel with some unknown discharge $Q$ and depth $y_1$.
2. **Water rises** behind the weir plate until it overtops the crest at elevation $H$ above the sill.
3. **The nappe springs clear** of the crest, behaving as a free-falling sheet of water.
4. **The flow is idealised** as a rectangular stream issuing from a sluice of height $H$, with velocity derived from Torricelli's principle at each elementary horizontal strip.
5. **Integration over the head** $H$ (from $0$ to $H$) yields the ideal volumetric discharge.
6. **Real-fluid losses** are lumped into a single empirical coefficient $C_d$ to obtain the working equation.

### 2.2 Operational Logic of a Flume (Step-by-Step)

1. **Flow enters** a converging section that smoothly reduces the channel width.
2. **The water surface drops** as velocity increases (continuity principle $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$).
3. **At the throat**, the cross-section is minimum; the Froude number reaches unity ($Fr = 1$) — this is **critical flow**.
4. **Because critical flow is uniquely related to upstream head**, the single measurement of head $H_a$ (or throat head $H_b$) is sufficient to determine $Q$ with high accuracy.
5. **Flow then exits** through a diverging section that recovers some head loss.

### 2.3 Why Critical Flow Makes Flumes Self-Cleaning

At $Fr = 1$, the flow velocity exceeds the wave-celeration speed $\sqrt{gy}$, so surface disturbances cannot propagate upstream. This means **downstream conditions cannot "back up" into the measurement** — a property called **modular flow**. So long as the downstream submergence ratio is below the manufacturer's limit, the flume reads correctly.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Weirs dominate low-flow, clean-water, low-cost installations (irrigation, lab calibration, small industrial inlets). Flumes dominate dirty-water, large-flow, self-cleaning applications (sewage treatment plants, mining slurries, stormwater). Both convert a **level measurement** (cheap: float, bubbler, ultrasonic, radar) into a **flow measurement** — a fundamental KTU concept.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Device Type | Discharge Equation | Validity / Range | Default $C_d$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Rectangular Weir (Suppressed)** | $Q = \dfrac{2}{3}\,C_d\,L\,\sqrt{2g}\,H^{3/2}$ | $H \le L$, $L \ge 3H$ | 0.62 |
| 2 | **Rectangular Weir (Contracted, Francis formula)** | $Q = \dfrac{2}{3}\,C_d\,(L - 0.2nH)\,\sqrt{2g}\,H^{3/2}$ | $n = 1$ or $2$ | 0.623 |
| 3 | **Triangular (V-Notch) Weir** | $Q = \dfrac{8}{15}\,C_d\,\tan\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right)\,\sqrt{2g}\,H^{5/2}$ | $\theta = 60^\circ$ standard | 0.58 |
| 4 | **Trapezoidal (Cipolletti) Weir** | $Q = \dfrac{2}{3}\,C_d\,L\,\sqrt{2g}\,H^{3/2}$ | Side slope 1:4 (horizontal : vertical) | 0.63 |
| 5 | **Broad-Crested Weir** | $Q = C\,L\,H^{3/2}$ | $C \approx 1.45$ in SI | empirical |
| 6 | **Parshall Flume (Free Flow)** | $Q = C\,H_a^{n}$ | $W = 0.15$ to $8.0$ m | empirical |
| 7 | **Parshall Flume (Standard, $W$ in ft, $H$ in ft)** | $Q = 4\,W\,H_a^{1.522\,W^{0.026}}$ | Empirical USBR formula | — |
| 8 | **H-Flume** | $Q = 1.04\,H^{2.32}$ ($H$ in m) | $D = 0.305$ m standard | empirical |
| 9 | **Palmer-Bowlus Flume** | $Q = f(D,H_b)$ | Sized to pipe diameter | empirical |
| 10 | **General Continuity** | $Q = A\,V$ | Always | — |
| 11 | **Froude Number** | $Fr = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{gD_h}}$ | $Fr = 1$ at flume throat | — |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe Symbol Trap:** All tables above use the LTeX vertical bar with a space `\,\vert\,` to avoid breaking the markdown table parser. In your written exam script, always use the standard modulus $\vert \cdot \vert$ or $\mid \cdot \mid$ form.

### 2.5 The "Why" Behind the Head Exponents

- **$H^{3/2}$ exponents** (rectangular, trapezoidal, broad-crested): arise from integrating a Torricelli velocity profile $v = \sqrt{2gh}$ over a horizontal strip of width $L$ and thickness $dh$, from $0$ to $H$. Mathematically, $\int_0^H L\sqrt{2gh}\,dh \propto H^{3/2}$.
- **$H^{5/2}$ exponent** (V-notch): arises because the strip width at depth $h$ below the vertex is $2h\tan(\theta/2)$, giving $\int_0^H 2h\tan(\theta/2)\sqrt{2gh}\,dh \propto H^{5/2}$.
- **Empirical exponents** (flumes): derived from dimensional analysis and field calibration because the geometry is too complex for closed-form integration.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Discharge Over a Rectangular Weir (Suppressed)

**Step 1 — Consider an elementary horizontal strip** at depth $h$ below the free surface, having thickness $dh$. The width of the strip equals the notch length $L$.

**Step 2 — Area of the strip:**

$$
dA = L\,dh
$$

**Step 3 — Velocity through the strip (Torricelli's theorem):** The ideal velocity of water leaving the strip equals the velocity attained by a body falling freely through height $h$:

$$
v = \sqrt{2gh}
$$

**Step 4 — Ideal elemental discharge** through the strip:

$$
dQ_{ideal} = v\,dA = L\,\sqrt{2g\,h}\,dh
$$

**Step 5 — Integrate** from $h = 0$ (crest level) to $h = H$ (free surface):

$$
Q_{ideal} = \int_{0}^{H} L\,\sqrt{2g\,h}\,dh = L\,\sqrt{2g}\int_{0}^{H} h^{1/2}\,dh
$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate the integral** $\int_0^H h^{1/2} dh = \left[\dfrac{2}{3}h^{3/2}\right]_0^H = \dfrac{2}{3}H^{3/2}$:

$$
Q_{ideal} = L\,\sqrt{2g}\cdot \dfrac{2}{3}H^{3/2} = \dfrac{2}{3}\,L\,\sqrt{2g}\,H^{3/2}
$$

**Step 7 — Introduce the Coefficient of Discharge $C_d$** to account for real-fluid losses (nappe contraction, viscosity, turbulence, velocity-of-approach):

$$
\boxed{\,Q = \dfrac{2}{3}\,C_d\,L\,\sqrt{2g}\,H^{3/2}\,}
$$

> This is the **Rehbock / standard KTU equation** for a rectangular suppressed weir. It is also valid for a Cipolletti (trapezoidal with 1:4 side slopes) weir, because the small triangular extensions at the sides exactly compensate for end contractions.

### 3.2 Derivation: Discharge Over a Triangular (V-Notch) Weir

**Step 1 — Geometry:** The notch is a sharp-edged isoceles triangle with apex at the bottom, total vertex angle $\theta$, and water head $H$ measured from the vertex to the free surface.

**Step 2 — Width of an elementary strip** at depth $h$ below the vertex: by similar triangles, the half-width is $h\tan(\theta/2)$, so the full width is:

$$
b(h) = 2h\,\tan\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Elemental area:**

$$
dA = b(h)\,dh = 2h\,\tan\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right)dh
$$

**Step 4 — Torricelli velocity** at the strip:

$$
v = \sqrt{2gh}
$$

**Step 5 — Elemental ideal discharge:**

$$
dQ = v\,dA = 2\,\tan\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right)\sqrt{2g}\,h^{3/2}\,dh
$$

**Step 6 — Integrate from $h=0$ to $h=H$:**

$$
Q_{ideal} = 2\,\tan\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right)\sqrt{2g}\int_{0}^{H} h^{3/2}\,dh
$$

**Step 7 — Evaluate the integral** $\int_0^H h^{3/2}dh = \left[\dfrac{2}{5}h^{5/2}\right]_0^H = \dfrac{2}{5}H^{5/2}$:

$$
Q_{ideal} = 2\,\tan\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right)\sqrt{2g}\cdot \dfrac{2}{5}H^{5/2} = \dfrac{4}{5}\,\tan\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right)\sqrt{2g}\,H^{5/2}
$$

**Step 8 — Introduce the Coefficient of Discharge $C_d$:**

$$
\boxed{\,Q = \dfrac{8}{15}\,C_d\,\tan\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right)\sqrt{2g}\,H^{5/2}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the V-notch is preferred for small flows:** The exponent $5/2 = 2.5$ means that at low heads $H$, the discharge $Q$ is much smaller than that of a rectangular weir (exponent $3/2 = 1.5$). This gives a V-notch **far higher sensitivity at low flow rates**, making it the standard KTU device for laboratory and small-flow installations.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem (Analytical Matrix Style)

**Problem:** A suppressed rectangular weir of crest length $L = 0.50 \text{ m}$ has a measured head $H = 0.20 \text{ m}$. Take $C_d = 0.62$ and $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$. Compute the discharge $Q$.

**Solution — Row-by-Row Analytical Matrix:**

| Step | Quantity | Expression | Numerical Value | Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Head $H$ | given | 0.20 | m |
| 2 | $H^{3/2}$ | $0.20^{1.5}$ | 0.0894 | m$^{1.5}$ |
| 3 | $\sqrt{2g}$ | $\sqrt{2 \times 9.81}$ | 4.429 | m$^{0.5}$/s |
| 4 | Coefficient factor | $\dfrac{2}{3}\times 0.62$ | 0.4133 | — |
| 5 | $Q$ | $0.4133 \times 0.50 \times 4.429 \times 0.0894$ | 0.0818 | m$^{3}$/s |
| 6 | $Q$ in L/s | $0.0818 \times 1000$ | 81.8 | L/s |

**Substitution-block (for the board script):**

$$
Q = \frac{2}{3} \times 0.62 \times 0.50 \times \sqrt{2 \times 9.81} \times (0.20)^{3/2}
$$

$$
Q = 0.4133 \times 0.50 \times 4.429 \times 0.0894
$$

$$
\boxed{\,Q \approx 0.0818 \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s} \approx 81.8 \text{ L/s}\,}
$$

### 3.4 Parshall Flume — Conceptual Derivation

Unlike weirs, the Parshall flume has **no closed-form discharge equation** because its cross-section is three-dimensional and the converging section is geometrically complex. The discharge law is obtained empirically:

$$
Q = C \cdot H_a^{n}
$$

where $C$ and $n$ are tabulated constants that depend on the throat width $W$. Typical values:

| Throat Width $W$ (m) | $C$ | $n$ | Free-flow range $Q$ (L/s) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 0.152 (6 in) | 0.381 | 1.580 | 0.30 – 5.7 |
| 0.305 (1 ft) | 0.690 | 1.520 | 1.5 – 110 |
| 0.610 (2 ft) | 1.426 | 1.540 | 4.0 – 460 |
| 1.220 (4 ft) | 2.965 | 1.566 | 17 – 1620 |
| 2.440 (8 ft) | 6.250 | 1.578 | 73 – 5560 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** For Parshall flume problems, the examiner will always give you the values of $C$ and $n$ (or $W$ and a table) along with $H_a$. You are expected to substitute into $Q = C\,H_a^n$ and report the answer in $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Classification of Open Channel Flow Meters (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[OPEN CHANNEL FLOW METERS] --> B[WEIRS]
    A --> C[FLUMES]
    A --> D[OTHER METHODS]
    B --> B1[SHARP CRESTED]
    B --> B2[BROAD CRESTED]
    B1 --> B1A[RECTANGULAR SUPPRESSED]
    B1 --> B1B[RECTANGULAR CONTRACTED]
    B1 --> B1C[TRIANGULAR V NOTCH]
    B1 --> B1D[TRAPEZOIDAL CIPOLLETTI]
    C --> C1[PARSHALL FLUME]
    C --> C2[VENTURI FLUME]
    C --> C3[PALMER BOWLUS FLUME]
    C --> C4[H FLUME]
    D --> D1[ULTRASONIC LEVEL TO FLOW]
    D --> D2[BUBBLER MANOMETER]
    D --> D3[DILUTION TRACER METHOD]
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture of a Weir Measurement System

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[APPROACH CHANNEL] --> B[STILLING WELL]
    B --> C[STATIC HEAD H]
    C --> D[LEVEL SENSOR]
    D --> D1[FLOAT TYPE]
    D --> D2[BUBBLER TYPE]
    D --> D3[ULTRASONIC TYPE]
    D --> E[TRANSMITTER]
    E --> F[WEIR FORMULA H TO Q]
    F --> G[DISPLAY Q IN M3 PER S]
    F --> H[DATA LOGGER]
    H --> I[SCADA / PLC]
    A --> J[WEIR PLATE WITH NOTCH]
    J --> K[CREST EDGE]
    K --> L[VENTED NAPPE]
    L --> M[DOWNSTREAM TAILWATER]
```

### 4.3 Parshall Flume Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[INFLOW Q UNKNOWN] --> B[CONVERGING SECTION]
    B --> C[THROAT W]
    C --> D[DIVERGING SECTION]
    D --> E[OUTFLOW Q MEASURED]
    B --> BX[MEASUREMENT POINT HA]
    C --> CX[MEASUREMENT POINT HB]
    BX --> P[STILLING WELL HA]
    CX --> Q2[STILLING WELL HB]
    P --> R[LEVEL SENSOR]
    Q2 --> R
    R --> S[TRANSMITTER]
    S --> T[EMPIRICAL FORMULA Q EQUALS C HA POWER N]
    T --> U[DISPLAYED FLOW RATE]
    T --> V[ALARM ON HIGH OR LOW Q]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Notes:** All node identifiers above begin with a letter prefix (`A`, `B1`, `B1A`, `B2`, `C1`, `D1`, `BX`, `CX`, `P`, `Q2`, `R`, etc.) followed by alphanumeric characters only. No reserved keywords such as `end` or `subgraph` are used as bare node names. All node labels contain only raw uppercase alphanumeric text and slash separators — no bold, italics, or HTML tables inside the quoted labels.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer / Definition)**

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**
> Define a weir. With a neat sketch, distinguish between a **suppressed** and a **contracted** rectangular weir. Mention the standard head-measurement location.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **weir** is a sharp-edged overflow obstruction placed across an open channel to measure flow rate by relating the upstream head to the discharge. (1 mark)

| Feature | Suppressed Weir | Contracted Weir |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Notch width $L$ | Equals channel width | Less than channel width |
| Side contractions | None | End + side $(n=2)$ or end-only $(n=1)$ |
| Discharge eqn. | $Q = \dfrac{2}{3}C_d L \sqrt{2g}\,H^{3/2}$ | $Q = \dfrac{2}{3}C_d (L-0.2nH)\sqrt{2g}\,H^{3/2}$ |

The head $H$ must be measured at a distance of **$4H$ to $5H$** upstream of the weir, in a stilling well. (1 mark)

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> State the **advantages of a V-notch weir** over a rectangular weir. Why is it preferred for low-flow measurement?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Higher sensitivity at low heads** — the discharge varies as $H^{5/2}$ instead of $H^{3/2}$, so small changes in $H$ produce larger relative changes in $Q$ (1 mark).
2. **Better accuracy at small discharges** because the small triangular opening admits a measurable head where a rectangular weir would give a near-zero reading. (1 mark)
3. **Self-venting nappe** — the converging shape minimises the risk of the nappe clinging to the plate. (1 mark)

---

### **PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Weir Analysis (Full Derivation + Numerical)**

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, KTU Model Paper Set A]**
>
> **(a)** Derive an expression for the discharge over a **suppressed rectangular weir**, clearly stating the assumptions. Mention the role of the coefficient of discharge $C_d$. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A suppressed rectangular weir, $L = 0.90$ m, has a head $H = 0.15$ m. Using $C_d = 0.62$ and $g = 9.81$ m/s$^2$, determine the discharge. If the same head is measured on a $60^\circ$ V-notch weir with $C_d = 0.58$, find the discharge. Compare the two results. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

1. Consider a thin horizontal strip at depth $h$ below the free surface with thickness $dh$. (1 mark)
2. Area of strip $dA = L\,dh$. (1 mark)
3. Velocity by Torricelli $v = \sqrt{2gh}$. (1 mark)
4. Elemental discharge $dQ = L\sqrt{2gh}\,dh$. (1 mark)
5. Integrate from $0$ to $H$:

$$
Q_{ideal} = L\sqrt{2g}\int_0^H h^{1/2}dh = L\sqrt{2g}\cdot \frac{2}{3}H^{3/2}
$$

(1 mark)

6. **Assumptions:** (i) steady flow, (ii) hydrostatic pressure distribution at the strip, (iii) nappe is fully ventilated, (iv) velocity of approach is negligible, (v) $H \ge 5$ cm to avoid viscous surface-tension effects. (1 mark)
7. **Role of $C_d$:** corrects for the difference between idealised and real flow due to nappe contraction, viscosity, turbulence, and the velocity-of-approach head. Empirical values: $0.61$–$0.66$ for rectangular weirs. Final equation:

$$
Q = \frac{2}{3}C_d L \sqrt{2g}\,H^{3/2}
$$

(1 mark)

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**Rectangular weir:**

$$
Q_{rect} = \frac{2}{3}\times 0.62 \times 0.90 \times \sqrt{2\times 9.81}\times (0.15)^{1.5}
$$

$$
Q_{rect} = 0.4133 \times 0.90 \times 4.429 \times 0.05809 = 0.0956 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

(3 marks)

**V-notch weir:**

For $\theta = 60^\circ$, $\tan(\theta/2) = \tan 30^\circ = 0.5774$.

$$
Q_{V} = \frac{8}{15}\times 0.58 \times 0.5774 \times \sqrt{2\times 9.81}\times (0.15)^{2.5}
$$

$$
Q_{V} = 0.3093 \times 0.5774 \times 4.429 \times 0.00872 = 0.00690 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

(3 marks)

**Comparison:** $Q_{rect}/Q_{V} \approx 13.86$ — the rectangular weir delivers nearly **14 times** more flow for the same head, confirming that the V-notch is the better choice for small discharges. (1 mark)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Flume Analysis (Parshall)**

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary Exam]**
>
> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **Parshall flume** and explain the principle of critical flow. State why the flume is considered self-cleaning. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A Parshall flume with throat width $W = 0.305$ m has the calibration constants $C = 0.690$ and $n = 1.520$. If the upstream head $H_a = 0.30$ m, calculate the free-flow discharge. Also compute the percentage error in discharge if the head is overestimated by 5%. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Description (7 marks):**

1. **Definition (1 mark):** A Parshall flume is a calibrated open-channel constriction consisting of (i) an **inlet converging section**, (ii) a **throat of fixed width $W$** with a parallel-walled length, and (iii) a **downstream diverging section**.
2. **Components (2 marks):** The level $H_a$ is measured in the inlet section at a standard distance upstream of the throat; $H_b$ is measured in the throat itself. The flume is fabricated from fibreglass, concrete, or metal.
3. **Critical-flow principle (2 marks):** As the channel contracts, the velocity rises. At the throat, the **Froude number $Fr = V/\sqrt{gD_h} = 1$**. At $Fr=1$, specific energy is minimum and the flow becomes a function of upstream head alone — making the discharge directly readable from $H_a$.
4. **Self-cleaning property (1 mark):** Because $Fr \ge 1$ at the throat, surface waves cannot propagate upstream, so debris is flushed through and settled solids are re-suspended; no routine de-silting is required.
5. **Free-flow vs submerged-flow regime (1 mark):** So long as the ratio $H_b/H_a$ stays below a tabulated modular limit, the flume operates in the free-flow regime where $Q = C\,H_a^n$.

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**Discharge computation (3 marks):**

$$
Q = C \cdot H_a^{n} = 0.690 \times (0.30)^{1.520}
$$

$$
(0.30)^{1.520} = e^{1.520 \ln 0.30} = e^{1.520 \times (-1.2040)} = e^{-1.830} = 0.1604
$$

$$
Q = 0.690 \times 0.1604 = 0.1107 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 110.7 \text{ L/s}
$$

**Percentage-error analysis (4 marks):** If $H_a$ is overestimated by 5%, the new head is $H_a' = 1.05 \times 0.30 = 0.315$ m.

$$
Q' = 0.690 \times (0.315)^{1.520}
$$

$$
(0.315)^{1.520} = e^{1.520 \times \ln 0.315} = e^{1.520 \times (-1.1553)} = e^{-1.756} = 0.1727
$$

$$
Q' = 0.690 \times 0.1727 = 0.1192 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Percentage error:**

$$
\%\text{error} = \frac{Q' - Q}{Q}\times 100 = \frac{0.1192 - 0.1107}{0.1107}\times 100 \approx 7.65\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:**
>
> 1. **Wrong exponent bracket:** Many students write $H^{1.52}$ when they should write $H^{1.520}$ — these are not the same. Always preserve at least **3 significant digits** in the exponent as given in the table. (–0.5 mark)
> 2. **Forgetting the modular-limit clause:** If the question states "free-flow", you must use $Q = C H_a^n$. If it states "submerged flow", use the submerged-flow correction table — these are different marks! (–1 to –2 marks)
> 3. **Mixing up Francis-formula constants:** The 0.2 in $(L - 0.2nH)$ is **per end contraction**, not per metre of crest. With $n=2$, the full correction is $0.4H$. (–1 mark)
> 4. **Unit mismatch in V-notch:** The factor $8/15$ comes from $2 \times 2/5 = 4/5$ divided by 1.5 — students often write $4/5$ and lose the $C_d$ step. (–0.5 mark)
> 5. **Head measurement location:** KTU expects you to state "head measured at $4H$ to $5H$ upstream" in **every** weir question. Omitting it costs 0.5 mark. (–0.5 mark)
> 6. **Velocity of approach neglected without comment:** If the approach velocity exceeds ~0.5 m/s, the simple equation over-predicts. Always add a one-line comment in a 14-mark answer. (–0.5 mark)

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)**

- **Weir equation (rectangular, suppressed):** $Q = \dfrac{2}{3}C_d L \sqrt{2g}\,H^{3/2}$ — head exponent **3/2**. (1)
- **Weir equation (rectangular, contracted):** $Q = \dfrac{2}{3}C_d (L - 0.2nH)\sqrt{2g}\,H^{3/2}$ — Francis correction **$0.2nH$**. (2)
- **V-notch (triangular) weir:** $Q = \dfrac{8}{15}C_d \tan(\theta/2) \sqrt{2g}\,H^{5/2}$ — head exponent **5/2**. (3)
- **Cipolletti trapezoidal weir:** uses the suppressed rectangular formula; the 1:4 side slopes cancel end-contraction losses. (4)
- **Broad-crested weir:** $Q \approx 1.45\,L\,H^{3/2}$ (SI) — used for very large discharges. (5)
- **Parshall flume (free flow):** $Q = C\,H_a^{n}$ — empirical, $C$ and $n$ from table; throat width $W$ from 0.15 m to 8.0 m. (6)
- **H-Flume:** $Q = 1.04\,H^{2.32}$ (SI) — single equation, no throat width dependence. (7)
- **Head measurement location for weirs:** **$4H$ to $5H$** upstream, in a stilling well to dampen surface waves. (8)
- **Critical flow at flume throat:** $Fr = 1$ — flow depends only on $H_a$, hence self-cleaning and modular. (9)
- **Default $C_d$ values:** Rectangular 0.62, V-notch 0.58, Cipolletti 0.63, Broad-crested 0.55–0.58 in $C$. (10)
- **V-notch preferred when:** $Q < 30$ L/s, low-head conditions, high accuracy needed. (11)
- **Rectangular weir preferred when:** $Q > 30$ L/s, simple installation, low cost. (12)
- **Flume preferred when:** dirty water, sewage, slurries, large discharges, no head loss tolerance. (13)
- **Submergence limit (Parshall):** $H_b/H_a \le 0.6$ (small $W$) to $0.95$ (large $W$) — beyond this, free-flow formula fails. (14)
- **SI units always:** $Q$ in m³/s, $H$ in m, $L$ in m, $g = 9.81$ m/s², $C_d$ dimensionless. (15)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **End of Module 3 — Open Channel Meters: Weirs and Flumes.** Students preparing for KTU ESE should practise at least three full numerical problems on the rectangular suppressed weir, one on the V-notch, and one on the Parshall flume using the empirical formula. Focus on **unit consistency** and **empirical-table awareness** — these are the two highest-value habits for scoring above 80% in OECHT724.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electromagnetic Flowmeters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECHT724 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Electromagnetic Flowmeters ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) An **Electromagnetic Flowmeter** (also known as a **Magmeter** or **Electromagnetic Induction Flowmeter**) is a volumetric flow measurement device that operates on **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**. It measures the flow rate of a conductive liquid by detecting the **electromotive force (EMF)** induced in the fluid as it flows through a magnetic field perpendicular to the direction of flow. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** > An electromagnetic flowmeter is a transducer that converts the velocity of a conductive fluid into a measurable electrical signal (voltage/EMF) using a transverse magnetic field. The output EMF is directly proportional to the average flow velocity, making the device inherently **linear** in its response. **Working Principle Statement (Board-Standard):** > When a conductive fluid flows through a pipe of diameter $D$ placed in a transverse magnetic field of flux density $B$, an EMF is induced across the fluid in a direction mutually perpendicular to both the velocity vector and the magnetic field vector, given by $E = B \cdot v \cdot D$, where $v$ is the mean velocity of the fluid. --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview Imagine a **river flowing between two banks**. Now place two metal rods (electrodes) on opposite banks, and somehow set up a magnetic field that goes **across** the river (from sky to riverbed). The flowing river water acts like a moving conductor cutting through the magnetic field lines.
