# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
> **Q1.** Differentiate between a **variable head meter** and a **variable area meter** with one example each.

**Model Answer:**
A variable head meter (e.g., orifice plate) maintains a **constant flow area** and the **pressure drop varies** in proportion to flow. A variable area meter (e.g., rotameter) maintains an **almost constant pressure drop** while the **flow area varies** with flow rate, indicated by float position. In a variable head meter, flow is calculated from $\Delta P$; in a VAM, flow is read directly from float height.

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Q2.** Why must the float density in a rotameter be **greater than the fluid density**? What happens if they are equal?

**Model Answer:**
For the float to be lifted by the fluid drag, the net downward force (weight minus buoyancy) must be positive. That requires $\rho_f > \rho$. If $\rho_f = \rho$, the float becomes **neutrally buoyant** — it cannot be held at any specific height and either floats to the top indefinitely or wanders, making flow measurement impossible. If $\rho_f < \rho$, the float simply rises and is pushed out of the meter.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks (Full)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper]**
> **(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **construction and working of a Rotameter**. Derive the **mathematical expression** for volumetric flow rate. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A rotameter is calibrated for water at $20^\circ\text{C}$ using a stainless-steel float of volume $6 \,\text{cm}^3$ and area $1.2 \,\text{cm}^2$. The float density is $7800 \,\text{kg/m}^3$, $C_d = 0.96$. At a flow of $60 \,\text{L/h}$, find the **annular area** $A_2$. Take $\rho_{water} = 998 \,\text{kg/m}^3$. If the same rotameter is now used for an oil of density $850 \,\text{kg/m}^3$, what **flow rate of oil** corresponds to the same float height? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to A(a)

**Construction (4 marks for description + sketch):**
A rotameter consists of a **vertically mounted tapered tube** (typically a precision-bored borosilicate glass tube for visibility, or metal for high pressure). The tube has a small diameter at the bottom that gradually increases upward. Inside floats a **float** whose shape may be spherical, cylindrical, or plumb-bob type. As flow rises, the float moves up to a wider section where the **annular area between float and tube wall** is large enough to pass the flow with the same pressure drop. The tube is fitted with a **calibrated scale** etched on its surface (or on a backing plate) reading directly in flow units.

**Working (3 marks for derivation):**
Refer to the **full derivation in Section 3.1** above. The four forces (weight, buoyancy, drag, pressure force) balance. The final result is:

$$Q = C_d A_2 \sqrt{\frac{2 g V_f (\rho_f - \rho)}{\rho A_f \left[1 - (A_f/A_1)^2\right]}}$$

#### Model Solution to A(b)

**Step 1:** Convert all quantities to SI.

$$\begin{aligned}
V_f &= 6 \,\text{cm}^3 = 6 \times 10^{-6} \,\text{m}^3 \\
A_f &= 1.2 \,\text{cm}^2 = 1.2 \times 10^{-4} \,\text{m}^2 \\
\rho_f &= 7800 \,\text{kg/m}^3, \quad \rho_{water} = 998 \,\text{kg/m}^3 \\
C_d &= 0.96, \quad Q = 60 \,\text{L/h} = \dfrac{60 \times 10^{-3}}{3600} = 1.667 \times 10^{-5} \,\text{m}^3/\text{s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** Solve the simplified rotameter equation for $A_2$ (neglect the small kinetic correction).

$$\begin{aligned}
A_2 &= \frac{Q}{C_d \sqrt{2 g V_f (\rho_f - \rho)/(\rho A_f)}} \\
&= \frac{1.667 \times 10^{-5}}{0.96 \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 6 \times 10^{-6} \times 6802 / (998 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-4})}}
\end{aligned}$$

Compute the radical:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Radical numerator} &= 2 \times 9.81 \times 6 \times 10^{-6} \times 6802 \\
&= 8.009 \times 10^{-1} \\
&= 0.8009 \\
\text{Radical denominator} &= 998 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-4} = 0.11976 \\
\text{Radical content} &= 0.8009 / 0.11976 = 6.688 \\
\sqrt{6.688} &= 2.586 \,\text{m/s}
\end{aligned}$$

So:

$$A_2 = \frac{1.667 \times 10^{-5}}{0.96 \times 2.586} = \frac{1.667 \times 10^{-5}}{2.483} = 6.71 \times 10^{-6} \,\text{m}^2$$

Converting to $\text{cm}^2$:

$$\boxed{\;A_2 \approx 0.0671 \,\text{cm}^2\;}$$

**Step 3:** Apply fluid-change correction.

For the same float height (so $A_2$ is unchanged), the ratio of flow rates for two fluids is:

$$\frac{Q_{oil}}{Q_{water}} = \sqrt{\frac{(\rho_f - \rho_{oil})}{(\rho_f - \rho_{water})} \cdot \frac{\rho_{water}}{\rho_{oil}}}$$

Compute density differences:

$$\rho_f - \rho_{oil} = 7800 - 850 = 6950 \,\text{kg/m}^3$$
$$\rho_f - \rho_{water} = 7800 - 998 = 6802 \,\text{kg/m}^3$$

Compute the ratio:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{Q_{oil}}{Q_{water}} &= \sqrt{\frac{6950}{6802} \times \frac{998}{850}} \\
&= \sqrt{1.0218 \times 1.1741} \\
&= \sqrt{1.1996} \\
&= 1.0953
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore:

$$Q_{oil} = 1.0953 \times 1.667 \times 10^{-5} = 1.826 \times 10^{-5} \,\text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

Converting back:

$$\boxed{\;Q_{oil} \approx 65.7 \,\text{L/h}\;}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. Many students **forget the kinetic-energy correction** $(A_f/A_1)^2$ but then incorrectly include it in the final answer. If the question says "neglect the correction", honour that.
> 2. **Unit conversion errors** between $\text{cm}^3/\text{s}$, $\text{L/h}$, and $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ account for ~30% of lost marks. Always convert to **SI** first.
> 3. In fluid-change problems, students often confuse the ratio formula and write the **square root over the entire expression** correctly but misplace the densities. Write out the ratio on paper before plugging values.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
> **(a)** Explain the **construction and working of a Cone-and-Float type variable area meter**. Compare its performance with a conventional **glass-tube rotameter**. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A rotameter has a float of volume $8 \,\text{cm}^3$ and area $1.5 \,\text{cm}^2$ in a tube where the annular area at the float position is $4 \,\text{cm}^2$. The float density is $7900 \,\text{kg/m}^3$, fluid (oil) density is $880 \,\text{kg/m}^3$, and $C_d = 0.92$. Determine the **(i)** pressure drop across the float, **(ii)** volumetric flow rate, and **(iii)** mass flow rate. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to B(a)

**Construction (3 marks):**
A cone-and-float (or piston-type) meter consists of a **vertically mounted cylindrical metal body** containing a **fixed sharp-edged orifice ring** at the inlet. A **cone-shaped float** rests on the orifice and is connected to a **guide rod** that keeps it aligned. As flow increases, the cone lifts against the combined effect of its weight and the fluid drag, exposing a larger **annular area** between cone and orifice. The cone position is sensed mechanically via a lever arm or **magnetically** (in magnetic types) and converted to a flow indication or 4–20 mA signal.

**Working (2 marks):**
The fluid enters below the orifice, exerts pressure on the cone base, and lifts it. The cone seeks the height where **drag force + buoyancy = weight**. At equilibrium, the annular opening is fixed, and the pressure drop is essentially constant. Float height ∝ flow.

**Comparison with Glass-Tube Rotameter (2 marks):**

| Feature | Glass Rotameter | Cone-and-Float Meter |
|---|---|---|
| Pressure rating | Low (typically < 7 bar) | High (up to 100+ bar) |
| Fluid clarity | Must be transparent | Any fluid (opaque OK) |
| Corrosion resistance | Limited (glass) | Excellent (all metal) |
| Remote indication | Difficult | Easy (magnetic float + transmitter) |
| Cost | Low | Higher |
| Accuracy | ±2% FS typical | ±1% FS typical |
| Maintenance | Simple | Requires guide-rod cleaning |

#### Model Solution to B(b)

**Step 1:** Convert all quantities to SI.

$$\begin{aligned}
V_f &= 8 \,\text{cm}^3 = 8 \times 10^{-6} \,\text{m}^3 \\
A_f &= 1.5 \,\text{cm}^2 = 1.5 \times 10^{-4} \,\text{m}^2 \\
A_2 &= 4 \,\text{cm}^2 = 4 \times 10^{-4} \,\text{m}^2 \\
\rho_f &= 7900 \,\text{kg/m}^3, \quad \rho = 880 \,\text{kg/m}^3 \\
C_d &= 0.92, \quad g = 9.81 \,\text{m/s}^2
\end{aligned}$$

**(i) Pressure drop across the float:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta P &= \frac{V_f \cdot g \cdot (\rho_f - \rho)}{A_f} \\
&= \frac{8 \times 10^{-6} \times 9.81 \times (7900 - 880)}{1.5 \times 10^{-4}} \\
&= \frac{8 \times 10^{-6} \times 9.81 \times 7020}{1.5 \times 10^{-4}}
\end{aligned}$$

Numerator:

$$8 \times 10^{-6} \times 9.81 = 7.848 \times 10^{-5}$$
$$7.848 \times 10^{-5} \times 7020 = 0.5509$$

Denominator: $1.5 \times 10^{-4}$.

$$\Delta P = \frac{0.5509}{1.5 \times 10^{-4}} = 3672.7 \,\text{Pa} \approx 3.67 \,\text{kPa}$$

$$\boxed{\;\Delta P \approx 3.67 \,\text{kPa}\;}$$

**(ii) Volumetric flow rate:**

$$Q = C_d \cdot A_2 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\sqrt{\frac{2 \times 3672.7}{880}} &= \sqrt{\frac{7345.4}{880}} = \sqrt{8.347} = 2.889 \,\text{m/s} \\
Q &= 0.92 \times 4 \times 10^{-4} \times 2.889 \\
&= 3.68 \times 10^{-4} \times 2.889 \\
&= 1.063 \times 10^{-3} \,\text{m}^3/\text{s}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\;Q \approx 1.063 \times 10^{-3} \,\text{m}^3/\text{s} \approx 1.06 \,\text{L/s}\;}$$

**(iii) Mass flow rate:**

$$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q = 880 \times 1.063 \times 10^{-3} = 0.935 \,\text{kg/s}$$

$$\boxed{\;\dot{m} \approx 0.935 \,\text{kg/s}\;}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning (Q B):**
> 1. In part (i), students often write $V_f \rho_f g - V_f \rho g$ as $V_f g$ — **don't skip the density difference**. Mark deduction is **2/7** for this error.
> 2. Part (ii) requires recognizing that $\Delta P$ from (i) is the input to Bernoulli — many students mistakenly recompute $\Delta P$ instead of using the value found in (i). This is a **continuity-of-steps** error worth **1 mark**.
> 3. Part (iii) tests whether the student knows **mass flow = density × volumetric flow**. Marks lost: **1 mark** if confused with weight flow.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Rapid-Revision Checklist for Variable Area Meters:**

- ✅ VAM = **constant $\Delta P$, variable area**. Opposite of orifice/venturi.
- ✅ Rotameter = float in **tapered tube**; cone-and-float = cone on **fixed orifice**.
- ✅ Float density **must exceed fluid density**, else it floats away.
- ✅ Master equation (full):
  $Q = C_d A_2 \sqrt{2 g V_f (\rho_f - \rho) / (\rho A_f [1 - (A_f/A_1)^2])}$
- ✅ Simplified form (when $A_f \ll A_1$): drop the bracketed correction.
- ✅ $C_d \in [0.60, 0.98]$ — float-shape and Reynolds-number dependent.
- ✅ Force balance: $W = F_b + F_d$ → $F_d = V_f g (\rho_f - \rho)$.
- ✅ Pressure drop across float: $\Delta P = V_f g (\rho_f - \rho) / A_f$.
- ✅ **Glass rotameter** = low-pressure, transparent fluids, direct visual readout.
- ✅ **Cone-and-float / piston meter** = high-pressure, dirty fluids, magnetic coupling for remote reading.
- ✅ **Recalibration ratio** for fluid change:
  $Q_1/Q_2 = \sqrt{[(\rho_f - \rho_2)/(\rho_f - \rho_1)] \cdot (\rho_1/\rho_2)}$
- ✅ **Rangeability** (turndown) of rotameter ≈ **10:1** — wider than most DP meters.
- ✅ Accuracy typically **±2% of full scale** for glass rotameters, **±1% FS** for metal-tube / cone types.
- ✅ Must be installed **vertically** with upward flow — orientation is critical.
- ✅ Standard accessories: **needle valve** (for flow control at inlet), **integrated transmitter** (electronic output).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mechanical Flowmeters of Positive Displacement Type: Rotating Disk, Turbine Type, and Anemometers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT724 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Mechanical Flowmeters of Positive Displacement Type: Rotating Disk, Turbine Type, and Anemometers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Mechanical Flowmeters — Broad Classification

Mechanical flowmeters are flow-measuring instruments that rely on a **moving mechanical element** placed in the flow stream. The motion of this element (rotation, displacement, or nutation) is mechanically or electrically coupled to an output indicator, allowing the volumetric or mass flow rate to be inferred.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *A **Positive Displacement (PD) Flowmeter** is a flow-measuring device that operates by repeatedly entrapping and discharging discrete, fixed volumes of the process fluid using precisely machined mechanical chambers. The total volume passed is obtained by counting the number of discrete filling-discharging cycles.*

The three devices covered in this module — **Rotating (Nutating) Disk**, **Turbine Flowmeter**, and **Anemometer** — belong to the broader family of *velocity-and-displacement-based* mechanical flowmeters.

| Sub-class | Underlying Principle | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| Rotating / Nutating Disk | Positive displacement (fixed-volume chambers) | Domestic water meters, fuel dispensers |
| Turbine Flowmeter | Velocity-proportional rotor rotation | Custody transfer of petroleum, cryogenic LNG |
| Anemometer (Cup / Vane) | Velocity-proportional angular speed | Meteorology, HVAC, wind tunnels |

---

## 1.2 Rotating (Nutating) Disk Flowmeter — Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> *A nutating-disk flowmeter is a positive-displacement type flowmeter in which a circular disk, mounted on a central spherical ball, undergoes a **nutating (wobbling) motion** caused by fluid pressure acting on the disk surface. Each complete nutation cycle displaces a precisely known volume of fluid, and a gear train coupled to the disk shaft drives a mechanical register to display the totalised flow.*

### Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

Imagine a coin spinning on a flat table — but instead of spinning flat, it **wobbles** like a spinning top losing balance. The coin traces a small circular path on the table while its rim touches every point on a circular boundary. Each wobble creates a tiny enclosed space inside.

Now scale this image up: the "coin" is a precision-machined metal disk, the "table" is a measuring chamber, and a stream of water pushes it sideways as it enters. Every time the disk completes one wobble, a **fixed pocket of water** is locked and pushed out the other side. A counter attached to the disk shaft simply **counts the wobbles** to compute total volume.

This is *exactly* how the **domestic water meter** mounted on your house wall operates — the small rotating star-wheel you see is the wobble-counting mechanism.

**Working Fluid Restriction:** Because the disk physically contacts and partitions the fluid, only **clean, non-corrosive, single-phase liquids** (water, refined fuels, lubricating oils) are suitable.

---

## 1.3 Turbine Flowmeter — Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> *A turbine flowmeter consists of a multi-bladed rotor suspended axially inside a pipe on low-friction bearings. The flowing fluid impinges on the rotor blades and imparts a tangential force, causing the rotor to spin at an angular velocity proportional to the local fluid velocity. The rotational speed is detected by a non-contact magnetic (reluctance-type) pickup coil, which produces a sinusoidal output voltage whose **frequency is directly proportional to the volumetric flow rate**.*

### Conceptual Analogy

Place a child's **pinwheel** at the mouth of a garden hose. The harder you open the tap, the faster the pinwheel spins. Now attach a small magnet to one of the blades and hold an induction coil nearby — every time the magnet passes, the coil "clicks" with an electrical pulse. **Count the pulses per second** and you know exactly how fast the water is flowing.

In essence, the turbine flowmeter is an industrial-grade, precision-engineered "smart pinwheel."

**Typical Accuracy:** $\pm 0.5\%$ of reading — among the highest of any industrial flowmeter. Used wherever **custody transfer** (billing) of liquids occurs, e.g., aviation fuel, gasoline, diesel.

---

## 1.4 Anemometer — Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> *An anemometer is an instrument used to measure the **velocity of a fluid (typically a gas / air)**. The mechanical (cup / vane) anemometer employs rotating cups or a propeller blade whose angular velocity is related to the linear velocity of the air stream. The rotation count per unit time is converted to a wind / air speed reading.*

### Conceptual Analogy

Stretch your arm out of a moving car window. Hold a small **cup-shaped paper cup on a stick**. The faster the car moves, the stronger the air pushes against the cup and the faster the stick rotates. An anemometer is a precision-instrumented version of this toy, with **3 or 4 hemispherical cups** on horizontal arms rotating about a vertical shaft.

**Historical Note:** The cup anemometer was invented in **1846** by **John Thomas Romney Robinson** — a form still in standard meteorological use worldwide.

---

## 1.5 Key Physical Constants & Standards

| Constant / Standard | Symbol | Value / Definition |
|---|---|---|
| Volumetric flow rate | $Q$ | $\mathrm{m^3/s}$ (SI) |
| Mass flow rate | $\dot{m}$ | $\mathrm{kg/s}$ |
| Density of water (4 °C) | $\rho_w$ | **1000 kg/m³** |
| Density of air (15 °C, 1 atm) | $\rho_{air}$ | **1.225 kg/m³** |
| Standard atmospheric pressure | $P_{atm}$ | **101.325 kPa** |
| Kinematic viscosity of water (20 °C) | $\nu_w$ | $1.004 \times 10^{-6}\ \mathrm{m^2/s}$ |
| Cup anemometer constant | $K_a$ | Device-specific, typically $\mathbf{2.5\text{–}3.0\ m/s\ per\ Hz}$ |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always state the SI unit **and** the practical industrial unit (e.g., $\mathrm{m^3/h}$, $\mathrm{L/min}$, $\mathrm{L/s}$) when solving flow problems — examiners reward unit-awareness.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Rotating (Nutating) Disk Flowmeter — Operating Principle

### 2.1.1 Construction Details

A nutating-disk flowmeter consists of the following key components:

1. **Measuring Chamber** — A precisely machined cylindrical cavity of volume $V_c$.
2. **Disk (Nutating Disk)** — A flat circular plate with a central spherical boss on its underside.
3. **Central Ball / Pivot** — A precision steel ball that supports the disk and provides the single point about which nutation occurs.
4. **Drive Shaft** — Coupled to the disk ball; protrudes through the chamber wall.
5. **Gear Train & Register** — External mechanical counter that totalises the nutation cycles.
6. **Inlet and Outlet Ports** — Positioned diametrically opposite; the pressure difference between them generates the torque driving the nutation.

### 2.1.2 Step-by-Step Operating Logic

**Step 1 — Fluid Entry:**
The process fluid enters the chamber through the **inlet port**, exerting pressure on one face of the disk.

**Step 2 — Pressure-Induced Nutation:**
Because the disk is mounted on a single central pivot (the ball), it cannot translate; instead, it tilts (nutates) so that its centre traces a small circle on the ball. This is called the **nutation cone**.

**Step 3 — Volume Trapping:**
At one angular position, the disk seals off a fixed volume $V_d$ of fluid. As the disk nutates further, this trapped volume is **pushed** toward the outlet.

**Step 4 — Discharge:**
At the opposite angular position, the trapped fluid is expelled through the **outlet port**.

**Step 5 — Counting:**
The drive shaft, mechanically linked to the disk ball, performs **one rotation per nutation cycle**. The external gear train counts these rotations and displays the cumulative volume.

### 2.1.3 The Displacement Equation

For **$N$ complete nutation cycles**, the total volume passed is:

$$V_{total} = N \times V_d$$

where:
- $V_{total}$ = cumulative volume displaced $(\mathrm{m^3})$
- $N$ = number of nutation cycles (dimensionless)
- $V_d$ = volume displaced per cycle $(\mathrm{m^3})$, determined by chamber geometry

### 2.1.4 Why It Works — The "How" Behind the Principle

- **Positive displacement** ensures that the fluid **must** pass through the metered volume — there is no bypass path.
- Accuracy is **independent of flow profile** (laminar vs. turbulent) as long as the disk follows the pressure differential fully.
- The only source of error is **mechanical leakage** past the disk edges — hence the need for tight machining tolerances.

### 2.1.5 Advantages & Limitations

| Advantages | Limitations |
|---|---|
| High accuracy ($\pm 1\%$ to $\pm 2\%$) | Limited to **clean liquids** — particulates damage the disk-chamber seal |
| Excellent at low flow rates | Pressure drop is relatively high (energy cost) |
| Direct volume readout — no need for power | Not suitable for very viscous fluids or slurries |
| Robust, no external power needed for register | Pulsating flow output (cyclic pressure variation) |

---

## 2.2 Turbine Flowmeter — Operating Principle

### 2.2.1 Construction Details

1. **Pipe Body (Flow Tube)** — Straight, constant-bore section with flow-straightening vanes upstream to condition the flow profile.
2. **Rotor** — A multi-bladed wheel (typically 4–8 helical blades) made of ferromagnetic stainless steel or aluminium.
3. **Bearings** — Sapphire / tungsten-carbide jewel bearings or ball bearings minimising frictional drag.
4. **Magnetic Pickup Coil (Pickoff)** — A permanent magnet with a sensing coil mounted *externally* on the housing. Each blade passing the coil alters the magnetic reluctance, inducing a voltage pulse.
5. **Signal Conditioning Electronics** — Converts the sinusoidal coil output into clean square-wave pulses.

### 2.2.2 Step-by-Step Operating Logic

**Step 1 — Flow Conditioning:**
Flow straighteners (honeycomb or vane packs) ensure the velocity profile entering the rotor is **uniform and axial**.

**Step 2 — Rotor Acceleration:**
The flowing fluid exerts a **tangential drag force** on the rotor blades. The rotor accelerates until the drag torque equals the frictional torque — at this point, it reaches a steady-state angular velocity.

**Step 3 — Magnetic Pulse Generation:**
As each ferromagnetic blade passes the pickup coil, it changes the magnetic flux through the coil. By **Faraday's law of induction**, an EMF is induced:

$$e(t) = -N_c \frac{d\Phi}{dt}$$

**Step 4 — Frequency-to-Flow Conversion:**
The frequency $f$ of the induced pulses is **linearly proportional** to the volumetric flow rate $Q$:

$$f = K \cdot Q$$

where $K$ is the **meter constant** (pulses per unit volume), calibrated individually for each meter.

**Step 5 — Display / Totalisation:**
The pulses are counted over a fixed time interval to obtain instantaneous flow, or accumulated for total volume.

### 2.2.3 The Turbine Rotor Equation (Ideal Linear Model)

The rotor blades, when in equilibrium, yield:

$$\omega = K_T \cdot \bar{v}$$

$$\bar{v} = \frac{Q}{A}$$

Combining:

$$f = \frac{N_b \cdot \omega}{2\pi} = \frac{N_b \cdot K_T}{2\pi \cdot A} \cdot Q$$

Hence the **meter constant**:

$$K = \frac{N_b \cdot K_T}{2\pi \cdot A}\quad \left[\frac{\text{pulses}}{\mathrm{m^3}}\right]$$

where:
- $N_b$ = number of rotor blades
- $\omega$ = rotor angular velocity $(\mathrm{rad/s})$
- $\bar{v}$ = mean axial fluid velocity $(\mathrm{m/s})$
- $A$ = pipe cross-sectional area $(\mathrm{m^2})$
- $K_T$ = turbine-blade torque coefficient

### 2.2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Application |
|---|---|
| Petroleum | Custody transfer of crude oil and refined fuels |
| Aerospace | Aircraft fuel quantity / flow measurement |
| Cryogenics | LNG, liquid nitrogen, liquid oxygen |
| Chemical | Acid / caustic flow with proper materials of construction |
| Water treatment | District-metered clean water |

---

## 2.3 Anemometer — Operating Principle

### 2.3.1 Cup Anemometer — Construction

1. **3 or 4 Hemispherical Cups** — Mounted on horizontal arms at equal angular spacing.
2. **Vertical Shaft** — Rotates in low-friction bearings.
3. **Counting Mechanism** — Mechanical reed-switch or optical encoder producing electrical pulses proportional to rotation rate.
4. **Tail Vane** *(optional)* — On directional anemometers, aligns the axis with the wind direction.

### 2.3.2 Step-by-Step Operating Logic

**Step 1 — Asymmetric Drag:**
The concave side of each cup **catches** the wind, experiencing high drag. The convex side deflects flow, experiencing low drag.

**Step 2 — Net Torque:**
This drag asymmetry produces a **net torque** on the rotor, causing it to spin about the vertical axis.

**Step 3 — Steady-State Rotation:**
The rotor reaches a steady angular velocity when aerodynamic torque = bearing friction torque.

**Step 4 — Pulse Generation:**
A magnet on the shaft passes a reed switch / Hall-effect sensor once per revolution, producing a pulse train.

**Step 5 — Linearisation:**
The pulse frequency is converted to wind speed using the empirical relation:

$$v = a + b \cdot N$$

where $N$ is the rotation rate (revolutions per second) and $a$, $b$ are device-specific calibration constants.

### 2.3.2.1 Cup-Anemometer Overspeed Phenomenon

A well-known anomaly: due to the angular momentum of the cups, a cup anemometer **over-reads** the true wind speed. The ratio:

$$v_{measured} = \frac{v_{true}}{1 - \frac{v_{true}}{v_{tip,max}}}$$

shows that as wind speed approaches the rim-tip speed, the error diverges. Hence cup anemometers are **calibrated for low-to-moderate wind ranges** ($< 30$ m/s typically).

### 2.3.3 Hot-Wire Anemometer (Brief — Comparison Context)

Although not strictly mechanical, the hot-wire anemometer deserves mention because KTU examiners often compare the two. It measures flow by sensing the **heat loss** from an electrically heated thin wire:

$$P_{loss} = I^2 R \propto \sqrt{v}$$

This provides microsecond response time and is the standard for **turbulence measurement**.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **definitive cheat sheet** for this topic. Memorise these equations before attempting any KTU 2024 problem on this module.

| # | Concept | Formula | Description of Terms | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Nutating Disk Total Volume | $V_{total} = N \cdot V_d$ | $N$ = cycles, $V_d$ = displaced volume per cycle | $\mathrm{m^3}$ |
| 2 | Nutating Disk Flow Rate | $Q = f_n \cdot V_d$ | $f_n$ = nutation frequency $(\mathrm{Hz})$ | $\mathrm{m^3/s}$ |
| 3 | Turbine Pulse Frequency | $f = K \cdot Q$ | $K$ = meter constant (pulses/$\mathrm{m^3}$) | $\mathrm{Hz}$ |
| 4 | Turbine Volumetric Flow | $Q = \dfrac{f}{K}$ | Inverse of the above | $\mathrm{m^3/s}$ |
| 5 | Turbine Rotor Velocity Relation | $\bar{v} = \dfrac{Q}{A}$ | $\bar{v}$ = mean fluid velocity | $\mathrm{m/s}$ |
| 6 | Turbine Rotor Speed | $\omega = K_T \cdot \bar{v}$ | $K_T$ = blade torque coefficient | $\mathrm{rad/s}$ |
| 7 | Turbine Meter Constant | $K = \dfrac{N_b \cdot K_T}{2\pi \cdot A}$ | $N_b$ = number of blades | pulses/$\mathrm{m^3}$ |
| 8 | Anemometer Linear Model | $v = a + b \cdot N$ | $N$ = shaft rotation rate $(\mathrm{Hz})$, $a, b$ constants | $\mathrm{m/s}$ |
| 9 | Anemometer Rotational Speed | $N = \dfrac{f_a}{n_c}$ | $f_a$ = pulse freq, $n_c$ = cups per revolution (typically 1 pulse/rev) | $\mathrm{Hz}$ |
| 10 | Cup Overspeed Correction | $v_{true} = \dfrac{v_{meas}}{1 + \dfrac{v_{meas}}{v_{tip,max}}}$ | $v_{tip,max}$ = tangential velocity of cup at full wind | $\mathrm{m/s}$ |
| 11 | Reynolds Number (pipe flow) | $\mathrm{Re} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot \bar{v} \cdot D}{\mu}$ | $D$ = pipe diameter, $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity | dimensionless |
| 12 | Continuity Equation | $Q = A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2$ | For incompressible flow in a turbine meter | $\mathrm{m^3/s}$ |
| 13 | Mass Flow from Volume Flow | $\dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q$ | Density $\rho$ required | $\mathrm{kg/s}$ |
| 14 | Faraday's Law (Pickup) | $e(t) = -N_c \dfrac{d\Phi}{dt}$ | $N_c$ = coil turns, $\Phi$ = flux | $\mathrm{V}$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Trick:** Many KTU 2024 questions combine (3), (5), and (13) into a single "compute the mass flow rate given pulse frequency" problem. Practice unit-conversion pathways — that is where 80 % of marks are lost.

---

## 2.5 Comparative Summary — When to Use Which?

| Parameter | Nutating Disk | Turbine | Cup Anemometer |
|---|---|---|---|
| Fluid Medium | Clean liquids | Clean liquids / gases | Air / gases |
| Output Type | Mechanical counter | Electrical frequency | Mechanical or electrical |
| Typical Accuracy | $\pm 1\text{–}2\%$ | $\pm 0.25\text{–}0.5\%$ | $\pm 3\text{–}5\%$ |
| Pressure Drop | High | Moderate | Negligible |
| Need for Power | No | Yes (signal conditioning) | No (mechanical) / Low (electrical) |
| Best Use Case | Domestic water, fuel batching | Custody transfer, aviation fuel | Weather stations, HVAC airflow |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation A — Total Volume Discharged by a Nutating Disk Meter

### Problem Context

A domestic water meter has a nutating disk of radius $R_d = 40\ \mathrm{mm}$. The disk chamber is cylindrical with the same radius. The disk makes one complete nutation cycle per revolution of its central ball. Determine the **volume displaced per cycle**, $V_d$.

### Given

- Disk radius: $R_d = 40\ \mathrm{mm} = 0.040\ \mathrm{m}$
- Chamber geometry: cylindrical with same radius as disk
- Working fluid: water at $\rho = 1000\ \mathrm{kg/m^3}$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the displaced volume geometry.**

As the disk nutates on the central ball, it sweeps out a known volume. In an ideal nutating disk meter, the displacement volume $V_d$ is set by the **chamber geometry**, not by the disk radius alone. For a **simplified cylindrical chamber** with the disk acting as a radial wiper:

$$V_d = \frac{2}{3}\pi R_d^{\,3}\tan(\alpha)$$

where $\alpha$ is the half-angle of the nutation cone (typically $3°\text{–}7°$). However, in most KTU 2024 problems, the **factory-calibrated displacement per cycle is given numerically**, so the derivation simply uses the energy-balance approach.

**Step 2 — Apply energy balance across the disk.**

The fluid entering at pressure $P_1$ does work against the disk; the fluid leaving at $P_2$ has lower pressure. Work done per cycle on the disk:

$$W_{cycle} = (P_1 - P_2) \cdot V_d$$

This work overcomes bearing friction $W_f$:

$$W_{cycle} = W_f + \Delta KE_{disk}$$

At steady state, $\Delta KE_{disk} = 0$ (one cycle ends where it began), so:

$$(P_1 - P_2) V_d = W_f$$

**Step 3 — Express total displaced volume after $N$ cycles.**

For $N$ complete cycles, summing over each cycle:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N}(P_1 - P_2)_i \cdot V_d = \sum_{i=1}^{N} W_{f,i}$$

For low-speed steady operation, $(P_1 - P_2)$ and $W_f$ are essentially constant, so:

$$V_{total} = N \cdot V_d$$

This is the **fundamental positive-displacement equation**.

**Step 4 — Convert to volumetric flow rate.**

Differentiate with respect to time $t$:

$$\frac{dV_{total}}{dt} = \frac{dN}{dt} \cdot V_d$$

$$Q = f_n \cdot V_d$$

where $f_n = \dfrac{dN}{dt}$ is the nutation frequency in $\mathrm{Hz}$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation B — Turbine Flowmeter Frequency-Flow Relationship

### Problem Context

A turbine flowmeter is installed in a pipe of inner diameter $D = 50\ \mathrm{mm}$. The rotor has $N_b = 6$ blades. The magnetic pickup produces **120 pulses in 30 seconds** when a steady flow of oil (kinematic viscosity $\nu = 5 \times 10^{-5}\ \mathrm{m^2/s}$) passes through the meter. Determine:
1. The meter constant $K$ (pulses/$\mathrm{m^3}$).
2. The volumetric flow rate $Q$ in $\mathrm{m^3/s}$.
3. The mean fluid velocity $\bar{v}$.

### Given Data

- Pipe diameter: $D = 50\ \mathrm{mm} = 0.050\ \mathrm{m}$
- Number of blades: $N_b = 6$
- Pulse count: $n = 120$ in $\Delta t = 30\ \mathrm{s}$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Calculate pulse frequency.**

The frequency is the number of pulses per unit time:

$$f = \frac{n}{\Delta t} = \frac{120}{30} = 4\ \mathrm{Hz}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate pipe cross-sectional area.**

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (0.050)^2}{4}$$

$$A = \frac{\pi \times 0.0025}{4} = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3}\ \mathrm{m^2}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the fundamental turbine equation.**

The frequency is given by:

$$f = K \cdot Q$$

Rearranging for $Q$:

$$Q = \frac{f}{K}$$

However, $K$ is what we need to determine. Use the geometric definition:

$$K = \frac{N_b \cdot K_T}{2\pi \cdot A}$$

**Step 4 — Empirically determine $K_T$ from calibration.**

In a calibration run, the rotor is driven at known $\omega$ and the corresponding flow rate is measured. For this problem, we assume the rotor makes **one revolution per $N_b$ pulses** (one pulse per blade passage). Therefore, the rotor angular velocity is:

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi f}{N_b} = \frac{2\pi \times 4}{6} = \frac{8\pi}{6} = 4.189\ \mathrm{rad/s}$$

**Step 5 — Relate rotor speed to mean fluid velocity.**

For an ideal linear turbine:

$$\omega = K_T \cdot \bar{v}$$

$$\bar{v} = \frac{Q}{A}$$

Therefore:

$$\omega = K_T \cdot \frac{Q}{A}$$

**Step 6 — Solve the simultaneous equations.**

We have two equations:

$$f = K \cdot Q \quad (i)$$

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi f}{N_b} \quad (ii)$$

$$\omega = K_T \cdot \frac{Q}{A} \quad (iii)$$

From (ii): $K_T \cdot \dfrac{Q}{A} = \dfrac{2\pi f}{N_b}$, so:

$$Q = \frac{2\pi f \cdot A}{K_T \cdot N_b}$$

Substituting into (i):

$$f = K \cdot \frac{2\pi f \cdot A}{K_T \cdot N_b}$$

$$1 = \frac{2\pi K A}{K_T N_b}$$

$$K = \frac{K_T N_b}{2\pi A}$$

**Step 7 — Numerical evaluation with assumed $K_T$.**

For the typical turbine-blade torque coefficient $K_T \approx 5.0$ (rad/m, i.e., rad/s per m/s of fluid velocity):

$$K = \frac{5.0 \times 6}{2\pi \times 1.9635 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$K = \frac{30}{1.2336 \times 10^{-2}} = 2432\ \mathrm{pulses/m^3}$$

**Step 8 — Compute volumetric flow rate.**

$$Q = \frac{f}{K} = \frac{4}{2432} = 1.645 \times 10^{-3}\ \mathrm{m^3/s}$$

**Step 9 — Compute mean fluid velocity.**

$$\bar{v} = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{1.645 \times 10^{-3}}{1.9635 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.838\ \mathrm{m/s}$$

**Step 10 — Sanity check with Reynolds number.**

$$\mathrm{Re} = \frac{\bar{v} \cdot D}{\nu} = \frac{0.838 \times 0.050}{5 \times 10^{-5}} = 838$$

Since $\mathrm{Re} > 4000$, the flow is **turbulent** — consistent with typical turbine meter operating conditions.

### Final Answer Summary

| Quantity | Symbol | Value |
|---|---|---|
| Pulse frequency | $f$ | $4\ \mathrm{Hz}$ |
| Meter constant | $K$ | $2432\ \mathrm{pulses/m^3}$ |
| Volumetric flow rate | $Q$ | $1.645 \times 10^{-3}\ \mathrm{m^3/s}$ |
| Mean fluid velocity | $\bar{v}$ | $0.838\ \mathrm{m/s}$ |
| Reynolds number | $\mathrm{Re}$ | $838$ (transitional — meter may need recalibration) |

---

## 3.3 Derivation C — Cup Anemometer Linearisation & Overspeed

### Problem Context

A 4-cup anemometer produces **18 pulses per minute** when exposed to a steady wind. The calibration constants are $a = 0.20\ \mathrm{m/s}$ and $b = 2.75\ \mathrm{m\cdot s^{-1}}$ (i.e., $\mathrm{m/s}$ per $\mathrm{Hz}$). Determine:
1. The shaft rotational speed in $\mathrm{Hz}$.
2. The true wind speed.
3. The **corrected true wind speed** accounting for cup overspeed, given $v_{tip,max} = 30\ \mathrm{m/s}$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Convert pulse rate to rotational frequency.**

One pulse per revolution (typical of cup anemometers with one magnet per shaft):

$$N = \frac{18\ \mathrm{pulses}}{60\ \mathrm{s}} = 0.300\ \mathrm{Hz}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the linear calibration equation.**

$$v = a + b \cdot N$$

$$v = 0.20 + 2.75 \times 0.300$$

$$v = 0.20 + 0.825 = 1.025\ \mathrm{m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the overspeed correction.**

The cup anemometer equation with overspeed is:

$$v_{true} = \frac{v_{meas}}{1 + \frac{v_{meas}}{v_{tip,max}}}$$

$$v_{true} = \frac{1.025}{1 + \frac{1.025}{30}}$$

$$v_{true} = \frac{1.025}{1 + 0.03417} = \frac{1.025}{1.03417}$$

$$v_{true} = 0.991\ \mathrm{m/s}$$

### Final Answer Summary

| Quantity | Symbol | Value |
|---|---|---|
| Shaft rotation frequency | $N$ | $0.300\ \mathrm{Hz}$ |
| Measured (uncorrected) speed | $v_{meas}$ | $1.025\ \mathrm{m/s}$ |
| True (corrected) wind speed | $v_{true}$ | $0.991\ \mathrm{m/s}$ |
| Overspeed correction magnitude | $\Delta v$ | $0.034\ \mathrm{m/s}$ (~3.3 %) |

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — KTU 2024 View

| Property | Nutating Disk | Turbine | Anemometer |
|---|---|---|---|
| Governing equation | $Q = f_n \cdot V_d$ | $f = K \cdot Q$ | $v = a + b \cdot N$ |
| Detected variable | Cycle count (mechanical) | Blade-pass frequency (electrical) | Cup rotation rate (mech./elec.) |
| Required calibration | Per-cycle volume $V_d$ | Meter constant $K$ (pulses/$\mathrm{m^3}$) | Constants $a, b$ |
| Energy source for reading | None (mechanical register) | External power for pickup | None / minimal |
| Effect of fluid density | Negligible | Slight (inertia of rotor) | Negligible |
| Effect of viscosity | Moderate | Strong (affects blade drag) | Negligible (air) |
| Best operating range | Low to moderate flow | Wide (laminar to turbulent) | Wind speed < 30 m/s |
| KTU 2024 Weight | Medium | **High** (numerical problems) | Medium (conceptual) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: With the help of a neat diagram, explain the working of a nutating-disk type flowmeter.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A nutating-disk flowmeter is a **positive-displacement** type flowmeter. It consists of a circular disk mounted on a **central spherical pivot ball** inside a precision cylindrical measuring chamber.

The process fluid enters through the **inlet port** and exerts pressure on one face of the disk. Because the disk is supported on a single ball, it cannot translate; instead, it performs a **nutating (wobbling) motion**, its centre tracing a small circle on the ball.

Each complete nutation cycle **entraps and discharges a fixed volume $V_d$** of fluid through the outlet. A drive shaft coupled to the disk ball rotates once per cycle, driving an external **gear train and mechanical register** that totalises the cycles into a cumulative volume reading.

$$V_{total} = N \times V_d$$

This meter is widely used in **domestic water supply** and **fuel dispensing** because it requires no external power and gives good accuracy ($\pm 1\%$ to $\pm 2\%$) for clean liquids.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Many students write only "the disk rotates" instead of "the disk *nutates* (wobbles on a fixed pivot)". Examiners specifically test this terminology — using "rotate" will cost you a mark.

---

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Distinguish between a cup anemometer and a hot-wire anemometer. Mention one application of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Cup Anemometer | Hot-Wire Anemometer |
|---|---|---|
| Principle | Mechanical rotation proportional to wind speed | Heat loss from a heated wire proportional to flow |
| Output | Mechanical rotation / electrical pulse | Resistance change / voltage change |
| Response Time | Slow (seconds) | Very fast (microseconds) |
| Fluid Suitability | Air (gas flow only) | Air, gas, even liquids |
| Power Required | None / minimal | Always required (to heat the wire) |
| Accuracy | $\pm 3\text{–}5\%$ | $\pm 0.5\%$ or better |
| Application | Weather stations, HVAC | Turbulence research, boundary-layer studies |

**Application:**
- **Cup anemometer:** Meteorological wind-speed measurement at airports and weather stations.
- **Hot-wire anemometer:** Measuring turbulence intensity in wind-tunnel experiments.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per **KTU 2024 ESE regulation**, students answer **one** of two internal-choice questions per module. Each 14-mark question typically splits into **Part (a) — 7 marks** and **Part (b) — 7 marks**.

---

### Question B-A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a)** With the help of a functional diagram, explain the construction and working of a **turbine flowmeter**. Derive the relation between the pulse frequency and the volumetric flow rate. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A turbine flowmeter with 6 blades is fitted in a pipe of 80 mm bore. The meter constant is **1250 pulses/m³**. The pickup coil records **300 pulses in 20 seconds**. Calculate:
1. Volumetric flow rate in $\mathrm{m^3/s}$ and $\mathrm{L/min}$.
2. Mean fluid velocity in $\mathrm{m/s}$.
3. Reynolds number if the fluid is water at 20 °C.

**(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Construction (3 marks):**
A turbine flowmeter consists of:
1. A **straight flow tube** with flow-straightening vanes at the inlet.
2. A **multi-bladed rotor** (typically 4–8 helical blades) mounted axially on frictionless bearings.
3. A **magnetic pickup coil** mounted externally on the housing — contains a permanent magnet and a sensing coil.
4. **Signal-conditioning electronics** that produce clean square-wave pulses.

**Working (2 marks):**
The flowing fluid exerts a tangential drag on the rotor blades. The rotor spins at an angular velocity $\omega$ proportional to the mean fluid velocity $\bar{v}$. Each ferromagnetic blade passing the pickup coil alters the magnetic flux, inducing an EMF. The frequency $f$ of this EMF is directly proportional to the volumetric flow rate $Q$.

**Derivation (2 marks):**

$$\omega = K_T \cdot \bar{v} = K_T \cdot \frac{Q}{A}$$

The frequency of blade passages is:

$$f = \frac{N_b \cdot \omega}{2\pi}$$

Substituting $\omega$:

$$f = \frac{N_b \cdot K_T}{2\pi A} \cdot Q = K \cdot Q$$

where the meter constant $K = \dfrac{N_b \cdot K_T}{2\pi A}$ (pulses/$\mathrm{m^3}$).

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- $N_b = 6$, $D = 80\ \mathrm{mm} = 0.080\ \mathrm{m}$, $K = 1250\ \mathrm{pulses/m^3}$
- $n = 300$ pulses in $\Delta t = 20\ \mathrm{s}$
- Water at 20 °C, $\nu_w = 1.004 \times 10^{-6}\ \mathrm{m^2/s}$

**Step 1 — Pulse frequency.**

$$f = \frac{n}{\Delta t} = \frac{300}{20} = 15\ \mathrm{Hz}$$

**Step 2 — Volumetric flow rate.**

$$Q = \frac{f}{K} = \frac{15}{1250} = 0.012\ \mathrm{m^3/s}$$

Convert to $\mathrm{L/min}$:

$$Q = 0.012 \times 1000 \times 60 = 720\ \mathrm{L/min}$$

**Step 3 — Pipe cross-sectional area.**

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (0.080)^2}{4} = 5.027 \times 10^{-3}\ \mathrm{m^2}$$

**Step 4 — Mean fluid velocity.**

$$\bar{v} = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{0.012}{5.027 \times 10^{-3}} = 2.387\ \mathrm{m/s}$$

**Step 5 — Reynolds number.**

$$\mathrm{Re} = \frac{\bar{v} \cdot D}{\nu_w} = \frac{2.387 \times 0.080}{1.004 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$\mathrm{Re} = 1.902 \times 10^{5}$$

This is well into the **turbulent regime** ($\mathrm{Re} > 4000$), confirming the meter is operating in its valid range.

**Final Answers:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| $Q$ | $0.012\ \mathrm{m^3/s} = 720\ \mathrm{L/min}$ |
| $\bar{v}$ | $2.387\ \mathrm{m/s}$ |
| $\mathrm{Re}$ | $1.902 \times 10^{5}$ (turbulent) |

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (Internal Choice)

**Q: (a)** Explain the construction and working of a **nutating disk flowmeter** with a neat functional diagram. List any **three advantages** and **two limitations**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **cup anemometer** has 4 cups. The magnet on the shaft produces **1 pulse per revolution**. During a 2-minute interval, **180 pulses** are recorded. The calibration equation is:

$$v = 0.15 + 2.50 \cdot N$$

where $v$ is in $\mathrm{m/s}$ and $N$ is the shaft frequency in $\mathrm{Hz}$. Calculate:
1. Shaft rotation frequency in $\mathrm{rpm}$ and $\mathrm{Hz}$.
2. Measured wind speed using the linear equation.
3. Corrected true wind speed using $v_{tip,max} = 25\ \mathrm{m/s}$.

**(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Construction (2.5 marks):**
1. **Cylindrical measuring chamber** with precisely machined inlet and outlet ports.
2. **Nutating disk** — circular plate with central spherical boss on its underside.
3. **Pivot ball** — supports the disk and acts as a single-point support for nutation.
4. **Drive shaft** coupled to the ball, protruding through the chamber wall.
5. **Gear train** and **mechanical register** outside the chamber.

**Working (2 marks):**
Fluid entering the chamber creates a pressure differential across the disk. Because the disk can only pivot on the central ball, it undergoes a **nutating motion** (wobbling), with its centre tracing a circle on the ball. Each nutation cycle **traps a fixed volume $V_d$** between the disk face and the chamber wall. The trapped fluid is then pushed toward the outlet as the nutation continues. The drive shaft performs **one rotation per nutation**, and the external register counts these rotations to display cumulative volume.

**Advantages (1.5 marks):**
1. High accuracy ($\pm 1\%$ to $\pm 2\%$) over a wide flow range.
2. Excellent performance at **low flow rates** where other meters fail.
3. **No external power** required for the register — purely mechanical.

**Limitations (1 mark):**
1. Only suitable for **clean liquids** — particulates damage the precision seal.
2. Causes **pulsating flow** output, which may affect downstream equipment.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- Cups = 4, pulses per revolution = 1
- Total pulses in 2 min = 180
- Calibration: $v = 0.15 + 2.50 N$
- $v_{tip,max} = 25\ \mathrm{m/s}$

**Step 1 — Pulse frequency.**

$$f_a = \frac{180}{120} = 1.5\ \mathrm{Hz}$$

Since 1 pulse/revolution:

$$N = f_a = 1.5\ \mathrm{Hz}$$

Convert to rpm:

$$N_{rpm} = 1.5 \times 60 = 90\ \mathrm{rpm}$$

**Step 2 — Measured wind speed.**

$$v_{meas} = 0.15 + 2.50 \times 1.5 = 0.15 + 3.75 = 3.90\ \mathrm{m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Apply overspeed correction.**

$$v_{true} = \frac{v_{meas}}{1 + \dfrac{v_{meas}}{v_{tip,max}}}$$

$$v_{true} = \frac{3.90}{1 + \dfrac{3.90}{25}}$$

$$v_{true} = \frac{3.90}{1 + 0.156} = \frac{3.90}{1.156}$$

$$v_{true} = 3.373\ \mathrm{m/s}$$

**Final Answers:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Shaft frequency | $1.5\ \mathrm{Hz} = 90\ \mathrm{rpm}$ |
| Measured wind speed | $3.90\ \mathrm{m/s}$ |
| Corrected (true) wind speed | $3.37\ \mathrm{m/s}$ |
| Correction magnitude | $\approx 13.5\%$ over-reading |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Carefully:**
>
> 1. **Unit conversion** ($rpm \leftrightarrow Hz$): Missing the $\times 60$ factor costs 1 mark outright. Always write both forms explicitly.
> 2. **Overspeed correction direction:** Students often write $v_{true} = v_{meas} \times (1 + \frac{v_{meas}}{v_{tip,max}})$ — **this is wrong**. The correct form is **division**, because the cup anemometer over-reads and the correction must *reduce* the value.
> 3. **Pulse-to-rpm confusion:** An anemometer with 4 cups and 1 magnet per shaft produces 1 pulse per *shaft revolution*, not 4 pulses. Do not multiply by the cup count.
> 4. **Nutating disk — do not write "disk rotates":** It *nutates* (wobbles). Examiner reads for this exact word.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Use this checklist the night before your KTU exam. Tick off every item.**

### ✅ Nutating Disk Flowmeter
- [ ] Principle: **Positive displacement** (fixed volume per cycle).
- [ ] Disk **nutates** on a **central ball** (NOT rotates).
- [ ] Each nutation displaces a **fixed volume $V_d$**.
- [ ] Total volume: $V_{total} = N \cdot V_d$.
- [ ] Flow rate: $Q = f_n \cdot V_d$.
- [ ] Use case: domestic water meters, fuel dispensers.
- [ ] Limitation: clean liquids only, high pressure drop.

### ✅ Turbine Flowmeter
- [ ] Multi-bladed rotor in flow stream, on low-friction bearings.
- [ ] **Magnetic pickup** detects blade passage → pulse train.
- [ ] Frequency-flow relation: $\boxed{f = K \cdot Q}$ (linear).
- [ ] Meter constant $K$ is calibrated in **pulses/$\mathrm{m^3}$**.
- [ ] Accuracy: $\pm 0.25\%$ to $\pm 0.5\%$ — among highest available.
- [ ] Use case: custody transfer, aviation fuel, cryogenics.
- [ ] Reynolds number check required for valid operation.

### ✅ Cup Anemometer
- [ ] 3 or 4 hemispherical cups on horizontal arms.
- [ ] **Concave vs. convex drag asymmetry** drives rotation.
- [ ] Linear model: $v = a + b \cdot N$.
- [ ] Overspeed correction: $v_{true} = \dfrac{v_{meas}}{1 + v_{meas}/v_{tip,max}}$.
- [ ] Use case: meteorology, HVAC airflow.
- [ ] Limitation: over-reads at high wind speeds.

### ✅ Cross-Cutting Principles
- [ ] **Continuity equation**: $Q = A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2$ (incompressible flow).
- [ ] **Mass from volume**: $\dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q$.
- [ ] **Reynolds number**: $\mathrm{Re} = \dfrac{\rho \bar{v} D}{\mu} = \dfrac{\bar{v} D}{\nu}$.
- [ ] **Faraday's law** for pickup: $e(t) = -N_c \dfrac{d\Phi}{dt}$.
- [ ] Always state **units** and **significant figures** in final answers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonics for the KTU Hall:**
> - **Nutating Disk:** "**N**utations **C**ount **V**olumes" → $Q = f_n V_d$.
> - **Turbine:** "**F**requency equals **K** times **Q**" → $f = KQ$.
> - **Anemometer:** "**V**elocity is **a** plus **b N**" → $v = a + bN$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Level Measurement: Direct Type and Indirect Type, Differential Pressure Method for Pressurized Vessels, and Solid Level Detectors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT724 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Level Measurement: Direct Type and Indirect Type, Differential Pressure Method for Pressurized Vessels, and Solid Level Detectors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Level Measurement

**Level Measurement** is the process of determining the position or height of the free surface (interface) of a fluid, slurry, or bulk solid contained within a vessel, tank, silo, or pipeline relative to a chosen reference datum. In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme (Industrial Instrumentation), it is classified as a fundamental process variable (alongside pressure, temperature, and flow) that must be reliably measured for inventory control, process safety, and regulatory compliance in chemical, petrochemical, power, and pharmaceutical industries.

Level measurement is broadly bifurcated into two main philosophical categories:

1. **Direct Level Measurement** — The level is read *visually* or *mechanically* without intermediate mathematical conversion. The output is a length (metres, mm, inches) read directly from a graduated scale.
2. **Indirect Level Measurement** — The level is *inferred* by measuring a secondary variable (pressure, capacitance, ultrasonic echo, radiation absorption, weight, etc.) and then mathematically computing the height.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** For the 2024 Scheme ESE, students are expected to (a) classify level measurement methods, (b) explain the operational principle of each, (c) derive the governing equations, and (d) solve numerical problems on the **Differential Pressure (DP) Method** for pressurized tanks and on the **Capacitance Method** for both conductive and non-conductive liquids.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are boiling water in a transparent kettle on your stove. You can see the water level through the glass wall — this is essentially **direct level measurement** (sight glass principle). Now imagine the kettle is made of thick stainless steel — you cannot see inside. However, if you stand on a weighing scale, the weight increases as water is added. The water level can be *inferred* from weight (this is the **weight/load cell method**). Alternatively, you can attach a small pressure gauge at the bottom of the kettle — the pressure reading rises linearly with the water column height above the gauge (this is the **hydrostatic / differential pressure method**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Direct methods are simple, cheap, and highly accurate, but they require human intervention or visual access. Indirect methods are automated, suitable for hazardous and remote applications, and form the backbone of modern DCS/SCADA-based process plants.

## 1.3 Why Is Level Measurement Critical?

| Industrial Need | Role of Level Measurement |
|---|---|
| **Inventory Management** | Real-time volume/mass accounting in bulk storage tanks |
| **Process Safety** | Prevention of tank overflow, dry-running of pumps, boiler low-water trip |
| **Quality Control** | Maintaining interface levels in separators, decanters, distillation columns |
| **Environmental Compliance** | Avoiding spills of toxic, flammable, or corrosive media |
| **Cost Optimization** | Avoid overfilling or underutilization of expensive chemicals |

The **standard reference units** for level in SI are **metres (m)**, with the working fluid properties characterized by **density (ρ) in kg/m³**, **gravitational acceleration g = 9.80665 m/s²**, and **pressure in Pascals (Pa) or bar (1 bar = 10⁵ Pa)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Level Measurement Methods

The complete taxonomy relevant to KTU 2024 Module 3 is structured below.

### A. Direct Type Methods
1. **Sight Glass (Gauge Glass)** — A transparent tube/column fitted parallel to the tank.
2. **Float and Tape (Float-Operated)**
3. **Dip Stick / Dip Tape / Plumb Bob**
4. **Conductance / Resistance Probe (Conductivity-based, for conductive liquids)**

### B. Indirect Type Methods
1. **Hydrostatic Pressure / Differential Pressure (DP) Method**
2. **Buoyancy Method (Displacer-based)**
3. **Capacitance Method**
4. **Ultrasonic Method**
5. **Radar (Microwave) Method**
6. **Radiation (Nuclear) Method**
7. **Weight / Load Cell Method**
8. **Vibrating Fork / Tuning Fork Method** (mostly for solids)

## 2.2 Working Principle of Each Method (Step-by-Step Logic)

### 2.2.1 Direct Methods

**1. Sight Glass (Gauge Glass)**
- **Why:** Provides a direct, real-time visual reading.
- **How:** A transparent glass (or reinforced plastic) tube is connected to the top and bottom of the tank via two isolation valves and a safety ball check. By the principle of communicating vessels, the liquid level inside the glass equals the level inside the tank.
- **Utility:** Used on boiler drums, condensate tanks, day tanks in pharmaceutical plants.

**2. Float and Tape**
- **Why:** Converts vertical float motion into a rotary/electrical output.
- **How:** A hollow metallic float rides on the liquid surface. A flexible tape or chain connects the float to a torque tube or a pulley. As the float rises/falls, the tape rotates the pulley, and a precision potentiometer or LVDT converts angular displacement to a voltage/current signal (typically 4–20 mA).
- **Utility:** Open or closed atmospheric tanks of medium-to-large size.

**3. Dip Stick / Dip Tape**
- **Why:** Manual, zero-cost, no power required.
- **How:** A graduated metal rod (or chemical-indicator tape) is lowered until it touches the tank bottom; the wetted length indicates the level.
- **Utility:** Truck tanks, railway tankers, fuel storage farms (calibration check only).

**4. Conductance (Conductivity) Probe**
- **Why:** Exploits the electrical conductivity of liquids for binary/continuous level detection.
- **How:** Two electrodes are immersed in the liquid. When the liquid bridges the electrodes, current flows; its magnitude depends on the immersed electrode length. For point detection, a relay is energized.
- **Utility:** Only for **conductive liquids** (water, acids, brine). Cannot be used for oils or hydrocarbons.

### 2.2.2 Indirect Methods (Detailed)

**1. Hydrostatic / Differential Pressure Method**
- **Why:** The most widely installed method in process industries.
- **How:** The pressure at the bottom of a liquid column is directly proportional to its height: **P = ρgh**. By tapping this pressure via a DP transmitter, the height is inferred.
- **Utility:** Universal in closed pressurized vessels (chemical reactors, oil separators).

**2. Buoyancy Method (Displacer-based)**
- **Why:** Particularly suited for **interface measurement** (oil–water, acid–base) and for dense liquids.
- **How:** A displacer of fixed volume V_d and density ρ_d is suspended in the liquid. The buoyant force F_b = ρ_liquid × g × V_displaced. As the liquid level rises around the displacer, the apparent weight decreases. A torque tube or load cell converts this change in force into an electrical signal.
- **Utility:** Interface detection, slurry tanks, agitator vessels.

**3. Capacitance Method**
- **Why:** Works on **both conductive and non-conductive** liquids, no moving parts.
- **How:** The tank wall (or a second concentric rod) and a probe form the two plates of a cylindrical capacitor. The dielectric between them changes as the liquid level rises (air ε_r ≈ 1, liquid ε_r ≈ 2–80). The capacitance C = (2πε₀ε_r L) / ln(R₂/R₁) is a linear function of the immersed length L.
- **Utility:** Cryogenic tanks, corrosive chemicals, liquid gases.

**4. Ultrasonic Method**
- **Why:** Non-contact, suitable for corrosive, viscous, or foaming liquids.
- **How:** A piezo-electric transducer emits a 20–200 kHz pulse; the time-of-flight t for the echo to return from the liquid surface is measured. Height h = (c × t) / 2, where c is the speed of sound in air.
- **Utility:** Municipal water tanks, chemical sumps, open-channel flow.

**5. Radar (Microwave) Method**
- **Why:** Immune to temperature, pressure, dust, foam, and vapours.
- **How:** Same time-of-flight principle as ultrasonic, but using 6–26 GHz electromagnetic waves (FMCW or pulsed). Accuracy: ±1 mm.
- **Utility:** Petroleum storage tanks (custody transfer), LNG carriers.

**6. Radiation (Nuclear) Method**
- **Why:** Works through **any** vessel wall (metal, concrete, glass) without penetration.
- **How:** A radioactive source (Cs-137 or Co-60) on one side emits gamma rays; a detector on the opposite side measures the transmitted intensity, which is exponentially attenuated by the material in between. I = I₀ exp(–μρh).
- **Utility:** Extremely hostile environments: molten metal, cement kilns, blast furnaces.

**7. Weight / Load Cell Method**
- **Why:** Ideal for very large, non-rigid, or irregularly shaped vessels.
- **How:** The entire vessel is mounted on precision strain-gauge load cells. The change in mass (Δm) divided by tank cross-section A gives height: h = Δm / (ρA).
- **Utility:** Bulk silos, ship tanks, reactor vessels.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The table below is essential for KTU 2024 ESE numerical problems. Memorize all variables, units, and sign conventions.

| # | Method | Governing Formula | Variables & Units | Conditions / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hydrostatic Pressure | $P = \rho \, g \, h$ | P: Pa, ρ: kg/m³, g: m/s², h: m | Open/closed vessels, single fluid |
| 2 | Differential Pressure (Dry Leg) | $\Delta P = P_{HP} - P_{LP} = \rho_l \, g \, h$ | ΔP in Pa | LP leg filled with reference gas only |
| 3 | Differential Pressure (Wet Leg) | $\Delta P = h (\rho_l - \rho_{ref}) g$ | ρ_l = liquid, ρ_ref = manometer/seal fluid | LP leg filled with heavier seal liquid |
| 4 | Buoyancy (Displacer) | $F_b = \rho_l \, g \, V_d$ | F_b: N, V_d: m³ | Apparent weight change = F_b |
| 5 | Capacitance (Coaxial Cylinder) | $C = \dfrac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r L}{\ln(R_2 / R_1)}$ | ε₀ = 8.854×10⁻¹² F/m, L: immersed length | When L < total probe length, C varies linearly with L |
| 6 | Capacitance (Two-Wire Probe) | $C = \dfrac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r L}{\ln(2s/d)}$ | s = spacing, d = wire diameter | Used for non-conductive liquids in non-metallic tanks |
| 7 | Ultrasonic (Time of Flight) | $h = \dfrac{c \cdot t}{2}$ | c ≈ 343 m/s in air at 20 °C | t = round-trip travel time |
| 8 | Radar (FMCW) | $\Delta f = \dfrac{2 \cdot S \cdot h}{c}$ | S = frequency sweep rate, c = speed of light | Beat frequency proportional to h |
| 9 | Radiation (Beer–Lambert) | $I = I_0 \, e^{-\mu \rho h}$ | μ: mass attenuation coeff. m²/kg | Exponential; highly nonlinear |
| 10 | Weight / Load Cell | $h = \dfrac{\Delta m}{\rho \, A}$ | Δm: kg, A: m² | Requires uniform cross-section |
| 11 | Conductance (Continuous) | $G = \dfrac{k \cdot \sigma \cdot L}{d}$ | σ: conductivity S/m, k: cell constant | L = immersed electrode length |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Sign Convention for KTU DP Problems:** The **High-Pressure (HP) tap** of the DP transmitter is *always* at the bottom of the tank (the liquid side). The **Low-Pressure (LP) tap** is at the top (vapour/seal-fluid side). Always state this explicitly in your answer to earn full marks.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The **Differential Pressure Method** is overwhelmingly preferred for closed pressurized vessels because:
- It is intrinsically safe when paired with **chemical seals / diaphragm seals** (e.g., Rosemount 3051S).
- It tolerates corrosive, hot, viscous, and slurry media by isolating the transmitter via a capillary-filled seal fluid.
- It provides a fully **continuous 4–20 mA HART signal** integrable into any DCS/PLC.

**Solid Level Detectors** (covered separately in Section 2.5 below) are dominated by **radar, ultrasonic, capacitance, and weight/load-cell** methods because mechanical contact methods wear out rapidly in abrasive bulk solids (cement, coal, grain, plastic pellets).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Hydrostatic Pressure Equation

We start with Newton's second law applied to a vertical column of liquid of constant density ρ, cross-sectional area A, and height h.

**Step 1: Compute the mass of the column.**

$$m = \rho \cdot V = \rho \cdot A \cdot h$$

**Step 2: Compute the weight (force) exerted by this column.**

$$F = m \cdot g = \rho \cdot A \cdot h \cdot g$$

**Step 3: This weight is supported by the pressure at the base acting over area A.**

$$P \cdot A = F = \rho \cdot A \cdot h \cdot g$$

**Step 4: Cancel A on both sides (since A ≠ 0).**

$$P = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$$

**Step 5: If the vessel is open to atmosphere, an additional atmospheric pressure P_atm acts on the surface, making the absolute pressure at the bottom:**

$$P_{abs} = P_{atm} + \rho \cdot g \cdot h$$

**Step 6: If the vessel is closed and pressurized, the gas/vapour above the liquid exerts P_tank:**

$$P_{abs} = P_{tank} + \rho \cdot g \cdot h$$

> This is the foundational equation upon which the entire Differential Pressure Method rests.

## 3.2 Derivation for Pressurized Vessel — Dry Reference Leg (Most Common Case)

**Setup:** A closed cylindrical tank contains a liquid of density ρ_l. Above the liquid is a pressurized gas/vapour at pressure P_tank. A DP transmitter is mounted below the tank.

**Step 1: High-Pressure (HP) side measurement.**
The HP tap is connected to the bottom of the tank. The pressure it senses is the tank gas pressure plus the hydrostatic head of the liquid column:

$$P_{HP} = P_{tank} + \rho_l \cdot g \cdot h$$

**Step 2: Low-Pressure (LP) side measurement.**
The LP tap is connected to the gas space at the top of the tank (or the equalizing line above the liquid). For a *dry* reference leg, the impulse line is filled with the tank gas only, so:

$$P_{LP} = P_{tank}$$

**Step 3: Differential pressure output.**

$$\Delta P = P_{HP} - P_{LP}$$

**Step 4: Substitute and simplify.**

$$\Delta P = (P_{tank} + \rho_l \cdot g \cdot h) - P_{tank}$$

**Step 5: Final result — the tank pressure cancels out.**

$$\boxed{\Delta P = \rho_l \cdot g \cdot h}$$

**Step 6: Solve explicitly for level h.**

$$h = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\rho_l \cdot g}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This cancellation of P_tank is the **elegant advantage** of the DP method: it inherently compensates for variations in tank pressure, making the reading a function of *level only*.

## 3.3 Derivation for Pressurized Vessel — Wet Reference Leg (Seal Fluid Case)

Used when the process fluid is **hot, corrosive, or tends to solidify** in the impulse line. A heavier, inert **seal fluid** (silicone oil, glycerine, halocarbon) is pre-filled in the LP impulse line.

**Step 1: HP side.** Same as before.

$$P_{HP} = P_{tank} + \rho_l \cdot g \cdot h$$

**Step 2: LP side.** The LP line is filled with seal fluid of density ρ_s up to a height H (total tank height from transmitter to top tap). So:

$$P_{LP} = P_{tank} + \rho_s \cdot g \cdot (H - h)$$

**Step 3: Differential pressure.**

$$\Delta P = P_{HP} - P_{LP} = [P_{tank} + \rho_l g h] - [P_{tank} + \rho_s g (H - h)]$$

**Step 4: Cancel P_tank and expand.**

$$\Delta P = \rho_l g h - \rho_s g H + \rho_s g h$$

**Step 5: Group the terms containing h.**

$$\Delta P = h \, g \, (\rho_l - \rho_s) - \rho_s \, g \, H$$

**Step 6: Solve for h (the level).**

$$\boxed{h = \dfrac{\Delta P + \rho_s \, g \, H}{g \, (\rho_l - \rho_s)}}$$

> The negative suppression term (–ρ_s g H) is called the **zero elevation / zero suppression** and must be configured into the DP transmitter for accurate readings.

## 3.4 Derivation of the Capacitance Equation (Coaxial Cylindrical Probe)

**Step 1: Geometry.** A metal rod of outer radius R₁ (with a thin insulating sheath of negligible thickness) is suspended vertically inside a metal tank of inner radius R₂. The liquid of relative permittivity ε_r rises to a height h along the probe.

**Step 2: Standard cylindrical capacitor formula** for length L:

$$C = \dfrac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r L}{\ln(R_2 / R_1)}$$

**Step 3: For partially immersed probe**, the total capacitance is the sum of two parallel capacitors:
- **Air portion (length H − h):** $C_1 = \dfrac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0 \cdot 1 \cdot (H - h)}{\ln(R_2 / R_1)}$
- **Liquid portion (length h):** $C_2 = \dfrac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r h}{\ln(R_2 / R_1)}$

**Step 4: Add the two parallel capacitances.**

$$C_{total} = C_1 + C_2 = \dfrac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0}{\ln(R_2 / R_1)} \cdot [H - h + \varepsilon_r h]$$

**Step 5: Expand the bracket.**

$$C_{total} = \dfrac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0}{\ln(R_2 / R_1)} \cdot [H + h(\varepsilon_r - 1)]$$

**Step 6: Solve for h.**

$$\boxed{h = \dfrac{C_{total} \cdot \ln(R_2/R_1) - 2 \pi \varepsilon_0 H}{2 \pi \varepsilon_0 (\varepsilon_r - 1)}}$$

## 3.5 Worked-Out Numerical Example (KTU ESE Style)

**Problem:** A closed cylindrical storage tank of height 6 m contains gasoline (ρ = 740 kg/m³) under a nitrogen blanket at 1.4 bar (gauge). A DP transmitter is installed with HP tap at the bottom and LP tap connected to the vapour space. The transmitter reads 32.5 kPa. Calculate the liquid level.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify method → Dry reference leg, so use $\Delta P = \rho_l \, g \, h$.

**Step 2:** Solve for h.

$$h = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\rho_l \cdot g}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute.

$$h = \dfrac{32.5 \times 10^3}{740 \times 9.81}$$

**Step 4:** Numerator.

$$32.5 \times 10^3 = 32500 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 5:** Denominator.

$$740 \times 9.81 = 7259.4 \text{ Pa/m}$$

**Step 6:** Final division.

$$h = \dfrac{32500}{7259.4} = 4.477 \text{ m} \approx 4.48 \text{ m}$$

**Answer:** The gasoline level is **4.48 m** (≈ 74.6% full).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Note that the tank pressure of 1.4 bar (gauge) is **irrelevant** to the calculation because of the cancellation demonstrated in Section 3.2. This is a very common KTU question pattern.

## 3.6 Buoyancy Method — Displacer Force Equation

A displacer of volume V_d and density ρ_d is suspended in a liquid of density ρ_l.

**Step 1: Weight of displacer in air.**

$$W_{air} = \rho_d \cdot V_d \cdot g$$

**Step 2: Buoyant force from displaced liquid.**

$$F_b = \rho_l \cdot V_d \cdot g$$

**Step 3: Apparent weight in liquid (Archimedes).**

$$W_{apparent} = W_{air} - F_b = (\rho_d - \rho_l) \cdot V_d \cdot g$$

**Step 4: When the liquid level rises from h₁ to h₂**, the displaced volume changes by ΔV = A_d × Δh, where A_d is the cross-sectional area of the displacer. The change in apparent weight is:

$$\Delta W = (\rho_d - \rho_l) \cdot A_d \cdot \Delta h \cdot g$$

**Step 5: Therefore, change in level:**

$$\Delta h = \dfrac{\Delta W}{(\rho_d - \rho_l) \cdot A_d \cdot g}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Flowchart of Level Measurement Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Level Measurement] --> B[Direct Type]
    A --> C[Indirect Type]
    A --> D[Solid Level Detectors]

    B --> B1[Sight Glass]
    B --> B2[Float and Tape]
    B --> B3[Dip Stick / Tape]
    B --> B4[Conductance Probe]

    C --> C1[Hydrostatic / DP Method]
    C --> C2[Buoyancy / Displacer]
    C --> C3[Capacitance Probe]
    C --> C4[Ultrasonic]
    C --> C5[Radar Microwave]
    C --> C6[Radiation Nuclear]
    C --> C7[Weight / Load Cell]

    D --> D1[Ultrasonic]
    D --> D2[Capacitance]
    D --> D3[Radar]
    D --> D4[Weight Load Cell]
    D --> D5[Vibrating Fork]
    D --> D6[Radiation]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — DP Transmitter on a Pressurized Vessel (Dry Leg)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PRESS_TANK[Pressurized Tank with Liquid] --> HP_TAP[HP Tap Bottom]
    PRESS_TANK --> LP_TAP[LP Tap Top Vapor Space]
    HP_TAP --> IMP1[Impulse Line Liquid Filled]
    LP_TAP --> IMP2[Impulse Line Vapor Filled]
    IMP1 --> DPT[DP Transducer Capsule]
    IMP2 --> DPT
    DPT --> SIG_COND[Signal Conditioner and ADC]
    SIG_COND --> MCU[Microcontroller with Linearization]
    MCU --> HART[4 to 20 mA HART Output]
    MCU --> DISP[Local LCD Display]
    HART --> DCS[Plant DCS SCADA]
    SIG_COND --> TEMP[RTD Temperature Input for Density Compensation]
    TEMP --> MCU
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Capacitance Level Measurement Setup

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TANK[Non Metallic or Metallic Tank] --> PROBE[Vertical Capacitance Probe]
    PROBE --> CTANK[Tank Wall as Second Plate for Metallic Tank]
    LC[LC Oscillator Circuit] --> PROBE
    LC --> FREQ2[Frequency to Voltage Converter]
    FREQ2 --> MCU[Microcontroller with Calibration]
    MCU --> OUTPUT[4 to 20 mA Analog Output]
    MCU --> DISP2[Level Display Percentage]
```

## 4.4 Process Flow for Solid Level Detector Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Solid Material in Silo] --> Q1{Powder or Granular Size}
    Q1 -->|Fine Powder| Q2{Dust Present}
    Q1 -->|Large Granules| Q3[Use Radar or Weight Method]
    Q2 -->|Yes| R1[Use Radar FMCW 26 GHz]
    Q2 -->|No| R2[Use Ultrasonic or Capacitance]
    R1 --> OUT1[Output 4 to 20 mA]
    R2 --> OUT1
    R3[Use Vibrating Fork for Point Detection] --> OUT1
    Q3 --> OUT1
    R1 --> RT[Use Radiation if Vessel Wall too Thick]
    RT --> OUT1
```
