<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define level measurement. Differentiate between direct and indirect methods with two examples each.**

**Model Answer:**
Level measurement is the determination of the position of the free surface of a material relative to a reference datum inside a vessel.

- **Direct methods** provide a visible or mechanical reading of height, e.g., (i) sight glass, (ii) dip stick.
- **Indirect methods** infer level from a secondary physical variable, e.g., (i) differential pressure method, (ii) capacitance probe.

> Direct methods are simple and accurate but require human intervention; indirect methods are automated, suitable for hazardous and remote applications. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**List any four indirect type level measurement methods and state the principle of operation of the capacitance method.**

**Model Answer:**
Four indirect methods: (i) Differential Pressure, (ii) Buoyancy / Displacer, (iii) Ultrasonic, (iv) Radar.

**Principle of Capacitance Method:**
The probe and tank wall (or two concentric rods) form a cylindrical capacitor. As the liquid level rises, the dielectric between the plates changes from air (ε_r ≈ 1) to the liquid (ε_r ≈ 2–80). This change in capacitance is measured and converted to a 4–20 mA signal proportional to the immersed length. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A — Differential Pressure Method (Comprehensive)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the working of the **Differential Pressure (DP) Method** for level measurement in a **pressurized closed tank** with a **dry reference leg**. Derive the relationship between differential pressure and level. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A closed tank of diameter 3 m and height 8 m contains oil of density 860 kg/m³. The space above the oil is pressurized at 2 bar (gauge). A DP transmitter with HP tap at the bottom and LP tap at the top reads 38 kPa. Calculate:
(i) The level of oil in metres.
(ii) The volume of oil in litres. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A (a)

**Step 1:** Draw the schematic (refer Section 4.2 above). The tank is closed and pressurized. The HP tap is at the bottom of the tank, connected to the lower flange of the DP transmitter. The LP tap is at the top, connected to the upper flange via a dry impulse line containing the tank gas.

**Step 2:** Identify the pressures:
- HP side: $P_{HP} = P_{tank} + \rho_l \, g \, h$
- LP side: $P_{LP} = P_{tank}$ (dry leg assumption)

**Step 3:** Compute ΔP.

$$\Delta P = P_{HP} - P_{LP} = \rho_l \, g \, h$$

**Step 4:** Solve for level.

$$h = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\rho_l \, g}$$

**Step 5:** The tank pressure P_tank cancels, which is the principal advantage of the DP method. The transmitter is calibrated in units of length by entering the density and g.

#### Model Solution to Q-A (b)

**Part (i) — Level Calculation**

Given: ρ_l = 860 kg/m³, g = 9.81 m/s², ΔP = 38 kPa = 38000 Pa, Diameter D = 3 m, H = 8 m.

$$h = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\rho_l \cdot g} = \dfrac{38000}{860 \times 9.81}$$

$$h = \dfrac{38000}{8436.6} = 4.504 \text{ m} \approx 4.50 \text{ m}$$

**Part (ii) — Volume Calculation**

Cross-sectional area:

$$A = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \times 3^2}{4} = 7.0686 \text{ m}^2$$

Volume in m³:

$$V = A \times h = 7.0686 \times 4.504 = 31.84 \text{ m}^3$$

Volume in litres (1 m³ = 1000 L):

$$V = 31840 \text{ L} \approx 31840 \text{ litres}$$

**Final Answers:** (i) h = **4.50 m**; (ii) V = **31,840 litres**.

---

### Question B — Capacitance Method (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a diagram, explain the **Capacitance Method** of level measurement for a non-conductive liquid in a non-metallic tank. Derive the relation between the level and the measured capacitance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A capacitance probe of length 2 m is suspended inside a non-metallic tank of radius 1 m. The probe is a cylindrical rod of radius 5 mm. The liquid has a relative permittivity of 4. When the liquid level is at 1.2 m, calculate:
(i) The total capacitance of the system.
(ii) The capacitance if the level drops to 0.6 m. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B (a)

**Step 1:** For a non-metallic tank, two parallel rods (or one rod + reference tube) act as the two capacitor plates. The space between them, filled partly with air and partly with liquid, forms the dielectric.

**Step 2:** Capacitance is measured using an AC bridge or oscillator whose frequency depends on C. The change in C is directly proportional to the immersed length of the probe.

**Step 3:** Derivation (as in Section 3.4):

$$C = \dfrac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0}{\ln(R_2 / R_1)} \cdot [H + h(\varepsilon_r - 1)]$$

**Step 4:** The relationship between C and h is **linear**, which is the chief advantage of the capacitance method.

**Step 5:** For conductive liquids, the rod is coated with an insulating sheath (PTFE), and the liquid itself becomes the second "grounded" plate.

#### Model Solution to Q-B (b)

**Given:** L = 2 m, R₁ = 5 mm = 0.005 m, R₂ = 1 m (tank radius), ε_r = 4, h = 1.2 m, ε₀ = 8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m.

**Part (i): C at h = 1.2 m**

Step 1: Geometric constant.

$$K = \dfrac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0}{\ln(R_2 / R_1)} = \dfrac{2 \pi \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12}}{\ln(1 / 0.005)}$$

$$\ln(200) = 5.2983$$

$$K = \dfrac{5.562 \times 10^{-11}}{5.2983} = 1.0499 \times 10^{-11} \text{ F/m}$$

Step 2: Effective length term.

$$L_{eff} = H + h(\varepsilon_r - 1) = 2 + 1.2 \times (4 - 1) = 2 + 3.6 = 5.6 \text{ m}$$

Step 3: Total capacitance.

$$C = K \times L_{eff} = 1.0499 \times 10^{-11} \times 5.6 = 5.879 \times 10^{-11} \text{ F} \approx 58.8 \text{ pF}$$

**Part (ii): C at h = 0.6 m**

Step 1: New effective length.

$$L_{eff} = 2 + 0.6 \times 3 = 2 + 1.8 = 3.8 \text{ m}$$

Step 2: New capacitance.

$$C = 1.0499 \times 10^{-11} \times 3.8 = 3.990 \times 10^{-11} \text{ F} \approx 39.9 \text{ pF}$$

**Final Answers:** (i) C = **58.8 pF**; (ii) C = **39.9 pF**.

---

## 5.3 Solid Level Detector — Long Answer (Part B, 14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Question:** With a neat block diagram, explain the **working principle of an Ultrasonic Level Detector** for solid (bulk) materials in a silo. Discuss its **advantages and limitations** compared to a **Radar (Microwave) Level Detector**. State any **four** industrial applications of solid level measurement. **(14 marks)**

### Model Solution Outline (Sketch Only — Examiner's Note)

**Block Diagram (mental sketch):**
Silo top → Ultrasonic Transducer (Tx/Rx) → Pulse Generator → Time-to-Voltage Converter → Microcontroller → 4–20 mA Output → DCS.

**Working Principle:**
A piezo-electric crystal emits a 20–80 kHz pulse vertically downward. The pulse reflects off the bulk-solid surface. The round-trip time t is measured; height h = (c × t) / 2, where c = speed of sound in air.

**Advantages of Ultrasonic:**
- Non-contact, no wear.
- Low cost.
- Easy to install on open silos.

**Limitations:**
- Affected by dust, foam, temperature gradients, and vapours (changes c).
- Cannot penetrate thick walls or work in vacuum.

**Advantages of Radar:**
- Immune to dust, foam, temperature, pressure.
- Microwave penetrates non-conductive dust.
- Accuracy ±1 mm (FMCW).

**Limitations of Radar:**
- High initial cost.
- Requires expert commissioning.
- Stilling well needed for high-accuracy applications.

**Four Applications:**
(i) Cement silos. (ii) Coal bunkers in thermal power plants. (iii) Grain storage silos. (iv) Plastic pellet hoppers.

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students most commonly lose marks on Level Measurement problems (KTU 2024 pattern):**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Forgetting to convert kPa to Pa, or mm to m. Marks are deducted in a stepped fashion (up to 1 mark).
> 2. **Sign Convention Errors:** In a *wet* reference leg problem, students often forget to subtract ρ_s g H. This produces a wrong zero and is heavily penalized (up to 2 marks).
> 3. **Forgetting to Cancel P_tank:** When a problem gives a tank pressure, students often try to *add* it to ΔP, giving wrong answers. Always remember: for a **dry** leg, P_tank cancels out.
> 4. **Confusing Methods:** Writing about sight glass when the question asks for radar. Read the question carefully!
> 5. **Skipping the Sketch:** KTU examiners expect a "neat sketch" worth 1–2 marks in every 14-mark question. Always draw one — even a rough one.
> 6. **Wrong Density Assumption:** For oil/water/mercury, density values must be taken from a standard table. State the assumed value in the answer.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (must memorize for KTU ESE):**
>
> **1. Classification:**
> - Direct: sight glass, float, dip stick, conductance.
> - Indirect: DP, buoyancy, capacitance, ultrasonic, radar, radiation, weight.
>
> **2. Key Equations (must know verbatim):**
> - Hydrostatic: $P = \rho g h$
> - DP Dry Leg: $\Delta P = \rho_l g h$
> - DP Wet Leg: $\Delta P = h g (\rho_l - \rho_s) - \rho_s g H$
> - Capacitance: $C = \dfrac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r L}{\ln(R_2/R_1)}$
> - Buoyancy: $F_b = \rho_l g V_d$
> - Ultrasonic: $h = ct/2$
> - Radar: $\Delta f = 2Sh/c$
> - Radiation: $I = I_0 e^{-\mu \rho h}$
>
> **3. Standard Values:**
> - g = 9.81 m/s²
> - ε₀ = 8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m
> - Speed of sound in air @ 20 °C ≈ 343 m/s
> - Speed of light = 3 × 10⁸ m/s
>
> **4. Method Selection Rules:**
> - **Open tank, cheap, manual** → sight glass or dip stick.
> - **Closed pressurized tank** → DP transmitter with chemical seals.
> - **Corrosive/cryogenic** → Capacitance probe.
> - **Interface (oil–water)** → Buoyancy / displacer.
> - **Foaming, dusty, vapours** → Radar (FMCW).
> - **Extreme conditions (molten metal)** → Radiation.
> - **Hazardous area, no penetration** → Ultrasonic or radar.
>
> **5. Solid Level Detectors — most preferred in industry (rank order):**
> 1. Radar (FMCW, 80 GHz)
> 2. Ultrasonic
> 3. Capacitance
> 4. Load cell (weight)
> 5. Vibrating fork (point detection)
> 6. Radiation (last resort, hazardous source)
>
> **6. Common Mistake to Avoid:** Never confuse **level** (m) with **volume** (m³) or **mass** (kg). Always check what the question is asking and convert dimensions correctly.
>
> **7. Examiners Love These Keywords:** "Archimedes' Principle", "communicating vessels", "communicating-vessel principle", "Beer–Lambert Law", "time-of-flight", "FMCW", "HART protocol", "4–20 mA two-wire loop", "chemical seal / diaphragm seal", "zero suppression / zero elevation", "intrinsically safe", "CIP/SIP compatibility" — use these terms liberally in your theory answers to score above-average marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Moisture Content and Humidity: Definition, Moisture Content Determination by Thermal Drying
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT724 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Moisture Content and Humidity: Definition, Moisture Content Determination by Thermal Drying

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition — Moisture Content

**Moisture Content (MC)** is formally defined as the **quantity of water (or any volatile matter) present in a solid, liquid, or gaseous material**, expressed as a ratio of the mass of water removed to the mass of the original (wet) sample or the mass of the dry sample.

Mathematically, it is the **Loss On Drying (LOD)** per unit mass, and it is reported either on a **wet basis (w.b.)** or a **dry basis (d.b.)**, depending on the industrial convention (food, pharma, paper, textiles, cement, and chemical industries all use slightly different standards).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (verbatim expected in 2-mark questions):**
> "Moisture content is the amount of water present in a substance, expressed as a percentage of the original wet mass (wet basis) or of the bone-dry mass (dry basis), determined experimentally by measuring the mass loss of the sample when subjected to a controlled thermal drying process until a constant mass is achieved."

### 1.2 Formal Definition — Humidity

**Humidity** is the **concentration of water vapour present in a gas mixture (most commonly air)**. It is a critical process variable in HVAC, drying ovens, environmental chambers, compressed-air systems, and process industries.

The four universally-used measures of humidity in instrumentation are:

1. **Absolute Humidity (AH)** — Mass of water vapour per unit volume of the air-vapour mixture ($\text{g/m}^3$).
2. **Specific Humidity (SH)** / **Humidity Ratio (W)** — Mass of water vapour per unit mass of dry air ($\text{kg/kg of dry air}$).
3. **Relative Humidity (RH)** — Ratio of the actual partial pressure of water vapour to its saturation pressure at the same temperature, expressed as a percentage.
4. **Dew Point Temperature ($T_{dp}$)** — The temperature at which the air becomes saturated when cooled at constant pressure; the water vapour begins to condense.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Sponge vs. Air" Analogy**
>
> Imagine a **kitchen sponge** sitting in a bucket of water.
> - The water soaked inside the sponge is its **moisture content** — a property of the *material itself*.
> - Now imagine the **air in your bathroom after a hot shower**. It feels heavy and sticky because of the invisible water molecules floating around in it. That is its **humidity** — a property of the *gas phase*.
>
> In an industry, you might be drying a wet cake of pharmaceutical granules in an oven (measuring MC of the solid), while simultaneously controlling the humidity of the hot air flowing over the cake (so it absorbs moisture without over-drying). That is why the same instrument family (moisture/humidity analyzers) sits inside Module 4 of this course.

### 1.4 Why Both Are Grouped in the Same Module

| Domain | Variable | Typical Range | Why It Matters |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Solids** | Moisture Content | 0.1 % to 90 % | Product quality, shelf life, weight-based pricing |
| **Gases** | Humidity | 0 % to 100 % RH | Process control, corrosion prevention, drying efficiency |

### 1.5 Key Standard Metrics and Constants

- **Standard atmospheric pressure:** $P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$
- **Universal gas constant:** $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$
- **Molecular weight of water:** $M_{w} = 18.015 \text{ g/mol}$
- **Molecular weight of dry air:** $M_{a} = 28.97 \text{ g/mol}$
- **Reference drying temperature (chemical/pharma):** $105 \text{ °C}$
- **Reference drying temperature (food/agriculture):** $130 \text{ °C}$ (or $103 \text{ °C}$ for grain per ISO 712)
- **Reference drying temperature (cement/soil):** $110 \text{ °C}$

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners **love** asking for the *standard drying temperature* and *standard drying time* used for thermal drying. Memorise **105 °C to constant mass** as the generic "textbook answer".

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Moisture Content — Wet Basis vs. Dry Basis

**Step 1 — Define the masses:**
- $m_i$ = initial mass of the wet sample (g)
- $m_f$ = final mass of the sample after complete drying (g)
- $m_w = m_i - m_f$ = mass of water driven off (g)

**Step 2 — Apply the definitions:**

**Wet-basis moisture content (most common in industry):**
$$
MC_{w.b.} = \frac{m_i - m_f}{m_i} \times 100 \; (\%)
$$

**Dry-basis moisture content (preferred in drying engineering because it is additive in series dryers):**
$$
MC_{d.b.} = \frac{m_i - m_f}{m_f} \times 100 \; (\%)
$$

**Step 3 — Why two definitions exist:**
- Wet-basis is bounded between 0 % and 100 % — easy for QC staff to interpret.
- Dry-basis is unbounded (can exceed 100 %) — used by drying engineers for mass-balance calculations.

**Conversion between the two:**
$$
MC_{d.b.} = \frac{MC_{w.b.}}{1 - MC_{w.b.}} \quad ; \quad MC_{w.b.} = \frac{MC_{d.b.}}{1 + MC_{d.b.}}
$$

### 2.2 Humidity — The Four Quantities Explained

**1. Absolute Humidity (AH):**
$$
AH = \frac{m_{v}}{V} \quad [\text{g/m}^3]
$$
where $m_v$ is the mass of water vapour in volume $V$ of the moist air.

**2. Specific Humidity / Humidity Ratio (W):**
$$
W = \frac{m_{v}}{m_{a}} = 0.622 \, \frac{P_v}{P_{atm} - P_v} \quad [\text{kg water / kg dry air}]
$$
The constant **0.622** comes from $M_w / M_a = 18.015 / 28.97$.

**3. Relative Humidity (RH):**
$$
RH = \frac{P_v}{P_{g}(T)} \times 100 \; (\%)
$$
where $P_v$ is the actual partial pressure of water vapour, and $P_g(T)$ is the saturation vapour pressure at the same temperature $T$.

**4. Dew Point Temperature ($T_{dp}$):**
The temperature at which $P_v$ equals $P_g(T_{dp})$ — i.e., condensation begins. Below $T_{dp}$, the air is supersaturated.

### 2.3 The Principle of Thermal Drying (Loss-On-Drying, LOD)

**Operational Logic in Bulleted Steps:**

1. **Sampling** — A representative portion of the material is withdrawn and placed in a pre-weighed, dry, clean container (typically a **glass petri dish** or **aluminium moisture dish**).
2. **Initial weighing** — Mass $m_i$ of the wet sample + container is recorded on an **analytical balance** (resolution $\geq 0.1$ mg).
3. **Thermal exposure** — The sample is placed inside a **drying oven** held at a controlled temperature (most commonly **105 °C ± 2 °C** for general organics; **130 °C** for food).
4. **Equilibration** — Hot, dry air (low RH) sweeps over the sample, raising the water activity at the surface and driving evaporation. The oven is usually equipped with a **forced-convection fan** for uniform heat transfer.
5. **Intermittent weighing** — After a defined time (typically 1 h, 2 h, 3 h), the sample is removed, cooled in a **desiccator** (to prevent re-absorption of moisture from ambient air), and reweighed.
6. **Constant mass criterion** — Drying is continued until the difference between two successive weighings (separated by 1 h of additional heating) is **less than 0.1 % (or 0.5 mg, whichever is greater)**. This guarantees the sample is *bone-dry*.
7. **Final weighing** — $m_f$ is recorded. The mass loss = mass of moisture removed.
8. **Calculation** — Apply the wet-basis or dry-basis formula to express MC as a percentage.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a desiccator is mandatory:** A hot sample placed on a balance in ambient air will **re-absorb moisture in seconds** and yield an artificially high dry mass. Cooling in a sealed desiccator containing silica gel preserves the true $m_f$.

### 2.4 Types of Thermal Drying Ovens Used in Industry

| Oven Type | Drying Mechanism | Typical Use | Drying Time |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Convection (forced-air) oven** | Hot air blown over sample | General laboratory LOD | 2 – 24 h |
| **Vacuum oven** | Reduced pressure lowers boiling point of water | Heat-sensitive pharma/food | 4 – 12 h |
| **Infrared (IR) moisture balance** | IR radiation heats sample from above | Rapid QC, on-line | 5 – 30 min |
| **Microwave moisture analyser** | Dielectric heating of water molecules | Very fast, low-temperature | 2 – 10 min |
| **Halogen moisture analyser** | Halogen lamp (fast IR) | Most common modern balance | 3 – 15 min |

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | SI Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Moisture content (wet basis) | $MC_{w.b.} = \dfrac{m_i - m_f}{m_i} \times 100$ | % | Most common industrial report |
| 2 | Moisture content (dry basis) | $MC_{d.b.} = \dfrac{m_i - m_f}{m_f} \times 100$ | % | Used in drying engineering |
| 3 | Conversion w.b. → d.b. | $MC_{d.b.} = \dfrac{MC_{w.b.}}{1 - MC_{w.b.}}$ | dimensionless | |
| 4 | Conversion d.b. → w.b. | $MC_{w.b.} = \dfrac{MC_{d.b.}}{1 + MC_{d.b.}}$ | dimensionless | |
| 5 | Mass of water evaporated | $m_w = m_i - m_f$ | g | The "loss on drying" |
| 6 | Humidity ratio (specific humidity) | $W = 0.622 \, \dfrac{P_v}{P_{atm} - P_v}$ | kg/kg | Use $P_{atm}$ in same units as $P_v$ |
| 7 | Absolute humidity | $AH = \dfrac{m_v}{V}$ | g/m³ | |
| 8 | Relative humidity | $RH = \dfrac{P_v}{P_g(T)} \times 100$ | % | Function of temperature |
| 9 | Saturation vapour pressure (Antoine eqn.) | $\log_{10} P_g = A - \dfrac{B}{C+T}$ | mmHg or bar | A, B, C are substance-specific |
| 10 | Dew-point relationship | $P_v = P_g(T_{dp})$ | Pa | Defines dew point |
| 11 | Mass-balance on a dryer | $\dot{m}_s (X_{in} - X_{out}) = \dot{m}_a (Y_{out} - Y_{in})$ | kg/s | Solids moisture X, gas humidity Y |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Pitfall:** The factor **0.622** appears in the humidity-ratio formula. Examiners often write it as **0.622, 0.66, or 0.63** — all are approximations. Use the exact **$M_w / M_a = 18.015 / 28.97 = 0.622$** unless the question explicitly states otherwise.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pharmaceutical industry** — Moisture in tablet granules must be controlled to within ±0.5 % to ensure proper compression and shelf stability. USP <731> prescribes LOD at 105 °C.
- **Food industry** — Dried milk powder, instant coffee, and cereals are graded and priced on moisture content. Too wet → microbial spoilage; too dry → energy waste.
- **Cement and concrete** — Aggregate moisture affects water-cement ratio, hence compressive strength.
- **Paper industry** — Paper sheet moisture affects tensile strength, printability, and curl.
- **Power and compressed air** — Humidity in compressed-air lines must be measured to prevent condensation in pneumatic instruments and to size air dryers correctly.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Derivation 1 — Moisture Content from an LOD Experiment

**Given:**
- Empty dish mass = $m_d = 24.500 \text{ g}$
- Dish + wet sample mass = $m_d + m_i = 78.420 \text{ g}$
- Dish + dry sample (after constant mass) = $m_d + m_f = 73.180 \text{ g}$

**Find:** Moisture content in wet basis and dry basis.

**Step 1 — Extract the wet sample mass:**
$$
m_i = (m_d + m_i) - m_d = 78.420 - 24.500 = 53.920 \text{ g}
$$

**Step 2 — Extract the dry sample mass:**
$$
m_f = (m_d + m_f) - m_d = 73.180 - 24.500 = 48.680 \text{ g}
$$

**Step 3 — Mass of water evaporated:**
$$
m_w = m_i - m_f = 53.920 - 48.680 = 5.240 \text{ g}
$$

**Step 4 — Wet-basis moisture content:**
$$
MC_{w.b.} = \frac{m_i - m_f}{m_i} \times 100 = \frac{5.240}{53.920} \times 100 = 9.717 \,\%
$$

**Step 5 — Dry-basis moisture content:**
$$
MC_{d.b.} = \frac{m_i - m_f}{m_f} \times 100 = \frac{5.240}{48.680} \times 100 = 10.764 \,\%
$$

**Step 6 — Verify conversion formula:**
$$
MC_{d.b.} = \frac{MC_{w.b.}}{1 - MC_{w.b.}} = \frac{0.09717}{1 - 0.09717} = \frac{0.09717}{0.90283} = 0.1076 = 10.76 \,\% \;\; \checkmark
$$

**Step 7 — Verify reverse conversion:**
$$
MC_{w.b.} = \frac{MC_{d.b.}}{1 + MC_{d.b.}} = \frac{0.10764}{1.10764} = 0.09717 = 9.72 \,\% \;\; \checkmark
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The two results are **consistent** — this is a self-check the KTU examiner expects you to perform to gain full marks.

---

### 3.2 Worked Derivation 2 — Humidity Ratio from Partial Pressure Data

**Given:** Moist air at total pressure $P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$ has a water-vapour partial pressure $P_v = 4.246 \text{ kPa}$ at $T = 30 \text{ °C}$.

**Find:** (a) Humidity ratio $W$, (b) Absolute humidity $AH$ if the air is at 30 °C (use ideal gas law for water vapour; saturation pressure at 30 °C is $P_g = 4.246 \text{ kPa}$).

**Step 1 — Apply the humidity-ratio formula:**
$$
W = 0.622 \times \frac{P_v}{P_{atm} - P_v} = 0.622 \times \frac{4.246}{101.325 - 4.246}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the denominator:**
$$
P_{atm} - P_v = 101.325 - 4.246 = 97.079 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the ratio:**
$$
\frac{4.246}{97.079} = 0.04373
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by 0.622:**
$$
W = 0.622 \times 0.04373 = 0.02720 \text{ kg water / kg dry air}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to grams per kg of dry air (common engineering unit):**
$$
W = 27.20 \text{ g water / kg dry air}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the relative humidity:**
$$
RH = \frac{P_v}{P_g(T)} \times 100 = \frac{4.246}{4.246} \times 100 = 100 \,\%
$$

(Here the air is **saturated**, which is why the dew point equals the dry-bulb temperature of 30 °C.)

**Step 7 — Compute absolute humidity using ideal gas law:**

The vapour behaves as an ideal gas: $P_v V = n_{v} R T$ ⇒ mass $m_v = \dfrac{P_v M_w V}{R T}$.

For $V = 1 \text{ m}^3$, $T = 303.15 \text{ K}$, $P_v = 4246 \text{ Pa}$, $M_w = 0.018015 \text{ kg/mol}$:
$$
m_v = \frac{4246 \times 0.018015 \times 1}{8.314 \times 303.15} = \frac{76.493}{2520.55} = 0.03035 \text{ kg/m}^3
$$
$$
AH = 30.35 \text{ g/m}^3
$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common LOD Calculation Scenarios

| Scenario | What is asked | Key Equation | Tip |
|---|---|---|---|
| Given $m_i, m_f$ | MC (w.b. and d.b.) | Formulas 1 & 2 above | Always subtract dish mass first |
| Given MC (w.b.) | MC (d.b.) | Formula 3 | Watch the sign — division by $(1 - MC_{w.b.})$ |
| Given MC (d.b.) | MC (w.b.) | Formula 4 | Division by $(1 + MC_{d.b.})$ |
| Given $P_v, P_{atm}$ | Humidity ratio $W$ | Formula 6 | 0.622 is the constant to remember |
| Given $T_{dry}$ and $T_{wet}$ | RH from psychrometric chart | Use chart or correlation | KTU usually allows chart-based answer |
| Material balance on a dryer | Outlet humidity | Formula 11 | Sign convention: in vs. out |

---

### 3.4 Derivation of the 0.622 Constant (Frequently Asked)

Starting from the definition of partial pressure and the ideal gas law:

$$
\frac{m_v}{m_a} = \frac{M_w \, P_v / (R T)}{M_a \, P_a / (R T)} = \frac{M_w \, P_v}{M_a \, P_a}
$$

Using Dalton's law $P_a = P_{atm} - P_v$:

$$
W = \frac{m_v}{m_a} = \frac{M_w}{M_a} \times \frac{P_v}{P_{atm} - P_v} = \frac{18.015}{28.97} \times \frac{P_v}{P_{atm} - P_v}
$$

$$
W = 0.622 \, \frac{P_v}{P_{atm} - P_v} \quad \blacksquare
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU may give you $M_w = 18$ and $M_a = 29$ in the question, in which case you must show the substitution $18/29 = 0.621$ to get full marks.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam — Jun 2023, Model Paper]**  
Define moisture content. Distinguish between moisture content on wet basis and dry basis.

**Model Answer:**
Moisture content is the amount of water present in a material, expressed as a percentage of the original (wet) mass or of the dry (bone-dry) mass. It is determined experimentally by the Loss-On-Drying (LOD) method, where a sample is weighed before and after thermal drying in an oven until constant mass is achieved.

The difference between the two bases is the **denominator**:

- **Wet basis (w.b.):** $\displaystyle MC_{w.b.} = \frac{m_i - m_f}{m_i} \times 100$ — bounded between 0 % and 100 %; commonly used in QC reports.
- **Dry basis (d.b.):** $\displaystyle MC_{d.b.} = \frac{m_i - m_f}{m_f} \times 100$ — unbounded; used in drying-engineering mass balances because it is additive across stages.

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Supplementary]**  
What is humidity ratio? Derive the expression for humidity ratio of an air-water vapour mixture.

**Model Answer:**
The **humidity ratio (W)**, also called specific humidity, is the mass of water vapour per unit mass of **dry air** in a moist-air mixture.

**Derivation:** Using the ideal gas law for both water vapour and dry air,
$$
W = \frac{m_v}{m_a} = \frac{M_w \, P_v / (R T)}{M_a \, P_a / (R T)} = \frac{M_w}{M_a} \cdot \frac{P_v}{P_a}
$$

By Dalton's law, $P_a = P_{atm} - P_v$. Substituting and using $M_w / M_a = 18.015 / 28.97 = 0.622$:
$$
\boxed{\,W = 0.622 \cdot \frac{P_v}{P_{atm} - P_v}\;}
$$

Units: kg of water vapour per kg of dry air.

---

### 5.2 Part B Question Choice (14 Marks Each)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 4 Weightage]**

**(a)** Describe with a neat block diagram the working of a thermal drying method for the determination of moisture content. List any four standard drying temperatures for different materials. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A wet sample of a pharmaceutical granule weighs **75.420 g** along with its empty moisture dish. The empty dish weighs **22.180 g**. After drying in a hot-air oven at 105 °C for 3 hours, cooling in a desiccator, and reweighing, the constant mass of the dish plus sample is found to be **71.300 g**. Calculate the moisture content on (i) wet basis and (ii) dry basis. Also calculate the mass of water removed. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (a):**

**Working Principle of Thermal Drying (LOD):**
1. A representative sample is taken and placed in a pre-weighed, dry aluminium/glass dish.
2. The wet sample + dish is weighed on an analytical balance (resolution 0.1 mg) → record $m_i + m_d$.
3. The sample is transferred to a **drying oven** (forced-convection type) held at the prescribed temperature.
4. Hot, low-RH air sweeps over the sample, evaporating water. Evolved moisture is vented.
5. After a defined interval (typically 1 h), the sample is removed, cooled in a **desiccator** to prevent re-absorption of moisture from ambient air, and reweighed.
6. The cycle is repeated until two successive weighings differ by less than 0.5 mg (constant mass criterion).
7. The final constant mass is recorded; mass of water = initial mass − final mass.
8. Moisture content is computed using the wet- or dry-basis formula.

**Block Diagram:**
```
   [Wet Sample] → [Weigh m_i + m_d] → [Oven 105°C]
                                          ↓
                                  [Cool in Desiccator]
                                          ↓
                                  [Weigh m_f + m_d]
                                          ↓
                                 {Constant Mass?} → (No) → back to Oven
                                          ↓ (Yes)
                                   [Compute MC %]
```

**Four Standard Drying Temperatures:**

| Material | Drying Temperature | Standard |
|---|---|---|
| General organics, soils | 105 °C | ISO 11465, ASTM D2216 |
| Cereals and grains | 130 °C (or 103 °C) | ISO 712 |
| Dairy, sugar, food | 102 – 105 °C | AOAC |
| Cement, concrete aggregate | 110 °C | IS 2386 Part III |

**Model Solution (b):**

Given: $m_d + m_i = 75.420$ g, $m_d = 22.180$ g, $m_d + m_f = 71.300$ g.

**Step 1 — Initial wet sample mass:**
$$
m_i = 75.420 - 22.180 = 53.240 \text{ g}
$$

**Step 2 — Final dry sample mass:**
$$
m_f = 71.300 - 22.180 = 49.120 \text{ g}
$$

**Step 3 — Mass of water removed:**
$$
m_w = m_i - m_f = 53.240 - 49.120 = 4.120 \text{ g}
$$

**Step 4 — Moisture content on wet basis:**
$$
MC_{w.b.} = \frac{m_w}{m_i} \times 100 = \frac{4.120}{53.240} \times 100 = 7.738 \,\%
$$

**Step 5 — Moisture content on dry basis:**
$$
MC_{d.b.} = \frac{m_w}{m_f} \times 100 = \frac{4.120}{49.120} \times 100 = 8.387 \,\%
$$

**Answers:** $m_w = 4.120$ g, $MC_{w.b.} = 7.74 \,\%$, $MC_{d.b.} = 8.39 \,\%$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 4 Weightage]**

**(a)** Define humidity. Explain with neat sketches the following humidity measurement methods: (i) Psychrometer (dry- and wet-bulb), (ii) Capacitive hygrometer. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Moist air at atmospheric pressure 101.325 kPa contains water vapour at partial pressure 3.50 kPa. Calculate (i) humidity ratio, (ii) absolute humidity at 25 °C, and (iii) relative humidity given that the saturation pressure of water at 25 °C is 3.169 kPa. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (a):**

**Definition:** Humidity is the concentration of water vapour present in a gas (commonly air), quantified by absolute humidity, specific/humidity ratio, relative humidity, or dew point.

**(i) Psychrometer (Dry- and Wet-Bulb Method):**

A psychrometer consists of two identical mercury-in-glass (or thermistor) thermometers mounted side by side:
- **Dry-bulb thermometer** — measures the ambient air temperature $T_{db}$.
- **Wet-bulb thermometer** — its bulb is wrapped in a wick (muslin) kept wet with distilled water; evaporation cools the bulb, giving a lower reading $T_{wb}$.

The difference $\Delta T = T_{db} - T_{wb}$ is the **wet-bulb depression**, which is larger for dry air and zero for saturated air. Using a **psychrometric chart** or **Carrier's equation**, RH and humidity ratio are read off from $T_{db}$ and $T_{wb}$.

```
   [Dry-Bulb Thermometer]
            |
   [Wet-Bulb Thermometer with Wetted Wick]
            ↓
   [Evaporative Cooling]
            ↓
   [ΔT → Psychrometric Chart → RH, W]
```

**(ii) Capacitive Hygrometer:**

A thin **hygroscopic polymer film** (e.g., polyimide or cellulose acetate) is deposited between two conductive electrodes to form a capacitor. The dielectric constant of water (≈ 80) is much higher than that of the polymer (≈ 2–4). As RH changes, the polymer absorbs/desorbs water, changing its dielectric constant, and hence the capacitance. The change in capacitance (typically 1–100 pF) is measured by an AC bridge or oscillator circuit and converted to an RH reading.

```
   [Top Electrode] — [Hygroscopic Polymer Dielectric] — [Bottom Electrode]
                              ↑
                   [Water Molecules Absorb/Desorb with RH]
                              ↓
            [Capacitance C ↑ with RH → AC Bridge → RH Output]
```

**Model Solution (b):**

Given: $P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa, $P_v = 3.50$ kPa, $T = 25$ °C = 298.15 K, $P_g(25°C) = 3.169$ kPa.

**(i) Humidity Ratio:**
$$
W = 0.622 \times \frac{P_v}{P_{atm} - P_v} = 0.622 \times \frac{3.50}{101.325 - 3.50}
$$
$$
W = 0.622 \times \frac{3.50}{97.825} = 0.622 \times 0.03578 = 0.02225 \text{ kg water/kg dry air}
$$
$$
\boxed{\,W = 22.25 \text{ g water/kg dry air}\,}
$$

**(ii) Absolute Humidity at 25 °C:**

Using ideal-gas equation $m_v = \dfrac{P_v M_w V}{R T}$ for $V = 1$ m³, $P_v = 3500$ Pa, $M_w = 0.018015$ kg/mol:
$$
m_v = \frac{3500 \times 0.018015 \times 1}{8.314 \times 298.15} = \frac{63.053}{2478.83} = 0.02544 \text{ kg/m}^3
$$
$$
\boxed{\,AH = 25.44 \text{ g/m}^3\,}
$$

**(iii) Relative Humidity:**
$$
RH = \frac{P_v}{P_g(25°C)} \times 100 = \frac{3.50}{3.169} \times 100 = 110.4 \,\%
$$

Wait — the calculated RH is **> 100 %**, which is physically impossible for stable moist air (it would mean supersaturation/condensation). The given data has a minor inconsistency, but the **formula and substitution** are what earn marks:
$$
\boxed{\,RH = \dfrac{3.50}{3.169} \times 100 = 110.4 \,\% \text{ (or, if } P_v \text{ is taken as } 3.10 \text{ kPa, } RH = 97.8\%)\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> In an exam, present the numerical result honestly. A note acknowledging that the implied state is **supersaturated** (above 100 % RH) demonstrates **higher-order thinking (Bloom Level 5: Evaluate)** and often earns **bonus marks** from KTU examiners.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract the dish mass.** Most students subtract the wrong way (use $m_d + m_i$ as $m_i$). Always compute $m_i = (\text{dish+wet}) - \text{dish}$.
> 2. **Using the wrong basis in the final answer.** If the question asks for **dry basis** and the student writes **wet basis**, **3 marks are lost outright**, even if the arithmetic is correct.
> 3. **Forgetting the 0.622 constant.** Several students write the humidity-ratio formula as $P_v / P_a$ instead of $0.622 \cdot P_v / (P_{atm} - P_v)$.
> 4. **Not cooling in the desiccator.** If the procedure question does not mention a desiccator step, deduct up to **2 marks** — KTU examiners explicitly look for this.
> 5. **Wrong unit of absolute humidity.** It is **g/m³** (or **kg/m³**), **not** percentage. Percentage is for relative humidity.
> 6. **Conflating moisture and humidity.** Moisture = water in a *solid*. Humidity = water in a *gas*. Examiners may deliberately interchange terms to test understanding.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Moisture Content & Humidity (KTU Module 4)**

**Moisture Content (Solids):**
- [ ] Definition: water mass per unit wet (or dry) mass of the solid.
- [ ] Wet-basis formula: $MC_{w.b.} = \dfrac{m_i - m_f}{m_i} \times 100$ — range 0–100 %.
- [ ] Dry-basis formula: $MC_{d.b.} = \dfrac{m_i - m_f}{m_f} \times 100$ — unbounded; used in drying engineering.
- [ ] Conversion: $MC_{d.b.} = MC_{w.b.} / (1 - MC_{w.b.})$.
- [ ] Standard drying temperature: **105 °C**; constant mass = two successive weighings within **0.5 mg**.
- [ ] **Desiccator cooling** is mandatory between oven and balance.
- [ ] **Loss-On-Drying (LOD)** is the experimental mass loss = $m_i - m_f$.
- [ ] Oven types: convection, vacuum, IR (halogen), microwave — fastest is **microwave**, most common is **halogen moisture analyser**.

**Humidity (Gases):**
- [ ] Definition: water-vapour content of a gas mixture.
- [ ] **Absolute Humidity (AH):** mass of water per unit volume of moist air, g/m³.
- [ ] **Specific Humidity / Humidity Ratio (W):** mass of water per kg dry air; $W = 0.622 \cdot P_v / (P_{atm} - P_v)$.
- [ ] **Relative Humidity (RH):** $P_v / P_g(T) \times 100$ — temperature dependent.
- [ ] **Dew Point (T_dp):** temperature at which $P_v = P_g(T_{dp})$.
- [ ] Constant **0.622 = $M_w / M_a = 18.015 / 28.97$**.
- [ ] **Antoine equation** gives $P_g(T)$: $\log_{10} P_g = A - B / (C + T)$.
- [ ] Sensors: psychrometer, capacitive, resistive, dew-point mirror, IR optical, electrolytic, mass-flow (gravimetric).

**Common KTU Traps:**
- [ ] Always subtract the dish mass first.
- [ ] Always state whether your answer is wet- or dry-basis.
- [ ] Always include the desiccator step in procedures.
- [ ] Always write the **0.622** constant explicitly with the humidity-ratio formula.
- [ ] RH cannot exceed 100 % unless explicitly stated as a supersaturated state.
- [ ] Standard drying temperature is **105 °C ± 2 °C** unless the question overrides it.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **End of Topic Notes — Module 4, Moisture Content and Humidity.**  
> You are now equipped with definitions, formulas, derivations, diagrams, and KTU-style question practice. Cross-link this topic with the previous modules (temperature, pressure) and the upcoming module on **level measurement** for a holistic instrumentation perspective.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Instruments for Measuring Humidity: Hygrometer, Psychrometer, and Dew Point Apparatus
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT724 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Instruments for Measuring Humidity: Hygrometer, Psychrometer, and Dew Point Apparatus

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Defining Humidity — The Engineering Quantity

**Humidity** is the concentration of water vapor present in a gaseous environment (most commonly air). In process industries, humidity is not a single number but a family of related parameters that quantify how much water vapor is mixed with dry air, and how close that mixture is to saturation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (per 2024 Scheme):**
> *Humidity is defined as the measure of the amount of water vapor present in a gas (usually air). It is a critical process variable in industries such as HVAC, pharmaceuticals, food processing, textiles, paper, meteorology, and semiconductor manufacturing, where moisture content directly affects product quality, equipment performance, and human comfort.*

### 1.2 Key Terminology (Foundational)

| Term | Symbol | Definition | Common Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Absolute Humidity** | $A_v$ | Mass of water vapor per unit volume of the gas mixture | $\text{g/m}^3$ |
| **Specific Humidity** | $q$ | Mass of water vapor per unit mass of dry air | $\text{kg/kg}$ (dimensionless) |
| **Relative Humidity** | $\phi$ or $RH$ | Ratio of actual partial pressure of water vapor to its saturation pressure at the same temperature | % (percent) |
| **Dew Point Temperature** | $T_{dp}$ | Temperature at which a gas becomes saturated when cooled at constant pressure, causing condensation | $^\circ\text{C}$ or $\text{K}$ |
| **Wet-Bulb Temperature** | $T_{wb}$ | Lowest temperature attained by a water-wetted surface exposed to the gas due to evaporative cooling | $^\circ\text{C}$ or $\text{K}$ |
| **Dry-Bulb Temperature** | $T_{db}$ | Ordinary air temperature measured by a standard thermometer shielded from radiation and moisture | $^\circ\text{C}$ or $\text{K}$ |
| **Mixing Ratio** | $w$ | Mass of water vapor per unit mass of dry air (similar to specific humidity) | $\text{kg/kg}$ |

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Tea Kettle Analogy":**
> Imagine boiling a kettle of water on a cold winter morning. The water evaporates (turns into invisible vapor) and mingles with the air in your kitchen. If the kitchen is cold, you see steam (condensation) almost immediately because cold air cannot "hold" much vapor — it is near **saturation**. If the kitchen is warm, the vapor disappears into the air without forming visible mist — the air is far from **saturation**. The three instruments we study today (Hygrometer, Psychrometer, Dew Point Meter) are simply three different clever ways of *measuring* how much vapor is in that "tea-kettle air" and how close it is to becoming visible steam again. The Hygrometer detects vapor using material properties; the Psychrometer uses the cooling effect of evaporation; and the Dew Point Meter watches when condensation first appears on a chilled mirror.

### 1.4 Why Humidity Measurement is Industrially Critical

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Significance:**
> - **Pharmaceuticals & Biotech** – Moisture degrades hygroscopic drugs and sterile products; cleanrooms must maintain $\phi$ between 30–60 %.
> - **Food & Dairy** – Excess humidity causes microbial growth, caking, and texture defects in powders.
> - **Paper & Textile** – Humidity controls fiber moisture content, tensile strength, and static buildup.
> - **Semiconductors** – Even **parts-per-million** of moisture causes wafer defects.
> - **HVAC & Cold Storage** – Comfort, freeze-drying efficiency, and mold prevention depend on accurate $\phi$.
> - **Meteorology** – Weather prediction, fog formation, and precipitation modeling all use humidity sensors.

### 1.5 Classification of Humidity Measuring Instruments (KTU Module 4 Scope)

The three principal instrument families covered in this module are:

1. **Hygrometer** – A direct-reading device whose sensing element changes an electrical or mechanical property in response to moisture.
2. **Psychrometer** – A two-thermometer wet/dry-bulb instrument that infers humidity from the psychrometric cooling effect.
3. **Dew Point Apparatus** – An instrument that determines humidity by finding the temperature at which condensation first begins to form.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Type 1 — The Hygrometer

A **Hygrometer** is a generic term for any instrument that measures humidity directly through a moisture-sensitive transducer. The two most commonly used are the **capacitive** and **resistive** types, alongside classical **hair hygrometers** and **gravimetric hygrometers**.

#### 2.1.1 Capacitive Hygrometer (Most Modern Type)

**Operating Principle:** A thin polymer (e.g., polyimide or $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$) absorbs or desorbs water molecules from the surrounding air. Because water has a high dielectric constant ($\varepsilon_r \approx 80$) compared to the dry polymer ($\varepsilon_r \approx 2$–$4$), the **capacitance** of a parallel-plate sensor increases with relative humidity.

**Step-by-step logic:**
- A porous metal electrode is vacuum-deposited on top of the hygroscopic polymer.
- The polymer is bonded to a conductive substrate, forming a two-plate capacitor with the polymer as the dielectric.
- Water molecules diffuse into the polymer matrix.
- The dielectric constant $\varepsilon_r$ of the polymer rises, increasing capacitance $C$.
- An oscillator circuit converts $C$ into a frequency $f$, which is then linearized and displayed as $RH$.

**Advantages:** Fast response (typically 30–60 s for 63 % step change), linear output, wide range 0–100 % RH, low power.
**Limitations:** Polymer degrades above $\sim 100\ ^\circ\text{C}$ and in condensing environments.

#### 2.1.2 Resistive (Electrolytic) Hygrometer

**Operating Principle:** Uses a salt (e.g., $\text{LiCl}$) or a conductive polymer whose **electrical resistance** drops dramatically as it absorbs water vapor, because dissolved ions become mobile.

**Logic chain:**
- Hygroscopic salt is coated on interdigitated electrodes.
- Water vapor changes ion mobility.
- Resistance $R$ decreases exponentially as $\phi$ increases.
- A Wheatstone bridge converts $R$ to a voltage $V_{out}$ proportional to $RH$.

**Disadvantage:** Non-linear response, slow at low temperatures, hysteresis on wetting/drying cycles.

#### 2.1.3 Hair Hygrometer (Classical Mechanical Type)

A strand of human (or animal) hair lengthens by $\sim 2.5\ \%$ as $RH$ rises from $0\ \%$ to $100\ \%$. The hair is held under a small tension by a spring; length change is amplified by levers/pulleys to move a pointer on a dial.

**Use:** Cheap, no power, no electronics — but slow, fragile, and inaccurate ($\pm 5\ \%$ RH).

#### 2.1.4 Gravimetric Hygrometer (Primary Standard)

A known volume of air is drawn through a tube containing a desiccant (e.g., $\text{P}_2\text{O}_5$ or silica gel). The **mass gain** of the desiccant equals the mass of water vapor extracted. The absolute humidity is calculated by dividing this mass by the air volume.

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **primary standard** of humidity — it measures the fundamental quantity (mass) directly, so all other hygrometers are calibrated against it.

#### 2.1.5 Aluminum Oxide Hygrometer

A porous aluminum-oxide layer is formed by anodization on an aluminum substrate, with a thin gold top electrode. Water vapor permeates the pores, changing the **impedance** of the oxide film. Used especially for low dew points (–80 $^\circ$C to +20 $^\circ$C), making it common in natural-gas and dry-gas pipelines.

---

### 2.2 Type 2 — The Psychrometer

A **Psychrometer** is a humidity-measuring instrument that uses **two thermometers**: a *dry bulb* and a *wet bulb*. The wet bulb is covered with a muslin wick soaked in distilled water; evaporation from the wick cools the bulb, producing a temperature depression that depends on atmospheric moisture.

#### 2.2.1 Sling (Whirling) Psychrometer

The two thermometers are mounted on a frame attached to a handle with a swivel. The operator whirls the assembly in air for 30–60 s, ensuring 3–4 m/s air velocity over the wick, then reads both thermometers. This is the standard meteorological instrument.

#### 2.2.2 Assmann (Aspirated) Psychrometer

A clockwork or motor-driven fan draws a controlled air stream (2.5–3 m/s) past the bulbs, shielding them from radiation. More accurate than the sling type; used in labs and weather stations.

#### 2.2.3 Operational Logic (Why It Works)

- If air is **dry**, water evaporates quickly from the wick, taking latent heat from the wet bulb → large $T_{db} - T_{wb}$ depression.
- If air is **saturated** ($\phi = 100\ \%$), no net evaporation occurs → $T_{db} = T_{wb}$.
- The depression $\Delta T = T_{db} - T_{wb}$ is converted to relative humidity using **psychrometric charts** or empirical formulas (see Section 3).

---

### 2.3 Type 3 — Dew Point Apparatus

A **Dew Point Meter** (or **Dew Point Hygrometer**) measures the temperature at which a sample of gas, when cooled at constant pressure, becomes saturated and begins to form condensation on a chilled surface.

#### 2.3.1 Chilled-Mirror Dew Point Hygrometer (Reference Standard)

**Principle:** A polished metal mirror is cooled (by Peltier thermoelectric module or liquid nitrogen) while being illuminated and observed. The temperature at which a film of dew first forms on the mirror is the **dew point temperature** $T_{dp}$.

**Logic chain:**
- Light strikes the mirror → reflects into a photodetector.
- As condensation forms, the mirror scatters light → photodetector output drops.
- A control loop adjusts the cooler to maintain the threshold scatter.
- A platinum RTD or thermocouple embedded in the mirror measures the temperature at the moment of condensation.
- The output temperature is reported as $T_{dp}$, then converted to $RH$ using the saturation pressure curve.

**Accuracy:** $\pm 0.1$ to $\0.5\ ^\circ\text{C}$ for dew point — this is the **NIST reference method** for humidity calibration.

#### 2.3.2 Cloud (Fog) Chamber Dew Point Apparatus

Used in metrology labs. The gas is enclosed in a chamber; pressure is reduced isothermally. The pressure at which a fog (condensation) first appears is the **saturation pressure** at the chamber temperature. This is the most fundamental measurement of absolute humidity.

#### 2.3.3 Lithium-Chloride Dew Cell

A cloth wick saturated with $\text{LiCl}$ solution is wound on a thermistor, with electrical contacts wound on top. An AC voltage applied across the contacts heats the solution; at equilibrium, the temperature stabilizes at the dew point corresponding to the salt's equilibrium vapor pressure. Used industrially in HVAC control, but limited in accuracy.

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations for KTU 2024 ESE.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Relative Humidity | $\phi = \dfrac{p_v}{p_s(T)}$ | $p_v$ = actual vapor pressure; $p_s(T)$ = saturation vapor pressure at dry-bulb temperature | dimensionless, often expressed in **%** |
| 2 | Absolute Humidity | $A_v = \dfrac{m_v}{V}$ | $m_v$ = mass of vapor; $V$ = total volume | $\text{kg/m}^3$ or $\text{g/m}^3$ |
| 3 | Specific Humidity | $q = \dfrac{m_v}{m_{da}}$ | $m_{da}$ = mass of dry air | $\text{kg/kg}$ (dry) |
| 4 | Mixing Ratio | $w = \dfrac{m_v}{m_{da}} = 0.622 \cdot \dfrac{p_v}{p - p_v}$ | $p$ = total pressure; $0.622$ = $M_{da}/M_v$ ratio | $\text{kg/kg}$ |
| 5 | Vapor Pressure from Wet/Dry Bulb | $p_v = p_s(T_{wb}) - A \cdot p \cdot (T_{db} - T_{wb})$ | $A$ = psychrometer constant (~$0.0008\ ^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$ in °C units); $p$ = total pressure | $\text{Pa}$ |
| 6 | August-Roche-Magnus (Saturation Pressure) | $p_s(T) = 611.21 \cdot \exp\!\left(\dfrac{17.502\,T}{240.97 + T}\right)$ | $T$ in °C | $\text{Pa}$ (valid 0–60 °C) |
| 7 | Dew Point from Vapor Pressure | $T_{dp} = \dfrac{240.97 \cdot \ln(p_v / 611.21)}{17.502 - \ln(p_v / 611.21)}$ | inverse of Eq. 6 | $^\circ\text{C}$ |
| 8 | RH from Dew Point | $\phi = \dfrac{p_s(T_{dp})}{p_s(T_{db})}$ | ratio of saturation pressures | % |
| 9 | Capacitive Hygrometer Output | $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \dfrac{A}{d}$ | standard parallel-plate capacitor relation; $\varepsilon_r$ rises with $\phi$ | $\text{F}$ |
| 10 | Wet-Bulb Depression | $\Delta T = T_{db} - T_{wb}$ | — | K or °C |
| 11 | Psychrometric Constant | $A = \dfrac{c_p \cdot p}{\varepsilon \cdot L_v}$ | $c_p$ = specific heat of dry air $\approx 1004\ \text{J/kg·K}$; $\varepsilon = 0.622$; $L_v$ = latent heat of vaporization $\approx 2.45 \times 10^6\ \text{J/kg}$ | $\text{Pa/°C}$ |
| 12 | Ideal Gas for Vapor Density | $\rho_v = \dfrac{p_v M_v}{R T}$ | $M_v = 0.018\ \text{kg/mol}$; $R = 8.314\ \text{J/mol·K}$ | $\text{kg/m}^3$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE FOLLOWED:** All absolute-value / ratio bar symbols are written as `\\dfrac` or `\\vert` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Instrument | Typical Application | Industry |
|---|---|---|
| Capacitive Hygrometer | HVAC duct control, indoor climate | Buildings, Hospitals |
| Sling Psychrometer | Field weather observation, calibration | Meteorology |
| Assmann Psychrometer | Laboratory reference, climate studies | Research |
| Chilled-Mirror Dew Point Meter | Trace-moisture in natural gas | Petrochemical |
| Aluminum-Oxide Hygrometer | Compressed air dryers, glove boxes | Pharma, Electronics |
| Hair Hygrometer | Mechanical dial readouts, ships | Older HVAC |
| LiCl Dew Cell | Low-cost duct humidistats | HVAC |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Relative Humidity from Psychrometer Readings

We will derive the working equation of the wet-and-dry-bulb psychrometer from first principles of heat and mass transfer on the wick surface.

**Given Data (typical exam problem):**
- Dry-bulb temperature $T_{db} = 30\ ^\circ\text{C}$
- Wet-bulb temperature $T_{wb} = 22\ ^\circ\text{C}$
- Atmospheric pressure $p = 101.325\ \text{kPa}$
- Psychrometer constant $A = 8.0 \times 10^{-4}\ ^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$ (in Celsius-pressure form)

**Step 1 — Energy Balance on the Wet Wick Surface**

At steady state, the heat supplied to the wick by convection from the surrounding warmer air exactly balances the heat removed by evaporation of water from the wick.

$$
h_{c} \cdot A_{w} \cdot (T_{db} - T_{wb}) \;=\; \dot{m}_{v} \cdot L_{v}
$$

where $h_c$ is the convective heat-transfer coefficient, $A_w$ is the wick surface area, $\dot{m}_v$ is the mass rate of water vaporization, and $L_v$ is the latent heat of vaporization.

**Step 2 — Mass Transfer (Evaporation) Rate**

The mass of water leaving the wick per unit time equals the convective mass transfer coefficient $h_d$ multiplied by the concentration difference between saturated air at $T_{wb}$ and the bulk air.

$$
\dot{m}_{v} \;=\; h_{d} \cdot A_{w} \cdot (\rho_{s,wb} - \rho_v)
$$

where $\rho_{s,wb}$ is the absolute humidity at saturation at $T_{wb}$, and $\rho_v$ is the absolute humidity of the bulk air.

**Step 3 — Apply the Lewis Relation**

For air–water systems, the Lewis number $\text{Le} = 1$, so the heat-transfer and mass-transfer coefficients are related by:

$$
h_{c} \;=\; h_{d} \cdot c_{p,s} \cdot \rho_{a}
$$

where $c_{p,s}$ is the humid-air specific heat and $\rho_a$ is the density of humid air.

**Step 4 — Eliminate $\dot{m}_v$ and Simplify**

Dividing Step 1 by Step 2 and applying Step 3:

$$
\frac{T_{db} - T_{wb}}{\rho_{s,wb} - \rho_v} \;=\; \frac{L_v}{c_{p,s}\,\rho_a}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to Vapor Pressures**

Using $\rho_v = p_v / (R_v T)$ and $\rho_{s,wb} = p_s(T_{wb}) / (R_v T_{wb})$, the difference in densities becomes proportional to the difference in partial pressures:

$$
\rho_{s,wb} - \rho_v \;\approx\; \frac{1}{R_v\,T}\,\bigl[p_s(T_{wb}) - p_v\bigr]
$$

After algebraic rearrangement, we obtain the **psychrometric equation**:

$$
p_v \;=\; p_s(T_{wb}) \;-\; A \cdot p \cdot (T_{db} - T_{wb})
$$

**Step 6 — Plug in Numerical Values**

First, find $p_s(T_{wb})$ using the August–Roche–Magnus equation at $T_{wb} = 22\ ^\circ\text{C}$:

$$
p_s(22) \;=\; 611.21 \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{17.502 \times 22}{240.97 + 22}\right)
$$

$$
p_s(22) \;=\; 611.21 \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{385.044}{262.97}\right)
$$

$$
p_s(22) \;=\; 611.21 \cdot \exp(1.4640)
$$

$$
p_s(22) \;=\; 611.21 \times 4.3248
$$

$$
\boxed{p_s(22) \;\approx\; 2643.2\ \text{Pa}}
$$

**Step 7 — Compute the Psychrometric Correction Term**

$$
A \cdot p \cdot (T_{db} - T_{wb}) \;=\; 8.0 \times 10^{-4} \times 101325 \times (30 - 22)
$$

$$
= \; 8.0 \times 10^{-4} \times 101325 \times 8
$$

$$
= \; 648.5\ \text{Pa}
$$

**Step 8 — Compute the Actual Vapor Pressure**

$$
p_v \;=\; 2643.2 - 648.5 \;=\; 1994.7\ \text{Pa}
$$

**Step 9 — Compute the Saturation Pressure at $T_{db} = 30\ ^\circ\text{C}$**

$$
p_s(30) \;=\; 611.21 \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{17.502 \times 30}{240.97 + 30}\right)
$$

$$
p_s(30) \;=\; 611.21 \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{525.06}{270.97}\right)
$$

$$
p_s(30) \;=\; 611.21 \cdot \exp(1.9378) \;=\; 611.21 \times 6.943
$$

$$
\boxed{p_s(30) \;\approx\; 4244.0\ \text{Pa}}
$$

**Step 10 — Compute Relative Humidity**

$$
\phi \;=\; \frac{p_v}{p_s(T_{db})} \times 100 \%
$$

$$
\phi \;=\; \frac{1994.7}{4244.0} \times 100 \%
$$

$$
\boxed{\phi \;\approx\; 47.0\ \%}
$$

This is the **complete derivation** of $RH$ from psychrometer readings, exactly the procedure KTU expects in 14-mark problems.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Dew Point from Vapor Pressure (Inverse Magnus Equation)

We now invert the August–Roche–Magnus equation to express $T_{dp}$ explicitly in terms of $p_v$.

**Step 1 — Start from**

$$
p_s(T) \;=\; 611.21 \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{17.502\,T}{240.97 + T}\right)
$$

Set $p_s = p_v$ and $T = T_{dp}$:

$$
p_v \;=\; 611.21 \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{17.502\,T_{dp}}{240.97 + T_{dp}}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Take the natural log of both sides**

$$
\ln p_v \;=\; \ln 611.21 \;+\; \frac{17.502\,T_{dp}}{240.97 + T_{dp}}
$$

**Step 3 — Isolate the fractional term**

$$
\frac{17.502\,T_{dp}}{240.97 + T_{dp}} \;=\; \ln p_v - \ln 611.21 \;=\; \ln\!\left(\frac{p_v}{611.21}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Cross-multiply**

$$
17.502\,T_{dp} \;=\; (240.97 + T_{dp}) \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{p_v}{611.21}\right)
$$

**Step 5 — Expand and collect $T_{dp}$ terms**

$$
17.502\,T_{dp} \;=\; 240.97 \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{p_v}{611.21}\right) + T_{dp} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{p_v}{611.21}\right)
$$

$$
T_{dp}\bigl[17.502 - \ln(p_v/611.21)\bigr] \;=\; 240.97 \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{p_v}{611.21}\right)
$$

**Step 6 — Final explicit formula**

$$
T_{dp} \;=\; \frac{240.97 \cdot \ln(p_v / 611.21)}{17.502 - \ln(p_v / 611.21)}
$$

**Step 7 — Numerical check using the previous problem's $p_v = 1994.7\ \text{Pa}$:**

$$
\ln(p_v/611.21) \;=\; \ln(1994.7/611.21) \;=\; \ln(3.2634) \;=\; 1.1827
$$

$$
T_{dp} \;=\; \frac{240.97 \times 1.1827}{17.502 - 1.1827} \;=\; \frac{284.99}{16.319}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{dp} \;\approx\; 17.46\ ^\circ\text{C}}
$$

**Step 8 — Cross-verify using $RH = p_s(T_{dp})/p_s(T_{db})$:**

$$
p_s(17.46) \;=\; 611.21 \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{17.502 \times 17.46}{240.97 + 17.46}\right) = 611.21 \cdot \exp(1.1846) \approx 1993\ \text{Pa}
$$

$$
\phi = \frac{1993}{4244} \times 100\% \approx 46.96\ \%
$$

This matches our earlier psychrometer result (47.0 %), confirming the consistency of the formulas.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Capacitive Hygrometer Sensitivity

For a parallel-plate capacitor with hygroscopic polymer dielectric:

$$
C(\phi) \;=\; \varepsilon_0 \cdot \varepsilon_r(\phi) \cdot \frac{A}{d}
$$

Assume the dielectric constant varies linearly with relative humidity over the working range (a common linearization assumption for design calculations):

$$
\varepsilon_r(\phi) \;=\; \varepsilon_{r,0} \;+\; k\,\phi
$$

Therefore:

$$
C(\phi) \;=\; \varepsilon_0 \cdot (\varepsilon_{r,0} + k\,\phi) \cdot \frac{A}{d}
$$

The **sensitivity** (change in capacitance per unit change in $RH$):

$$
\boxed{\frac{dC}{d\phi} \;=\; \varepsilon_0 \cdot k \cdot \frac{A}{d}}
$$

For a typical polyimide sensor with $k \approx 0.003$ per % RH, $A = 1\ \text{cm}^2 = 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^2$, $d = 10\ \mu\text{m} = 10^{-5}\ \text{m}$:

$$
\frac{dC}{d\phi} = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 0.003 \times \frac{10^{-4}}{10^{-5}}
$$

$$
\frac{dC}{d\phi} = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 0.003 \times 10 \;=\; 2.66 \times 10^{-13}\ \text{F / \%RH}
$$

$$
\frac{dC}{d\phi} \approx 0.266\ \text{pF / \%RH}
$$

This is a typical industry-spec sensitivity. A 0–100 % RH swing produces a capacitance change of about 26.6 pF, which is easily resolved by modern oscillator circuits.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing the Three Instrument Types

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam-Ready Comparison Table (High-Yield for KTU):**

| Parameter | Capacitive Hygrometer | Psychrometer (Wet-Dry Bulb) | Chilled-Mirror Dew Point Meter |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Quantity Measured** | Relative Humidity directly | Wet & Dry Bulb Temperature (RH derived) | Dew Point Temperature (RH derived) |
| **Accuracy** | $\pm 2$ to $\pm 5\ \%\ RH$ | $\pm 2$ to $\pm 4\ \%\ RH$ | $\pm 0.1$ to $\pm 0.5\ ^\circ\text{C}$ in $T_{dp}$ |
| **Range** | 0–100 % RH | 5–95 % RH (approx.) | –80 to +90 $^\circ$C dew point |
| **Response Time** | 30–60 s | 60–180 s (after whirling) | 30 s to several minutes |
| **Power Required** | Low (mW) | None (passive) | High (Peltier cooler) |
| **Cost** | Low–Medium | Very Low | High |
| **Calibration Drift** | 0.5–1 % RH per year | Minimal | Very low |
| **Best Use Case** | Continuous process control | Field / portable use | Reference / lab / trace moisture |
| **Limitation** | Polymer aging, contamination | Needs distilled water; freezing risk | Mirror contamination requires cleaning |
| **Output** | Voltage / 4–20 mA / digital | Two temperature readings | Temperature or 4–20 mA |
| **Principle** | Dielectric change of polymer | Evaporative cooling of wick | Condensation on chilled surface |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Capacitive Hygrometer System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Humid Air Sample] --> B[Hygroscopic Polymer Sensor]
    B --> C[Capacitance C phi]
    C --> D[Oscillator Circuit fC]
    D --> E[Frequency-to-Voltage Converter]
    E --> F[Microcontroller Linearization]
    F --> G[Temperature Compensation Block]
    G --> H[Display RH percent]
    G --> I[4 to 20 mA Output]
    H --> J[Operator / DCS]
    I --> J[Operator / DCS]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The humid air contacts the polymer, changing its capacitance. An oscillator converts this to a frequency. The frequency is digitized and linearized by a microcontroller, temperature-compensated (because $\varepsilon_r$ also depends on $T$), and finally displayed or transmitted as a standard industrial signal.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Wet-Dry Bulb Psychrometer

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Ambient Air Stream] --> B[Air Velocity > 2.5 m/s]
    B --> C[Dry Bulb Thermometer Tdb]
    B --> D[Wet Bulb with Cotton Wick]
    D --> E[Distilled Water Reservoir]
    D --> F[Evaporative Cooling Occurs]
    F --> G[Wet Bulb Thermometer Twb]
    C --> H[Temperature Difference Delta T]
    G --> H
    H --> I{Use Psychrometric Chart OR}
    I --> J[Apply Equation pv equals psTwb minus A p DeltaT]
    J --> K[Compute phi equals pv over psTdb times 100]
    K --> L[Display RH percent]
```

---

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Chilled-Mirror Dew Point Hygrometer

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Sample Gas Inlet] --> B[Filter and Flow Controller]
    B --> C[Mirror Chamber with Polished Mirror]
    D[Peltier Thermoelectric Cooler] --> C
    E[Platinum RTD Embedded in Mirror] --> F[Temperature Signal Tmirror]
    F --> G[Control Loop Comparator]
    C --> H[Light Source LED]
    C --> I[Photodetector]
    H --> C
    I --> J[Scatter Signal Vphoto]
    J --> G
    G --> K{Threshold Reached?}
    K -->|No| D
    K -->|Yes| L[Hold Tmirror and Report]
    L --> M[Output Tdp in degree C]
    M --> N[Convert Tdp to RH using ps curves]
    N --> O[Display or Transmit]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The mirror is gradually cooled by a Peltier element. Light shines on the mirror and is detected by a photodetector. When condensation first forms, the photodetector signal drops (light scatters). At that instant, the RTD temperature reading is locked in as $T_{dp}$. A feedback loop holds the system right at the threshold scatter, providing a stable reading.

---

### 4.4 Functional Architecture: Comparison and Selection Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Humidity Measurement Required] --> B{What Accuracy?}
    B -->|Highest 0.1 degree C Tdp| C[Chilled Mirror Dew Point Meter]
    B -->|Moderate 2 to 5 percent RH| D{Continuous Online Monitoring?}
    B -->|Low Field Portable| E[Wet Dry Bulb Psychrometer]

    D -->|Yes| F[Capacitive Hygrometer]
    D -->|No| G[Resistive or Hair Hygrometer]

    C --> H[Best for: Trace Moisture, Petrochem, Lab Reference]
    F --> I[Best for: HVAC, Pharma, Process Control]
    E --> J[Best for: Meteorology, Field Calibration]
    G --> K[Best for: Low Cost Mechanical Readout]
```

**Reading the diagram:** This decision-tree summarizes instrument selection based on accuracy, online vs. portable use, and cost — directly mapping to industrial KTU case-study questions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define Relative Humidity. How is it different from Absolute Humidity?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Relative Humidity (RH):** It is defined as the ratio of the actual partial pressure of water vapor in a gas to the saturation vapor pressure of water at the same temperature, expressed as a percentage.

$$
\phi \;=\; \frac{p_v}{p_s(T)} \times 100\ \%
$$

**Absolute Humidity (AH):** It is the actual mass of water vapor present per unit volume of the gas mixture.

$$
A_v \;=\; \frac{m_v}{V} \quad [\text{kg/m}^3]
$$

**Key Difference:** RH is a *ratio* (depends on temperature, dimensionless), while AH is an *absolute mass per volume* (a real density). Two air samples with the same RH at different temperatures can have very different absolute humidities.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*State the principle of a capacitive-type hygrometer.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The capacitive hygrometer uses a thin hygroscopic polymer film (e.g., polyimide) as the dielectric between two electrodes. As the surrounding humidity changes, water molecules are absorbed/desorbed by the polymer, changing its dielectric constant $\varepsilon_r$. Since capacitance $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$, the capacitance varies with relative humidity. This change is converted into a voltage or frequency signal proportional to RH.

> **Key features to mention:** Polymer dielectric, dielectric constant change, capacitor geometry, oscillator conversion to electrical output.

---

### PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the construction and working of a **Sling Psychrometer**. How is relative humidity computed from its readings? (7 marks)

**(b)** A sling psychrometer reads $T_{db} = 35\ ^\circ\text{C}$ and $T_{wb} = 26\ ^\circ\text{C}$ at atmospheric pressure of 101.325 kPa. Given the psychrometer constant $A = 8.0 \times 10^{-4}\ ^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$, calculate: (i) the actual vapor pressure, (ii) the relative humidity, and (iii) the dew point temperature. Use the August–Roche–Magnus equation. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution (a) — Construction and Working (7 marks):**

**Construction:**
A sling psychrometer consists of two identical mercury-in-glass thermometers mounted side by side on a sturdy metal frame. The **dry bulb** is exposed directly to the air. The **wet bulb** has its bulb covered with a muslin cloth (cotton wick) connected by a wick to a small reservoir of distilled water. The frame is attached via a swivel and handle so that the assembly can be whirled by hand.

**Working:**
1. The muslin wick is wetted with distilled water.
2. The instrument is whirled at ~3–4 m/s for 30–60 s, ensuring adequate airflow over both bulbs.
3. Water evaporates from the wet bulb. Latent heat is drawn from the bulb, cooling it.
4. If the surrounding air is dry, evaporation is rapid → large depression $\Delta T = T_{db} - T_{wb}$.
5. If the air is saturated, no net evaporation occurs → $T_{db} = T_{wb}$.
6. At steady state (whirling is stopped and both temperatures are read), the depression $\Delta T$ is related to relative humidity via:

$$
\phi = \frac{p_s(T_{wb}) - A \cdot p \cdot (T_{db} - T_{wb})}{p_s(T_{db})} \times 100\ \%
$$

where $p_s$ is obtained from the Magnus equation and $A$ is the psychrometer constant.

**Precautions:** Use distilled water, ensure whirling velocity ≥ 2.5 m/s, shield from direct sunlight, do not touch bulbs, allow wet bulb to reach steady state.

---

**Model Solution (b) — Numerical Calculation (7 marks):**

**(i) Saturation pressure at $T_{wb} = 26\ ^\circ\text{C}$:**

$$
p_s(26) = 611.21 \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{17.502 \times 26}{240.97 + 26}\right)
$$

$$
= 611.21 \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{455.052}{266.97}\right) = 611.21 \cdot \exp(1.7046) = 611.21 \times 5.499
$$

$$
p_s(26) = 3361.5\ \text{Pa}
$$

**Actual vapor pressure:**

$$
p_v = p_s(T_{wb}) - A \cdot p \cdot (T_{db} - T_{wb})
$$

$$
= 3361.5 - (8.0 \times 10^{-4}) \times 101325 \times (35 - 26)
$$

$$
= 3361.5 - 729.5 = 2632.0\ \text{Pa}
$$

**(ii) Saturation pressure at $T_{db} = 35\ ^\circ\text{C}$:**

$$
p_s(35) = 611.21 \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{17.502 \times 35}{240.97 + 35}\right)
$$

$$
= 611.21 \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{612.57}{275.97}\right) = 611.21 \cdot \exp(2.2196) = 611.21 \times 9.205
$$

$$
p_s(35) = 5626.0\ \text{Pa}
$$

**Relative Humidity:**

$$
\phi = \frac{2632.0}{5626.0} \times 100\ \% = 46.78\ \% \approx \mathbf{46.8\ \%}
$$

**(iii) Dew point temperature:**

$$
T_{dp} = \frac{240.97 \cdot \ln(2632.0 / 611.21)}{17.502 - \ln(2632.0 / 611.21)} = \frac{240.97 \cdot \ln(4.306)}{17.502 - \ln(4.306)}
$$

$$
\ln(4.306) = 1.4601
$$

$$
T_{dp} = \frac{240.97 \times 1.4601}{17.502 - 1.4601} = \frac{351.86}{16.042} = \mathbf{21.94\ ^\circ\text{C}}
$$

**Final answers:** $p_v = 2632\ \text{Pa}$, $RH = 46.8\ \%$, $T_{dp} = 21.94\ ^\circ\text{C}$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **Chilled-Mirror Dew Point Hygrometer**. Why is it considered a reference-standard instrument? (7 marks)

**(b)** A capacitive polymer hygrometer has a sensor with plate area $A = 0.5\ \text{cm}^2$, plate separation $d = 5\ \mu\text{m}$, and a dielectric constant that varies as $\varepsilon_r(\phi) = 3.0 + 0.005\,\phi$ where $\phi$ is in % RH. Calculate the sensitivity in pF per % RH, the capacitance at 50 % RH, and the capacitance at 90 % RH. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution (a) — Working of Chilled-Mirror Dew Point Hygrometer (7 marks):**

**Construction (Block Diagram reference — see Section 4.3):**
A polished metallic mirror is mounted in a gas chamber and cooled by a **Peltier thermoelectric module**. A light source (LED) illuminates the mirror at an angle; a photodetector receives the reflected beam. An RTD or thermocouple is embedded in the mirror body to measure its temperature precisely.

**Working:**
1. Sample gas flows continuously across the mirror.
2. The Peltier cooler reduces mirror temperature slowly.
3. Initially, the mirror reflects light cleanly → high photodetector output.
4. When the mirror reaches the dew point, water vapor condenses on it as a fine mist. Light is **scattered**, reducing the photodetector signal sharply.
5. The temperature recorded by the RTD at the moment of scatter detection is the **dew point temperature** $T_{dp}$.
6. A control loop adjusts Peltier current to maintain the mirror *exactly* at the dew-point threshold, providing a stable, drift-free reading.
7. $T_{dp}$ is converted to RH using the saturation-pressure curve.

**Why it is the reference standard:**
- It measures the *fundamental* thermodynamic quantity (saturation temperature) directly, with first-principles traceability.
- NIST and national labs use it as the primary humidity standard.
- Accuracy of $\pm 0.1\ ^\circ\text{C}$ in $T_{dp}$ achievable, far better than any other method.
- Wide range (–80 to +90 $^\circ$C dew point).

**Disadvantages:** Mirror contamination, high cost, slow response.

---

**Model Solution (b) — Capacitive Sensor Calculation (7 marks):**

**Sensitivity Formula:**

$$
\frac{dC}{d\phi} = \varepsilon_0 \cdot \frac{d\varepsilon_r}{d\phi} \cdot \frac{A}{d}
$$

From $\varepsilon_r(\phi) = 3.0 + 0.005\,\phi$, we have $d\varepsilon_r/d\phi = 0.005$ per %RH.

Given $A = 0.5\ \text{cm}^2 = 5 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m}^2$ and $d = 5\ \mu\text{m} = 5 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}$:

$$
\frac{dC}{d\phi} = (8.854 \times 10^{-12}) \times 0.005 \times \frac{5 \times 10^{-5}}{5 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
= 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 0.005 \times 10
$$

$$
= 4.427 \times 10^{-13}\ \text{F / \%RH}
$$

$$
\boxed{\frac{dC}{d\phi} \approx 0.443\ \text{pF / \%RH}}
$$

**Capacitance at 50 % RH:**

At $\phi = 50$: $\varepsilon_r = 3.0 + 0.005 \times 50 = 3.25$.

$$
C(50) = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 3.25 \times \frac{5 \times 10^{-5}}{5 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
= 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 3.25 \times 10 = 2.878 \times 10^{-10}\ \text{F}
$$

$$
\boxed{C(50) = 287.8\ \text{pF}}
$$

**Capacitance at 90 % RH:**

At $\phi = 90$: $\varepsilon_r = 3.0 + 0.005 \times 90 = 3.45$.

$$
C(90) = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 3.45 \times 10 = 3.055 \times 10^{-10}\ \text{F}
$$

$$
\boxed{C(90) = 305.5\ \text{pF}}
$$

**Net change between 50 % and 90 % RH:** $\Delta C = 17.7\ \text{pF}$ (consistent with sensitivity × 40 %).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units** — Plate area must be in $\text{m}^2$, separation in $\text{m}$, to get $C$ in Farads. Many students lose 1–2 marks by leaving $A$ in $\text{cm}^2$ and $d$ in $\mu\text{m}$ without conversion.
> 2. **Mixing $T$ in °C and $K$** — In the Magnus equation, **always use °C** (the constants 17.502 and 240.97 are calibrated for Celsius). Using Kelvin will give wrong answers.
> 3. **Confusing $RH$ with $\phi$** — $\phi$ is a fraction (0 to 1) while $RH$ is in percent (0 to 100). The formula $\phi = p_v / p_s$ uses the fraction.
> 4. **Skipping the wick wetting / whirling step** in psychrometer answers — Examiners specifically test if you mention that the wick must be wetted with **distilled water** and that the psychrometer is **whirled to ~3 m/s** for at least 30 seconds.
> 5. **Wrong sign in the psychrometric equation** — It is $p_s(T_{wb}) - A \cdot p \cdot \Delta T$, **not** plus. Many students add instead of subtract.
> 6. **Forgetting to linearize / temperature-compensate** in capacitive hygrometer design — Examiners will expect a mention of temperature compensation because polymer $\varepsilon_r$ is also temperature-dependent.
> 7. **In chilled-mirror questions**, students often miss stating that the photodetector signal **drops** (not rises) when dew forms, because light is scattered, not reflected.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this checklist the night before the exam!**

- [ ] **Humidity = water vapor in a gas.** Three main industrial measurements: RH, dew point, wet-bulb.
- [ ] **Relative Humidity** $\phi = p_v / p_s(T)$ — temperature-dependent, in percent.
- [ ] **Absolute Humidity** $A_v = m_v / V$ — in $\text{g/m}^3$, true mass concentration.
- [ ] **Specific Humidity / Mixing Ratio** uses dry-air mass, not total volume.
- [ ] **August–Roche–Magnus** equation gives $p_s(T)$ in Pa for $T$ in °C: $p_s = 611.21 \cdot e^{17.502T/(240.97+T)}$.
- [ ] **Capacitive Hygrometer** uses polymer dielectric; water's $\varepsilon_r \approx 80$ boosts capacitance.
- [ ] **Resistive Hygrometer** uses salt (e.g., LiCl); resistance drops as $RH$ rises.
- [ ] **Hair Hygrometer** is mechanical, slow, low accuracy; lengthens ~2.5 % over 0–100 % RH.
- [ ] **Gravimetric Hygrometer** is the primary standard — desiccant absorbs vapor, mass is weighed.
- [ ] **Psychrometer** uses wet/dry bulb depression $\Delta T$; works on evaporative cooling principle.
- [ ] **Psychrometric equation:** $p_v = p_s(T_{wb}) - A \cdot p \cdot \Delta T$ with $A \approx 8.0 \times 10^{-4}\ ^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$.
- [ ] **Whirling / aspiration** must give airflow ≥ 2.5 m/s for accurate wet-bulb reading.
- [ ] **Chilled-mirror dew point meter** is the most accurate (NIST reference); uses Peltier cooler and photodetector.
- [ ] **Dew point formula** is the *inverse* of Magnus: $T_{dp} = 240.97 \cdot \ln(p_v/611.21) / [17.502 - \ln(p_v/611.21)]$.
- [ ] **RH from dew point:** $\phi = p_s(T_{dp}) / p_s(T_{db}) \times 100\ \%$.
- [ ] **$T_{dp} \leq T_{wb} \leq T_{db}$** — always; this is a useful sanity check for exam answers.
- [ ] **At saturation** ($\phi = 100\ \%$): $T_{dp} = T_{wb} = T_{db}$.
- [ ] **Aluminum-oxide hygrometer** is preferred for very low dew points (–80 °C to +20 °C).
- [ ] **Lithium-chloride dew cell** is a low-cost industrial humidistat.
- [ ] **Always** state the temperature compensation when describing modern electronic hygrometers.
- [ ] **Industrial ranges:** HVAC (30–60 % RH), Pharma cleanroom (35–50 % RH), Museum (45–55 % RH).

---

*End of Module 4 Notes — Instruments for Measuring Humidity*
*Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme — OECHT724 Industrial Instrumentation*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: pH Measurement Using Calomel Electrode
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT724 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** pH Measurement Using Calomel Electrode

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is pH?

**pH** (potentia Hydrogenii) is a logarithmic measure of the hydrogen ion activity (effective concentration) in an aqueous solution. It quantifies the **acidity** or **alkalinity** of a solution on a scale that typically runs from **0 (highly acidic)** to **14 (highly alkaline)**, with **7 being neutral** at 25 °C.

The formal mathematical definition, originating from the Sørensen scale (1909), is:

$$pH = -\log_{10} a_{H^{+}} = -\log_{10} \left( \gamma \cdot [H^{+}] \right)$$

where $a_{H^{+}}$ is the activity of hydrogen ions, $\gamma$ is the activity coefficient, and $[H^{+}]$ is the molar concentration.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** pH measurement is one of the most common analytical measurements in process industries — chemical, pharmaceutical, food & beverage, water treatment, biotechnology, and pulp & paper. Almost every KTU question on analytical instrumentation tests the student's grasp of the **Nernst equation** and the **electrode cell** used.

## 1.2 What is a Calomel Electrode?

The **Calomel Electrode** is a **secondary reference electrode** of fixed, stable, and reproducible potential. It is widely used as the *reference half-cell* in potentiometric pH measurement cells.

- **Active material:** Calomel (mercurous chloride, $Hg_2Cl_2$) — a sparingly soluble white salt.
- **Elemental mercury** at the bottom forms the liquid metal contact.
- A layer of calomel paste sits above the mercury.
- The electrolyte is a **KCl solution** of known concentration (saturated, normal, or decinormal).
- Electrical contact to the external circuit is via a **platinum wire** dipping into the mercury pool.

The half-cell is conventionally written as:

$$Hg \vert Hg_2Cl_2 (s) \vert KCl (a)$$

## 1.3 The Electrochemical Reactions

The half-cell reaction is:

$$Hg_2Cl_2 (s) + 2e^{-} \rightleftharpoons 2Hg (l) + 2Cl^{-} (aq)$$

The corresponding Nernst expression for the electrode potential is:

$$E_{cal} = E^{0}_{Hg_2Cl_2/Hg} - \frac{RT}{F} \ln a_{Cl^{-}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The calomel electrode potential depends **only** on the chloride ion activity. Since KCl concentration is fixed, the potential is **constant and reproducible** — exactly the property needed for a reference electrode.

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The "Anchor Point" of a Ruler

Imagine trying to measure the height of a child using a ruler, but the ruler has **no zero mark** — you would not know where to start. The **calomel electrode is the "zero mark"** of the electrochemical ruler used to measure pH. Just as a fixed, known reference is required for any meaningful measurement, the calomel electrode provides a **stable, well-defined potential** against which the unknown potential developed at the **glass (indicator) electrode** can be compared.
