## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Beer-Lambert unit traps** — ε in L·mol⁻¹·cm⁻¹, b in cm, c in mol/L. A 1-mark deduction is automatic for any unit mismatch.
> 2. **Atomic vs. Molecular emission confusion** — Flame photometry is *atomic* emission; fluorescence is *molecular* emission. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 3. **Mass spectrometer resolution formula** is *not* the same as optical resolution. R = M/ΔM is the unambiguous MS definition; do not substitute the Rayleigh criterion.
> 4. **Magnetic-sector radius derivation** — students commonly forget the **√(m/z)** term; this is the *most-marks-lost* area in Part B numericals.

---

### Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Define absorbance and molar absorptivity. State the Beer-Lambert Law and indicate which combinations of its variables produce a dimensionless quantity.**

**Model Answer:**

*   **Absorbance (A):** A dimensionless quantity that measures the amount of light absorbed by a sample. Formally, **A = log₁₀(I₀ / I)**, where **I₀** is the incident intensity and **I** is the transmitted intensity.
*   **Molar Absorptivity (ε):** Also called the molar extinction coefficient, it is a constant of proportionality that quantifies *how strongly* a chemical species absorbs light at a given wavelength, per unit concentration and per unit path length. Units: **L·mol⁻¹·cm⁻¹**.
*   **Beer-Lambert Law:** $A = \varepsilon \, b \, c$
*   **Dimensional check:** $\varepsilon$ (L·mol⁻¹·cm⁻¹) × b (cm) × c (mol/L) → (L·mol⁻¹·cm⁻¹)(cm)(mol/L) = **dimensionless**. ✓

*(3 marks: 1 for each definition + law; 1 for dimensional check.)*

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between Absorption Spectrometer and Emission Spectrometer with one industrial application of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Absorption Spectrometer | Emission Spectrometer |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Energy source** | External (lamp) | Internal (flame/plasma/spark) |
| **Measurement** | Transmitted light deficit | Self-emitted radiation |
| **Sample condition** | At ground state | Atomised *and* excited |
| **Industrial application** | UV-Vis measurement of dye concentration in textile industry effluent | ICP-OES for trace-metal QC in semiconductor wafer-cleaning baths |

*(3 marks: 1 mark per distinct feature, 1 mark for each application.)*

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module-4 Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU Model — Internal Choice Pattern]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **UV-Vis Absorption Spectrometer**. List its main components and state the function of each. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** Derive the **Beer-Lambert Law** starting from the differential equation of fractional absorption. A solution of a drug has a concentration of **2.5 × 10⁻⁵ mol/L** in a 1 cm cell. If the molar absorptivity is **18,000 L·mol⁻¹·cm⁻¹**, calculate (i) the absorbance and (ii) the percentage transmittance. **\[7 Marks\]**

**Model Solution (a):**

The UV-Vis spectrometer consists of:

1.  **Light source:** Tungsten-halogen lamp (visible, 320–2500 nm) and deuterium lamp (UV, 190–400 nm). Source selection is automatic based on wavelength.
2.  **Monochromator:** A diffraction grating (preferred over prism in modern instruments) that isolates a narrow wavelength band (~1–2 nm).
3.  **Beam splitter / chopper:** Splits the monochromatic beam into a sample beam and a reference beam (dual-beam design) for drift compensation.
4.  **Sample holder:** A quartz cuvette (quartz required for UV transmission) of path length 1 cm containing the analyte solution.
5.  **Detector:** Photomultiplier tube (PMT) or photodiode array. PMTs offer higher sensitivity; PDAs allow *simultaneous* multi-wavelength acquisition.
6.  **Readout / Computer:** Displays absorbance or transmittance vs. wavelength.

**Working principle:** The monochromatic beam is alternately sent through the sample and the reference. The detector measures **I** (sample) and **I₀** (reference). The instrument electronics compute **A = log₁₀(I₀ / I)**, which is printed or plotted against wavelength to give the **absorption spectrum**.

*(7 marks: 1 mark per component + 2 marks for working principle narrative.)*

**Model Solution (b):**

**Derivation:**
The fractional decrease in intensity across an infinitesimally thin slice of thickness **db** is proportional to the concentration **c** of the absorbing species and the intensity **I** itself:

$$dI \;=\; -k \, c \, I \, db$$

Separating variables and integrating from the cell entrance (**I = I₀** at **b = 0**) to the cell exit (**I = I** at **b = b**):

$$\int_{I_0}^{I} \frac{dI}{I} \;=\; -k \, c \int_{0}^{b} db \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \ln\!\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) \;=\; -k \, c \, b$$

Converting to base-10 logarithm and defining **ε = k / 2.303**:

$$\boxed{\,A \;=\; \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right) \;=\; \varepsilon \, b \, c\,}$$

**Numerical evaluation:**

(i) Absorbance:

$$A \;=\; \varepsilon \, b \, c \;=\; 18000 \times 1.00 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-5} \;=\; 0.45$$

(ii) Percentage transmittance:

$$T \;=\; 10^{-A} \;=\; 10^{-0.45} \;=\; 0.3548$$

$$T\% \;=\; 0.3548 \times 100 \;=\; 35.48\%$$

*(7 marks: 4 for derivation, 1 for A calculation, 1 for T conversion, 1 for final %.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the principle and working of a **Magnetic-Sector Mass Spectrometer** with a block diagram. Derive the expression for the radius of the ion path in terms of mass-to-charge ratio, accelerating voltage, and magnetic flux density. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** A **Quadrupole Mass Analyser** uses DC voltage **U = 200 V** and RF amplitude **V = 1500 V** at frequency **f = 1.5 MHz**. Calculate (i) the angular frequency ω, and (ii) the Mathieu stability parameters **a** and **q** for an ion of **m/z = 100** (assume the quadrupole rod spacing **r₀ = 5 mm**, ion charge **z = 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ C**, mass **m = 100 × 1.66 × 10⁻²⁷ kg**). Comment on the stability of the trajectory. **\[7 Marks\]**

**Model Solution (a):**

**Principle:** Ions of different mass-to-charge ratios travel on circular paths of *different radii* in a uniform magnetic field, allowing physical separation in space.

**Working (refer to block diagram in Section 4.3):**
1.  Sample is introduced through the inlet, vaporised, and ionised by electron impact (70 eV).
2.  Positive ions are accelerated through potential **V**, gaining kinetic energy **zV**.
3.  Ions enter the magnetic field **B** perpendicular to their velocity. The Lorentz force curves them into circles of radius **r**.
4.  The collector slit is fixed at a specific radius. Scanning B brings different m/z ions onto the slit sequentially, producing a mass spectrum.

**Derivation (as derived in Section 3.2):**

From energy conservation $zV = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^2$, we obtain $v = \sqrt{2zV/m}$.
Equating Lorentz force to centripetal force: $z v B = m v^2 / r$, giving $r = m v / (z B)$.
Substituting v:

$$\boxed{\,r \;=\; \frac{1}{B}\sqrt{\frac{2 m V}{z}}\,}$$

Or equivalently:

$$r \;=\; \frac{\sqrt{2 V (m/z)}}{B}$$

*(7 marks: 2 for principle, 2 for working with diagram, 3 for derivation.)*

**Model Solution (b):**

(i) Angular frequency:

$$\omega \;=\; 2 \pi f \;=\; 2 \pi \times 1.5 \times 10^{6} \;=\; 9.425 \times 10^{6} \; \text{rad/s}$$

(ii) Mathieu stability parameters (the canonical quadrupole stability equations):

$$a \;=\; \frac{4 \, z \, U}{m \, \omega^2 \, r_0^2}$$

$$q \;=\; \frac{2 \, z \, V}{m \, \omega^2 \, r_0^2}$$

**Compute a:**

$$a \;=\; \frac{4 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 200}{(1.66 \times 10^{-25}) \times (9.425 \times 10^{6})^2 \times (5 \times 10^{-3})^2}$$

Denominator: $(1.66 \times 10^{-25}) \times (8.883 \times 10^{13}) \times (2.5 \times 10^{-5})$
$= 1.66 \times 10^{-25} \times 8.883 \times 10^{13} \times 2.5 \times 10^{-5}$
$= 3.687 \times 10^{-16}$

Numerator: $4 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 200 = 1.28 \times 10^{-16}$

$$a \;=\; \frac{1.28 \times 10^{-16}}{3.687 \times 10^{-16}} \;\approx\; 0.347$$

**Compute q:**

$$q \;=\; \frac{2 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 1500}{3.687 \times 10^{-16}} \;=\; \frac{4.8 \times 10^{-16}}{3.687 \times 10^{-16}} \;\approx\; 1.302$$

**Stability comment:**
For a quadrupole, the *first stability region* in (a, q) space is approximately bounded by $0 < a < 0.236$ and $0 < q < 0.908$. The point (a ≈ 0.347, q ≈ 1.302) lies **outside** the first stability region, meaning *this particular (U, V) combination would not transmit the m/z = 100 ion stably* — the ion would hit the rods. In a real instrument, the operator adjusts U and V such that the operating point (a, q) falls *inside* the stable region for the desired m/z.

*(7 marks: 2 for ω, 2 each for a and q, 1 for stability comment.)*

---

### 5.1 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4 (Composition Analysis by Spectroscopic Methods)**

*   **Beer-Lambert Law** $A = \varepsilon b c$ is the foundation of *all* absorption spectroscopy. It is **linear in concentration** for dilute solutions, and **non-linear** (exponential in transmission) at high concentrations.
*   **Absorption** measures *what is removed* from the beam; **emission** measures *what is given off* by the sample; **mass spectrometry** measures *the mass of ionised fragments*. Three fundamentally different physical observables.
*   **Beer-Lambert requires monochromatic light** — a *narrow* bandwidth (1–2 nm) is essential; a broadband source gives non-quantitative results.
*   **IR absorption is selective** — only vibrations that change the molecular dipole moment are IR-active. This is why **CO₂** has a strong IR signature but **N₂** does not.
*   **Atomic emission wavelength:** $\lambda_{emit} = hc / \Delta E$. The *energy levels are unique* to each element → unique spectral fingerprint.
*   **Flame photometry** detects only the easily-excited alkali/alkaline-earth metals (Na, K, Li, Ca). For *all* other elements, **ICP-OES or ICP-MS** is required.
*   **Mass spectrometer has 4 essential stages:** inlet → ion source → mass analyser → detector. The **vacuum system** is the unsung 5th stage — without it ions cannot fly.
*   **Magnetic sector radius:** $r = \sqrt{2V(m/z)} \,/\, B$ — heavier ions curve less for fixed V, B; this is the physical basis of mass separation.
*   **Quadrupole operation:** Trajectory stability is determined by the Mathieu parameters (a, q); only ions in the stability region are transmitted.
*   **TOF mass analyser:** $t = L \sqrt{m / (2zV)}$ — lighter ions arrive first; the entire spectrum can be recorded in microseconds.
*   **Resolution** is the key performance metric for MS, not sensitivity. $R = M / \Delta M$ is the standard.
*   **Industrial applications** you must remember for KTU viva: *Beer-Lambert → pharmaceutical QC & water treatment*; *FTIR → polymer identification*; *ICP-OES → multi-element trace analysis*; *ICP-MS → isotopic ratio measurement*; *XRF → scrap-metal sorting*.
*   **Common unit conversions to memorise:** 1 nm = 10⁻⁹ m; 1 amu = 1.6605 × 10⁻²⁷ kg; 1 eV = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ J; wavenumber cm⁻¹ = 10⁷ / λ(nm).
*   **Pitfall to avoid:** Always check that absorbance **A** lies in the **0.1 to 1.0** range for accurate UV-Vis measurement. Outside this range, dilute the sample.
*   **Instrument architecture mantra:** *Source → Monochromator/Separator → Sample → Detector → Signal Processor.* This 5-block chain is the skeleton of *every* spectrometer — recognising it is half the battle in any KTU question.

> **Final study tip:** KTU examiners reward students who can *compare* methods and justify *why* a particular spectrometer is chosen for a given industrial application. Memorise the comparison table in Section 4.6 and you will answer 70% of the Part-B choice questions confidently.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analysis of Solids by X-Ray Diffraction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECHT724 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Analysis of Solids by X-Ray Diffraction # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **X-Ray Diffraction (XRD)** is a non-destructive analytical technique used to investigate the **crystallographic structure, phase composition, and physical properties** of solid materials by measuring the **constructive interference** (diffraction) patterns produced when a collimated beam of **monochromatic X-rays** interacts with the periodic atomic arrangements (lattice planes) inside a crystalline solid. The phenomenon is governed by **Bragg's Law**, mathematically expressed as: $$n\lambda = 2d_{hkl} \sin\theta$$ Where: - $n$ = Order of diffraction (a positive integer) - $\lambda$ = Wavelength of incident X-rays (typically **0.5–2.5 Å**) - $d_{hkl}$ = Interplanar spacing between crystallographic planes indexed by Miller indices $(hkl)$ - $\theta$ = Angle of incidence (Bragg angle) The technique was pioneered by **Max von Laue (1912)** and mathematically formalized by **William Henry Bragg and William Lawrence Bragg (1913)**, earning them the **Nobel Prize in Physics (1915)**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Echo Wall" Model > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine shouting at a tall, ornate picket fence. The spaces between the pickets act like the atomic planes in a crystal. Only at **specific angles** will your voice echo back clearly (constructive interference). Tilt your head even slightly, and the echo vanishes. X-ray diffraction works exactly the same way — **X-rays "echo" off the regular rows of atoms in a crystal only at precise angles**, and by measuring those angles, we can deduce the exact spacing between atoms. ## 1.3 Why X-Rays? The Wavelength Match Principle > [!IMPORTANT] > X-rays are the ideal probe for atomic-scale structures because their wavelength range (**0.1–10 Å**) is **commensurate with the interatomic distances in crystals (1–3 Å)**. Visible light ($\lambda \approx 4000\text{–}7000\ \text{Å}$) is too "long" to resolve atomic features — it simply passes around atoms as if they weren't there. | Property | Value | | :--- | :--- | | X-ray wavelength range | **0.1 – 10 Å** (0.01 – 1 nm) | | Typical Cu K$\alpha$ wavelength | **1.5406 Å** | | Typical interatomic distance | **1 – 3 Å** | | Photon energy (Cu K$\alpha$) | **8.05 keV** | | Speed of X-rays | **$3 \times 10^8$ m/s** (same as light) | ## 1.4 Generation of X-Rays X-rays used in diffraction are produced in a sealed **Coolidge-type X-ray tube** by two distinct mechanisms: 1. **Bremsstrahlung (Braking Radiation)** — Continuous spectrum produced when high-velocity electrons are abruptly decelerated upon striking a metal target (usually Cu, Mo, Co, or Cr). 2. **Characteristic X-rays** — Discrete sharp peaks produced when incident electrons knock out inner-shell (K-shell) electrons, and outer-shell electrons drop down to fill the vacancy, emitting photons of element-specific energies. > [!NOTE] > For Cu target, the dominant **K$\alpha$ line (1.5406 Å)** and weaker **K$\beta$ line (1.3922 Å)** are produced. A **monochromator** (often a graphite crystal or Ni filter) is used to isolate the K$\alpha$ line for diffraction work. --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Crystal Systems and Bravais Lattices > [!IMPORTANT] > All crystalline solids can be classified into **7 crystal systems** and **14 Bravais lattices**. The unit cell is described by 6 parameters: edge lengths $(a, b, c)$ and inter-axial angles $(\alpha, \beta, \gamma)$. | Crystal System | Lattice Parameters | Bravais Lattices | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Cubic** | $a = b = c$; $\alpha = \beta = \gamma = 90°$ | Simple (SC), BCC, FCC | | **Tetragonal** | $a = b \neq c$; $\alpha = \beta = \gamma = 90°$ | Simple, Body-centered | | **Orthorhombic** | $a \neq b \neq c$; $\alpha = \beta = \gamma = 90°$ | Simple, Base, Body, Face | | **Hexagonal** | $a = b \neq c$; $\alpha = \beta = 90°$, $\gamma = 120°$ | Simple | | **Trigonal (Rhombohedral)** | $a = b = c$; $\alpha = \beta = \gamma \neq 90°$ | Simple | | **Monoclinic** | $a \neq b \neq c$; $\alpha = \gamma = 90° \neq \beta$ | Simple, Base-centered | | **Triclinic** | $a \neq b \neq c$; $\alpha \neq \beta \neq \gamma$ | Simple | ## 2.2 Miller Indices — The "Address System" of Crystal Planes A crystal plane is denoted by Miller indices $(hkl)$, obtained by: 1. Finding the intercepts of the plane with the crystallographic axes (in units of $a, b, c$). 2. Taking reciprocals. 3. Clearing fractions to obtain the smallest integers. > [!NOTE] > **Negative indices** are denoted with an overbar, e.g., $(\bar{h}kl)$. **Curly braces** $\{hkl\}$ denote a *family of equivalent planes*; **square brackets** $[uvw]$ denote a *crystal direction*. ## 2.3 Interplanar Spacing ($d_{hkl}$) — The Heart of XRD The relationship between Miller indices, lattice parameters, and interplanar spacing $d_{hkl}$ depends on the crystal system. **These are HIGH-YIELD formulas for KTU exams:** | Crystal System | $d_{hkl}$ Formula | | :--- | :--- | | **Cubic** | $\dfrac{1}{d^2} = \dfrac{h^2 + k^2 + l^2}{a^2}$ | | **Tetragonal** | $\dfrac{1}{d^2} = \dfrac{h^2 + k^2}{a^2} + \dfrac{l^2}{c^2}$ | | **Orthorhombic** | $\dfrac{1}{d^2} = \dfrac{h^2}{a^2} + \dfrac{k^2}{b^2} + \dfrac{l^2}{c^2}$ | | **Hexagonal** | $\dfrac{1}{d^2} = \dfrac{4}{3}\left(\dfrac{h^2 + hk + k^2}{a^2}\right) + \dfrac{l^2}{c^2}$ | | **Monoclinic** | $\dfrac{1}{d^2} = \dfrac{1}{\sin^2\beta}\left(\dfrac{h^2}{a^2} + \dfrac{k^2\sin^2\beta}{b^2} + \dfrac{l^2}{c^2} - \dfrac{2hl\cos\beta}{ac}\right)$ | ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master these equations — they appear in nearly every KTU examination question on this topic.** | # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Typical Use | | :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | $n\lambda = 2d\sin\theta$ | **Bragg's Law** | Find $d$, $\lambda$, or $\theta$ | | 2 | $d_{hkl} = \dfrac{a}{\sqrt{h^2+k^2+l^2}}$ | Cubic interplanar spacing | Cubic crystals only | | 3 | $\sin^2\theta = \dfrac{\lambda^2}{4a^2}(h^2+k^2+l^2)$ | Cubic Bragg combination | Indexing cubic patterns | | 4 | $D = \dfrac{K\lambda}{\beta\cos\theta}$ | **Scherrer Equation** | Crystallite size | | 5 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\beta}{4\tan\theta}$ | Lattice strain | Strain analysis | | 6 | $F_{hkl} = \sum_j f_j \exp[2\pi i(hx_j + ky_j + lz_j)]$ | **Structure Factor** | Determining which peaks appear | | 7 | $\rho_{cal} = \dfrac{nM}{N_A V}$ | Crystallographic density | Density check | | 8 | $2\sin\theta = \dfrac{n\lambda}{d_{hkl}}$ | Rearranged Bragg form | Reciprocal space | Where: - $D$ = Crystallite size (nm) - $K$ = Shape factor (**Scherrer constant ≈ 0.9** for spherical crystals) - $\beta$ = Full Width at Half Maximum (FWHM) in **radians** - $\varepsilon$ = Microstrain - $F_{hkl}$ = Structure factor - $f_j$ = Atomic scattering factor of atom $j$ - $n$ = Atoms per unit cell - $M$ = Molar mass - $N_A$ = Avogadro's number ($6.022 \times 10^{23}$ mol$^{-1}$) - $V$ = Unit cell volume ## 2.5 Structure Factor — Why Some Peaks Disappear > [!NOTE] > The **structure factor** $F_{hkl}$ determines whether a reflection from a set of planes $(hkl)$ will be **observed** or **systematically absent**. **Selection Rules for Common Cubic Lattices:** | Lattice Type | Allowed Reflections | Condition | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Simple Cubic (SC)** | All $(hkl)$ | No restriction | | **BCC** | $(h + k + l)$ = even | Odd sums forbidden | | **FCC** | $(h, k, l)$ all odd OR all even | Mixed parity forbidden | | **HCP (Hexagonal)** | Various | See HCP-specific rules | **Example — FCC Copper (Cu):** - $(111)$ → allowed (all odd) ✓ - $(200)$ → allowed (all even) ✓ - $(110)$ → forbidden (mixed parity) ✗ - $(100)$ → forbidden (mixed parity) ✗ ## 2.6 Engineering Applications — Why This Matters > [!IMPORTANT] > XRD is the **gold-standard** analytical tool in: > - **Materials Science** — Phase identification (e.g., distinguishing austenite vs. martensite in steel) > - **Pharmaceuticals** — Detecting polymorphism in drug crystals > - **Cement Industry** — Quantitative phase analysis of clinker minerals > - **Semiconductors** — Verifying epitaxial layer quality > - **Geology/Mineralogy** — Mineral identification > - **Failure Analysis** — Identifying corrosion products, residual phases > - **Nanotechnology** — Crystallite size measurement via Scherrer equation --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Bragg's Law (Exhaustive) **Setup:** Consider a parallel beam of monochromatic X-rays of wavelength $\lambda$ striking a family of parallel, equally spaced crystallographic planes separated by distance $d$. Let the planes be indexed as $(hkl)$ and let $\theta$ be the angle between the incident ray and the plane. > [!NOTE] > **Note:** $\theta$ is measured from the **plane**, not from the **normal**. This convention is unique to crystallography (in optics, angles are usually measured from the normal). ### Step 1: Path Difference Between Adjacent Planes Consider two parallel rays **Ray 1** and **Ray 2** striking two consecutive planes at points **A** and **B**. - Ray 1 travels: From source to **A**, then reflects to detector. - Ray 2 travels: From source to **B** (must penetrate to the second plane), then reflects to detector. The **extra distance** traveled by Ray 2 compared to Ray 1 is the **path difference** $\Delta$. Drop perpendiculars from the point of reflection of Ray 1 to the path of Ray 2: - One perpendicular meets Ray 2 at point **P** (before reflection). - The other meets Ray 2 at point **Q** (after reflection). By the geometry of equal angles of incidence and reflection: $$\Delta = PB + BQ$$ ### Step 2: Geometric Relationship In triangle **PBA**, the angle at **A** equals $\theta$ (angle of incidence), and the side opposite this angle is $PB$, with hypotenuse $AB = d$: $$PB = d \sin\theta$$ By symmetry (same geometry on the exit side): $$BQ = d \sin\theta$$ Therefore, the **total path difference** is: $$\Delta = d \sin\theta + d \sin\theta = 2d \sin\theta$$ ### Step 3: Condition for Constructive Interference For the two reflected rays to constructively interfere (produce a bright diffraction peak), the path difference must equal an **integer number of wavelengths**: $$\boxed{\Delta = n\lambda}$$ ### Step 4: Combining the Results Setting $\Delta = 2d \sin\theta$ equal to $n\lambda$: $$\boxed{n\lambda = 2d \sin\theta}$$ This is **Bragg's Law** — the foundational equation of X-ray crystallography. $\blacksquare$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Physical Interpretation:** Diffraction only occurs when this equation is satisfied. At all other angles, the waves destructively interfere and no peak is observed. This is why XRD produces a **discrete pattern of peaks** rather than a continuous signal. ## 3.2 Derivation of the Scherrer Equation (Crystallite Size) ### Step 1: The Fundamental Concept In an **ideal infinite crystal**, diffraction peaks would be infinitely sharp (delta functions). However, in **real polycrystalline samples**, diffraction peaks are broadened. This broadening has two principal sources: 1. **Size broadening** — finite crystallite size (Scherrer effect) 2. **Strain broadening** — lattice distortions ### Step 2: The Diffraction Width-Size Relationship Consider a crystallite of finite size $D$. The path difference between X-rays scattered from the topmost and bottommost atomic planes produces a measurable broadening $\beta$ of the diffraction peak. The angular spread $\Delta(2\theta)$ is related to $D$ via: $$\beta_{size} = \dfrac{K\lambda}{D \cos\theta}$$ ### Step 3: Separating Size Broadening from Instrumental Broadening The measured peak FWHM $\beta_{measured}$ includes instrumental broadening $\beta_{instrumental}$. The true sample broadening is: $$\beta = \sqrt{\beta_{measured}^2 - \beta_{instrumental}^2}$$ (using the Warren correction, valid when both broadenings are Gaussian). ### Step 4: Final Form of the Scherrer Equation Rearranging for the crystallite size: $$\boxed{D = \dfrac{K\lambda}{\beta \cos\theta}}$$ Where: - $K$ = **Scherrer constant** (typically **0.89 to 0.94**, often taken as **0.9** for spherical crystallites) - $\lambda$ = X-ray wavelength (Å) - $\beta$ = FWHM of the diffraction peak (in **radians** — must convert from 2$\theta$ degrees if necessary) - $\theta$ = Bragg angle (in **radians**) - $D$ = Crystallite size (Å or nm) ### Step 5: Numerical Demonstration **Problem:** A powder XRD pattern of nano-crystalline alumina shows a peak at $2\theta = 45.86°$ with FWHM $\beta = 0.48°$ using Cu K$\alpha$ radiation ($\lambda = 1.5406$ Å, $K = 0.9$). Calculate the crystallite size. **Solution:** Convert $\beta$ to radians: $$\beta = 0.48° \times \dfrac{\pi}{180°} = 0.008378 \text{ rad}$$ Convert $2\theta$ to $\theta$: $$\theta = \dfrac{45.86°}{2} = 22.93° = 0.4002 \text{ rad}$$ Apply Scherrer equation: $$\begin{aligned} D &= \dfrac{K\lambda}{\beta \cos\theta} \\[4pt] &= \dfrac{0.9 \times 1.5406\ \text{Å}}{0.008378 \times \cos(22.93°)} \\[4pt] &= \dfrac{1.38654\ \text{Å}}{0.008378 \times 0.9211} \\[4pt] &= \dfrac{1.38654\ \text{Å}}{0.007717} \\[4pt] &= 179.7\ \text{Å} \approx 18\ \text{nm} \end{aligned}$$ **Result:** $D \approx 18$ nm — this is a typical value for nanocrystalline materials. ✓ ## 3.3 Derivation of Cubic Interplanar Spacing ### Step 1: General Expression For any crystal system, the interplanar spacing is derived from the **metric tensor** of the lattice. For the cubic system ($a = b = c$, $\alpha = \beta = \gamma = 90°$), this simplifies dramatically. ### Step 2: Cubic Metric Tensor The metric tensor $G$ for a cubic system is: $$G = \begin{pmatrix} a^2 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & a^2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & a^2 \end{pmatrix}$$ ### Step 3: Reciprocal Lattice Vector The reciprocal lattice vector $\mathbf{g}_{hkl}$ for a plane $(hkl)$ has magnitude: $$\vert \mathbf{g}_{hkl} \vert = \dfrac{1}{d_{hkl}}$$ ### Step 4: Magnitude Computation $$\begin{aligned} \vert \mathbf{g}_{hkl} \vert^2 &= \left(\dfrac{h}{a}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{k}{a}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{l}{a}\right)^2 \\[4pt] &= \dfrac{h^2 + k^2 + l^2}{a^2} \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 5: Final Result Taking the reciprocal: $$\boxed{d_{hkl} = \dfrac{a}{\sqrt{h^2 + k^2 + l^2}}}$$ Substituting into Bragg's law: $$n\lambda = \dfrac{2a\sin\theta}{\sqrt{h^2 + k^2 + l^2}}$$ Squaring both sides: $$n^2\lambda^2 = \dfrac{4a^2 \sin^2\theta}{h^2 + k^2 + l^2}$$ Rearranging (this is the **most useful form for cubic indexing**): $$\boxed{\sin^2\theta = \dfrac{\lambda^2}{4a^2}(h^2 + k^2 + l^2)}$$ > [!NOTE] > For FCC and BCC structures, this equation combined with the **selection rules** allows complete **indexing of all diffraction peaks** — a routine KTU exam problem. --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram of an X-Ray Powder Diffractometer ```mermaid flowchart LR node1["X-RAY SOURCE
Coolidge Tube
Cu Target K alpha 1.5406 Angstrom"] --> node2["PRIMARY MONOCHROMATOR
Ni Filter or Graphite Crystal"] node2 --> node3["SLIT SYSTEM
Divergence Slit
Receiving Slit"] node3 --> node4["SAMPLE STAGE
Flat Plate or
Bragg Brentano Geometry"] node4 --> node5["GONIOMETER
Theta Two Theta Scan"] node5 --> node6["DETECTOR
Scintillation Counter
or CCD Array"] node6 --> node7["DATA ACQUISITION
PC with XRD Software
Peak Search and Indexing"] node7 --> node8["OUTPUT
Diffractogram Intensity
versus Two Theta"] node8 -.feedback.-> node5 ``` ## 4.2 Flowchart: X-Ray Diffraction Analysis Procedure ```mermaid flowchart TD startA["START
Receive Solid Sample"] --> step1["STEP 1
Sample Preparation
Grind to Fine Powder
Particle Size less than 10 micrometer"] step1 --> step2["STEP 2
Mount Sample
in Specimen Holder
Flat Surface Pressed"] step2 --> step3["STEP 3
Insert into Diffractometer
Set Scan Parameters
Two Theta range and Step Size"] step3 --> step4["STEP 4
Acquire Diffractogram
Record Intensity
versus Two Theta"] step4 --> step5["STEP 5
Peak Search
Identify Two Theta
Positions of Peaks"] step5 --> step6["STEP 6
Index Peaks
Assign hkl Miller Indices
using Selection Rules"] step6 --> step7["STEP 7
Calculate Lattice
Parameters a b c
and Unit Cell Volume"] step7 --> step8["STEP 8
Determine Phase
Compare with ICDD
Powder Diffraction File"] step8 --> step9["STEP 9
Estimate Crystallite
Size via Scherrer
Equation"] step9 --> step10["STEP 10
Report Results
Phase ID plus Lattice
plus Crystallite Size"] ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram: Bragg Diffraction Geometry ```mermaid flowchart LR sourceA["INCIDENT X-RAY BEAM
Wavelength Lambda"] --> plane1["CRYSTAL PLANE 1
Top Layer"] sourceA --> plane2["CRYSTAL PLANE 2
Middle Layer"] sourceA --> plane3["CRYSTAL PLANE 3
Bottom Layer"] plane1 --> detect1["REFLECTED RAY 1
to Detector"] plane2 --> detect2["REFLECTED RAY 2
to Detector"] plane3 --> detect3["REFLECTED RAY 3
to Detector"] detect1 --> finalA["CONSTRUCTIVE
INTERFERENCE"] detect2 --> finalA detect3 --> finalA finalA --> finalB["PATH DIFFERENCE
2 d sin Theta = n Lambda
BRAGG CONDITION"] ``` ## 4.4 Comparison Block Diagram: Powder vs. Single-Crystal XRD ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph methodA["POWDER XRD METHOD"] direction LR pa1["Polycrystalline Sample"] --> pa2["Many Random Orientations"] --> pa3["Concentric Diffraction Cones"] --> pa4["Debye Scherrer Pattern
or Diffractogram"] end subgraph methodB["SINGLE CRYSTAL XRD"] direction LR pb1["Oriented Single Crystal"] --> pb2["Controlled Rotation"] --> pb3["Discrete Reflections"] --> pb4["3D Reciprocal Lattice Map"] end methodA --> compare["COMPARISON"] methodB --> compare compare --> finalA["Powder XRD: Faster, Simpler
Single Crystal XRD: More Detailed Structure"] ``` --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q: State and explain Bragg's Law. What is its significance in X-ray diffraction analysis?** **Model Answer:** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Bragg's Law** states that for a crystal with interplanar spacing $d$, X-rays of wavelength $\lambda$ will produce a constructive diffraction peak only when: > > $$n\lambda = 2d \sin\theta$$ > > where $n$ is a positive integer (order of diffraction) and $\theta$ is the angle between the incident ray and the crystal plane. **Significance:** 1. It provides the **fundamental relationship** between measurable angles ($\theta$), X-ray wavelength ($\lambda$), and crystal geometry ($d$). 2. By measuring $\theta$ experimentally, the **interplanar spacings $d_{hkl}$** can be calculated. 3. These spacings serve as a **"fingerprint"** to identify unknown crystalline phases via comparison with the **ICDD Powder Diffraction File**. 4. Combined with Miller indices, Bragg's law enables the determination of **lattice parameters** and **crystal structure**. --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q: Distinguish between Bremsstrahlung and Characteristic X-rays. Why is monochromatization necessary in XRD?** **Model Answer:** | Feature | Bremsstrahlung | Characteristic X-rays | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Origin** | Deceleration of electrons in target | Electron shell transitions | | **Spectrum** | Continuous (white radiation) | Discrete sharp peaks | | **Wavelength** | Spans a wide range | Element-specific (e.g., Cu K$\alpha$ = 1.5406 Å) | | **Intensity** | Generally weaker | Intense sharp lines | | **Used in XRD** | Filtered out | Selected and used | **Why Monochromatization is Necessary:** 1. Bragg's law requires a **single wavelength** ($\lambda$) — using white radiation would smear peaks and prevent precise $d$-spacing measurement. 2. The **K$\beta$ line** is typically removed using a **Ni filter** (for Cu K$\alpha$); the K$\alpha$ doublet (K$\alpha_1$ + K$\alpha_2$) is further resolved using a **crystal monochromator** (e.g., graphite or Si). 3. Monochromatic radiation ensures **sharp, indexable peaks** and high signal-to-noise ratio. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Internal Choice:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. --- ### ❖ QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]` **Q: (a) Derive Bragg's Law for X-ray diffraction. Explain its physical significance with a neat schematic. (7 Marks)** **Solution:** **Definition:** Bragg's Law is the fundamental equation governing X-ray diffraction by crystals, expressed as: $$n\lambda = 2d_{hkl}\sin\theta$$ **Derivation (as shown in Section 3.1):** Consider two parallel X-ray beams striking two consecutive crystal planes separated by distance $d$. The path difference between the rays reflected from the two planes is: $$\Delta = PB + BQ = d\sin\theta + d\sin\theta = 2d\sin\theta$$ For **constructive interference**, $\Delta = n\lambda$. Equating: $$\boxed{n\lambda = 2d_{hkl}\sin\theta}$$ **Physical Significance:** 1. **Discrete Peaks:** Only at specific Bragg-angles do diffraction maxima occur, producing the characteristic "fingerprint" XRD pattern. 2. **Crystal Structure Determination:** By measuring $\theta$ and knowing $\lambda$, we compute $d_{hkl}$, which reveals crystal symmetry and lattice parameters. 3. **Phase Identification:** Every crystalline phase has a unique set of $d$-spacings — comparison with ICDD database enables identification. 4. **Limitations:** Bragg's law applies only to **crystalline** materials; **amorphous solids** produce only broad halos, not sharp peaks. **Schematic Description (Bragg Geometry):** Two parallel crystal planes separated by $d$. Incident ray at angle $\theta$ to plane. Reflected rays emerge at angle $\theta$. Path difference between rays = $2d\sin\theta$. --- **Q: (b) With a neat block diagram, explain the working of an X-ray powder diffractometer. List any four applications of XRD. (7 Marks)** **Solution:** **Working Principle:** A modern powder diffractometer operates in **Bragg-Brentano parafocusing geometry**. The components and their functions are: | Block | Function | | :--- | :--- | | **X-ray Tube (Source)** | Generates Cu K$\alpha$ radiation (1.5406 Å) at 30–40 kV, 20–40 mA | | **K$\beta$ Filter (Ni foil)** | Absorbs K$\beta$ line, transmits K$\alpha$ | | **Slit System** | Collimates beam (divergence slit), defines resolution (receiving slit) | | **Sample Stage** | Flat powder specimen, often spinning to improve statistics | | **Goniometer** | Rotates sample by $\theta$ and detector by $2\theta$ simultaneously | | **Detector** | Scintillation counter or solid-state detector measures intensity | | **Recorder/Software** | Plots intensity vs. $2\theta$ to produce the diffractogram | **Working Sequence:** 1. X-rays are generated in the sealed tube. 2. The beam is filtered and collimated. 3. It strikes the sample at angle $\theta$. 4. The detector scans $2\theta$ from typically $5°$ to $90°$ or higher. 5. Whenever Bragg's law is satisfied for some $(hkl)$, a sharp peak is recorded. 6. The output is the **diffractogram**: intensity (y-axis) vs. $2\theta$ (x-axis). **Four Applications of XRD:** 1. **Phase Identification** — Compare measured $d_{hkl}$ values with ICDD PDF database. 2. **Crystallite Size Measurement** — Use the Scherrer equation: $D = K\lambda / (\beta\cos\theta)$. 3. **Lattice Parameter Determination** — From peak positions using cubic/tetragonal formulas. 4. **Residual Stress Analysis** — Measure peak shifts ($\Delta d/d$) as a function of $\sin^2\psi$. 5. **Quantitative Phase Analysis** — Relative intensities via Rietveld refinement. 6. **Polymorphism Detection in Pharmaceuticals** — Critical for drug efficacy and patent protection. --- ### ❖ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]` **Q: (a) Explain the different methods of X-ray diffraction analysis (Laue, Rotating Crystal, and Powder methods). Compare their applications. (7 Marks)** **Solution:** **1. Laue Method (Single Crystal, Stationary):** - **Setup:** A stationary single crystal is illuminated by **polychromatic (white) X-rays**. - **Principle:** Different wavelengths satisfy Bragg's law for different $(hkl)$ planes simultaneously. - **Pattern:** Spots arranged along **Laue cones** (or hyperbolas on flat film). - **Use:** Determining **crystal symmetry** and **orientation** of single crystals. - **Disadvantage:** Wavelengths are unknown → cannot directly compute $d$-spacing. **2. Rotating Crystal Method (Single Crystal, Monochromatic):** - **Setup:** A single crystal is rotated about a crystallographic axis while being struck by **monochromatic** X-rays. - **Principle:** As the crystal rotates, different $(hkl)$ planes successively satisfy Bragg's condition. - **Pattern:** Rows of spots on cylindrical film; layer lines spaced according to the rotation axis periodicity. - **Use:** Determining **complete crystal structure**, unit cell parameters, and space group. **3. Powder (Debye-Scherrer) Method (Polycrystalline, Monochromatic):** - **Setup:** A fine powder sample (or polycrystalline solid) is illuminated by **monochromatic** X-rays. - **Principle:** With millions of randomly oriented crystallites, every $(hkl)$ plane has some grains at the Bragg angle, producing **cones** of diffracted radiation. - **Pattern:** Concentric rings on film (Debye-Scherrer) OR peaks in diffractogram (Bragg-Brentano). - **Use:** Phase identification, lattice parameter refinement, crystallite size estimation — **most widely used in industry**. **Comparison Table:** | Feature | Laue | Rotating Crystal | Powder | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Sample type | Single crystal | Single crystal | Polycrystalline | | Radiation | Polychromatic | Monochromatic | Monochromatic | | Sample motion | None | Rotated | Usually none | | Information | Symmetry, orientation | Full structure | Phase ID, lattice | | Speed | Fast | Slow | Fast | --- **Q: (b) The X-ray powder pattern of a cubic metal shows diffraction peaks at the following $2\theta$ values (Cu K$\alpha$, $\lambda = 1.5406$ Å): 43.3°, 50.4°, 74.1°, 89.9°, 95.1°. Determine the crystal structure (SC, BCC, or FCC) and calculate the lattice parameter. (7 Marks)** **Solution:** **Step 1 — Calculate $\sin^2\theta$ for each peak:** | Peak | $2\theta$ (°) | $\theta$ (°) | $\sin\theta$ | $\sin^2\theta$ | | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | | 1 | 43.3 | 21.65 | 0.3691 | 0.1362 | | 2 | 50.4 | 25.20 | 0.4258 | 0.1813 | | 3 | 74.1 | 37.05 | 0.6029 | 0.3635 | | 4 | 89.9 | 44.95 | 0.7063 | 0.4988 | | 5 | 95.1 | 47.55 | 0.7381 | 0.5448 | **Step 2 — Compute the Ratios (Normalize to smallest value):** $$\frac{\sin^2\theta_n}{\sin^2\theta_1} = \frac{h^2+k^2+l^2}{h_1^2+k_1^2+l_1^2}$$ | Peak | $\sin^2\theta$ | Ratio | Integer Value (×3) | $(hkl)$ | | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- | | 1 | 0.1362 | 1.000 | **3** | $(111)$ | | 2 | 0.1813 | 1.331 | **4** | $(200)$ | | 3 | 0.3635 | 2.669 | **8** | $(220)$ | | 4 | 0.4988 | 3.663 | **11** | $(311)$ | | 5 | 0.5448 | 4.000 | **12** | $(222)$ | **Step 3 — Identify Crystal Structure:** The allowed $h^2+k^2+l^2$ values for the three cubic structures are: | Structure | Allowed $h^2+k^2+l^2$ | | :--- | :--- | | **SC** | 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 9, 10, 11, 12, ... (all) | | **BCC** | 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12, ... (even only) | | **FCC** | 3, 4, 8, 11, 12, 19, 20, ... (mixed parity rule) | Our sequence: **3, 4, 8, 11, 12** → matches **FCC** exactly! ✓ > [!IMPORTANT] > **This is the diffraction pattern of FCC Copper (Cu) or FCC Nickel (Ni)** — both have $a \approx 3.52$–$3.61$ Å. **Step 4 — Calculate Lattice Parameter:** Using Bragg's law for cubic system: $$a = \dfrac{\lambda}{2\sin\theta}\sqrt{h^2+k^2+l^2}$$ Using **Peak 1** $(111)$, $h^2+k^2+l^2 = 3$: $$\begin{aligned} a &= \dfrac{1.5406}{2 \times 0.3691}\sqrt{3} \\[4pt] &= \dfrac{1.5406}{0.7382} \times 1.7321 \\[4pt] &= 2.0869 \times 1.7321 \\[4pt] &= 3.6149\ \text{Å} \end{aligned}$$ **Verification using Peak 2** $(200)$: $$\begin{aligned} a &= \dfrac{1.5406}{2 \times 0.4258}\sqrt{4} \\[4pt] &= \dfrac{1.5406}{0.8516} \times 2 \\[4pt] &= 1.8092 \times 2 = 3.618\ \text{Å} \end{aligned}$$ **Average $a \approx 3.615$ Å** → consistent with **FCC structure**. ✓ **Answer:** The crystal is **Face-Centered Cubic (FCC)** with lattice parameter $a \approx 3.615$ Å. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert — Common Mistakes:** > 1. **Forgetting to convert $\beta$ to radians** in Scherrer equation — a single 1-mark deduction. > 2. **Using $\theta$ instead of $\cos\theta$** in Scherrer equation — full marks lost. > 3. **Confusing angle definitions**: $\theta$ is measured from the **plane**, not the **normal**, in crystallography. > 4. **Mixing up selection rules** for BCC vs. FCC — practice the $h^2+k^2+l^2$ sequences by heart. > 5. **Not including instrumental broadening correction** in Scherrer calculations (when specified in the problem). > 6. **In indexing cubic patterns**, students often forget that the **smallest** $\sin^2\theta$ value may not be 1 — must always normalize and check for integer ratios. > 7. **Scherrer constant** is typically $K = 0.9$; using $K = 1$ (wrong) loses 1 mark. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Bookmark these key points before every KTU exam on this topic:** ### 🔑 Foundational Concepts - ⭐ **Bragg's Law:** $n\lambda = 2d\sin\theta$ — the **MOST critical** formula. - ⭐ X-rays ($\lambda \approx 0.5\text{–}2.5$ Å) match atomic spacings → enables diffraction. - ⭐ Two X-ray generation mechanisms: **Bremsstrahlung** (continuous) + **Characteristic** (sharp K-lines). ### 🔑 Crystal Geometry - ⭐ 7 crystal systems, 14 Bravais lattices. - ⭐ Miller indices $(hkl)$ = reciprocals of intercepts (cleared to integers). - ⭐ Cubic $d$-spacing: $d_{hkl} = a / \sqrt{h^2 + k^2 + l^2}$. - ⭐ Cubic indexing equation: $\sin^2\theta = \dfrac{\lambda^2}{4a^2}(h^2 + k^2 + l^2)$. ### 🔑 Selection Rules (Critical for Indexing) - ⭐ **SC:** All $(hkl)$ allowed. - ⭐ **BCC:** Only $h + k + l$ = even. - ⭐ **FCC:** $h, k, l$ all odd OR all even. - ⭐ **Diamond Cubic:** FCC + additional rule ($h+k+l = 4n$). ### 🔑 XRD Instrumentation - ⭐ Components: X-ray tube → Monochromator → Slits → Sample → Goniometer → Detector → Recorder. - ⭐ Standard target: **Cu** ($\lambda_{K\alpha} = 1.5406$ Å). - ⭐ Bragg-Brentano geometry: sample rotates by $\theta$, detector by $2\theta$. ### 🔑 Three Classical Methods - ⭐ **Laue:** Single crystal + white radiation → symmetry study. - ⭐ **Rotating Crystal:** Single crystal + monochromatic → full structure. - ⭐ **Powder (Debye-Scherrer):** Polycrystal + monochromatic → phase ID (industry standard). ### 🔑 Scherrer Equation for Crystallite Size - ⭐ $D = K\lambda / (\beta \cos\theta)$ with $K \approx 0.9$. - ⭐ **$\beta$ must be in radians** (convert from degrees: × $\pi/180$). - ⭐ Valid only for crystallites $< 100$ nm typically. ### 🔑 Engineering Applications - ⭐ Phase identification, lattice parameters, crystallite size, residual stress, polymorphism, quantitative analysis. ### 🔑 Common Exam Pitfalls - ⭐ Always convert angles to **radians** for trigonometric functions in formulas. - ⭐ Use **$\cos\theta$** (not $\sin\theta$) in Scherrer equation. - ⭐ Double-check selection rule sequences: SC(1,2,3,4…), BCC(2,4,6,8…), FCC(3,4,8,11,12…). ---

topics.title: Gas Analysis by Thermal Conductivity, Polarography, and Chromatography
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT724 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Gas Analysis by Thermal Conductivity, Polarography, and Chromatography

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 MODULE 4 — GAS ANALYSIS BY THERMAL CONDUCTIVITY, POLAROGRAPHY, AND CHROMATOGRAPHY

## 1. Core Technical Definitions & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Gas Analysis — The Big Picture

**Gas Analysis** refers to the qualitative identification and quantitative determination of the components present in a gaseous (or vaporizable liquid) mixture. It is a critical sub-domain of **Industrial Process Analyzers** because almost every chemical plant — ammonia synthesis, hydrogen production, flue gas monitoring, petroleum refining, fermentation, and semiconductor fabrication — relies on continuous gas composition data for safety, efficiency, and product quality.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping (Module 4 Highlight):**
> The module specifically covers three classic analytical techniques: **Thermal Conductivity Analyzer (TCA)** for binary/multi-component gas streams, **Polarography** for dissolved ionic species (trace metals, oxygen), and **Chromatography** for separating and quantifying complex mixtures. Expect direct questions comparing principles, derivations of governing equations, and sketching instrument block diagrams.

---

### 1.2 Thermal Conductivity Analysis — Definition & Analogy

**Formal Definition (KTU Terminology):**
A *Thermal Conductivity Analyzer* (TCA) is a continuous, non-dispersive physical analyzer that exploits the difference in **thermal conductivity (k, measured in W·m⁻¹·K⁻¹)** between a sample gas and a reference gas. A heated sensing element (filament or thermistor) loses heat at a rate proportional to the surrounding gas's ability to conduct that heat, and the resulting temperature (hence resistance) change is measured using a **Wheatstone bridge** to yield the gas composition.

**Conceptual Analogy — "The Sweating Finger Test":**
Imagine dipping your finger into two cups of water — one icy cold, one lukewarm. The cold water "pulls heat" out of your skin faster than the warm water, so your finger feels colder in one and warmer in the other. A TCA works on exactly the same idea: a hot platinum wire loses heat quickly into **hydrogen** (thermal conductivity ≈ **0.182 W·m⁻¹·K⁻¹**) but loses heat slowly into **carbon dioxide** (thermal conductivity ≈ **0.017 W·m⁻¹·K⁻¹**). The faster the heat leaves, the cooler the wire, the lower its resistance — and that change in resistance is the analytical signal!

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constant:** The thermal conductivity of air at 0 °C is **k = 0.024 W·m⁻¹·K⁻¹**. Hydrogen has ~7× the conductivity of air, making it the easiest gas to detect by TCA.

---

### 1.3 Polarography — Definition & Analogy

**Formal Definition:**
*Polarography* is an **electroanalytical technique** introduced by Jaroslav Heyrovský (Nobel Prize, 1959) in 1922. It measures the **diffusion current (i_d)** flowing through an electrochemical cell as a function of the **applied DC potential (E)**, using a **Dropping Mercury Electrode (DME)** as the working electrode. The resulting **polarogram** (current–voltage curve) yields both the *identity* (via the **half-wave potential E₁/₂**) and the *concentration* (via the diffusion current) of electroactive species in solution.

**Conceptual Analogy — "The Salt-Water Voltage Test":**
Picture two electrodes immersed in a glass of water. If you slowly turn a knob to increase the voltage across them, nothing happens in pure water (no ions to carry charge). But add a pinch of salt — suddenly current flows at a specific voltage threshold. Each dissolved metal has its own unique "activation voltage" the way each key has a unique shape. Polarography listens for that "click" of current onset, records how big the click is, and from both pieces of information tells you *what* metal is present and *how much*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Mercury?** The DME is favoured because mercury forms **amalgams** with many metals, gives a **high hydrogen over-potential** (so it doesn't itself evolve H₂ and interfere), and provides a **constantly renewable, reproducible surface** — each new drop starts fresh, eliminating passivation problems.

---

### 1.4 Chromatography — Definition & Analogy

**Formal Definition:**
*Chromatography* (from Greek *chroma* = "color" + *graphein* = "to write") is a family of **separation techniques** in which the components of a mixture are partitioned between two immiscible phases: a **stationary phase** (fixed) and a **mobile phase** (moving). Components travel at different velocities because of differing affinities for the two phases, and are detected as they elute.

**Conceptual Analogy — "The Marathon with Sticky Shoes":**
Imagine 50 runners starting a marathon wearing shoes with different amounts of stickiness. Some have Teflon-coated soles (low affinity for the stationary surface), others have Velcro soles (high affinity). All start at the same line, but the Teflon runners zoom to the finish while the Velcro runners lag behind, picking up speed only where the surface is less sticky. A chromatographic column works identically: a tiny injected sample is swept by a carrier gas (mobile phase) through a long tube packed with coated particles (stationary phase). Different chemicals "stick" for different durations, so they emerge at different times, allowing both identification (by **retention time**) and quantification (by **peak area or height**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Classification Snapshot for KTU:** Paper, Thin-Layer (TLC), Column, Gas (GC), High-Performance Liquid (HPLC), Ion-Exchange, Size-Exclusion. In Module 4 of Industrial Instrumentation, the focus is overwhelmingly on **Gas Chromatography (GC)** since it interfaces directly with thermal-conductivity and flame-ionization detectors.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Thermal Conductivity Analyzer — Operational Logic

The TCA operates on the principle of **convective + conductive heat transfer** from a heated filament into a gas film surrounding it.

**Operational Steps (Why & How):**

- **Step 1 — Filament Heating:** A constant current (or constant voltage) is passed through a thin platinum (or tungsten, or nickel) filament, raising it to roughly **100–200 °C** above the cell wall.
- **Step 2 — Heat Loss by Gas:** Heat flows from filament → gas film → cell wall. The rate of heat loss is proportional to the **thermal conductivity (k)** of the gas. For a binary mixture, **k_mix ≈ x_A·k_A + (1 − x_A)·k_B** (Wassily equation approximation for low concentrations).
- **Step 3 — Resistance Change:** Lower filament temperature → lower electrical resistance (metals have positive temperature coefficient, **α ≈ 3.85 × 10⁻³ K⁻¹** for Pt). This change is tiny — typically **< 0.5 %** of nominal resistance.
- **Step 4 — Bridge Measurement:** The filament is one arm of a **Wheatstone bridge**. A *reference cell* (sealed with a known reference gas such as air or pure component) balances the *measurement cell* (through which sample flows). The bridge unbalance voltage is proportional to composition.
- **Step 5 — Signal Conditioning:** The differential millivolt output is amplified, linearized, and displayed as % concentration.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a Reference Cell?** Without a reference, ambient temperature and pressure drifts would swamp the analytical signal. The differential arrangement provides excellent common-mode rejection, making TCAs among the most stable industrial analyzers available (drift < 1 %/week typical).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Thermal Conductivity Analyzer

| # | Quantity / Symbol | Equation | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mixture thermal conductivity (Wassiljewa approx.) | $k_{mix} \approx x_A \, k_A + (1-x_A)\, k_B$ | W·m⁻¹·K⁻¹ | Linear approximation valid for dilute components |
| 2 | Heat loss rate from filament | $Q = \dfrac{k_{mix}\, A \, \Delta T}{\delta}$ | W | A = surface area, δ = film thickness |
| 3 | Filament resistance vs. temperature | $R_T = R_0 \, [1 + \alpha \, (T - T_0)]$ | Ω | α = temperature coefficient of resistance |
| 4 | Bridge unbalance voltage | $\Delta V \approx \dfrac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R}{R}$ | V | Linearized Wheatstone output |
| 5 | Sensitivity index | $S = \dfrac{k_{sample} - k_{ref}}{k_{ref}}$ | dimensionless | Larger S → easier measurement |

**Real-World Utility:** TCAs are the workhorse of **H₂ purity analyzers** in ammonia plants, **CO₂ meters** in fermentation, **SO₂ monitors** in sulfuric acid contact towers, and **binary refrigerant leak detectors** (R-12, R-22, R-134a vs. air).

---

## 2.3 Polarography — Operational Logic (DME Cell)

**Polarographic Cell Components:**
- **Working Electrode:** Dropping Mercury Electrode (DME) — mercury falling from a capillary (~0.03 mm bore) at a rate of 1 drop per 2–4 seconds.
- **Reference Electrode:** Saturated Calomel Electrode (SCE) or Ag/AgCl.
- **Counter Electrode:** Platinum wire or pool of mercury.
- **Supporting Electrolyte:** KCl (0.1–1 M) to suppress migration current.
- **Depolarizer:** The species under analysis (e.g., Pb²⁺, Cd²⁺, Zn²⁺).

**Operational Steps (Why & How):**

- **Step 1 — Drop Formation:** A new mercury drop forms every few seconds, giving a fresh, reproducible, hydrogen-free cathode surface.
- **Step 2 — Voltage Ramp:** A linearly increasing DC potential is applied between DME and reference (typical scan: 0 to −2.0 V).
- **Step 3 — Residual Current:** Before the analyte reduces, only a tiny charging current flows (capacitive current at the mercury–solution interface).
- **Step 4 — Decomposition Potential Reached:** When E reaches the **decomposition potential**, the metal ion begins reducing at the DME surface: $\text{M}^{n+} + n e^- + \text{Hg} \rightarrow \text{M(Hg)}$ (amalgam).
- **Step 5 — Diffusion-Limited Plateau:** Current rises sharply, then plateaus at the **diffusion current (i_d)** because analyte arrival at the electrode is now limited by diffusion through the depleted boundary layer.
- **Step 6 — Oscillographic Display:** On classical polarographs the current oscillates between minimum and maximum as each drop grows and falls — this characteristic "saw-tooth" is a hallmark of DME polarography.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Polarography

| # | Quantity / Symbol | Equation | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Ilkovič Equation (Diffusion Current)** | $i_d = 607 \, n \, D^{1/2} \, m^{2/3} \, t^{1/6} \, C$ | µA | **Most-tested KTU formula on this topic** |
| 2 | Diffusion coefficient (Stokes–Einstein) | $D = \dfrac{k_B \, T}{6 \pi \, \eta \, r}$ | m²·s⁻¹ | k_B = 1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K |
| 3 | Half-wave potential (reversible) | $E_{1/2} = E^0 + \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln \dfrac{D_R^{1/2}}{D_O^{1/2}}$ | V | Identifies the species |
| 4 | Nernst Equation (cell) | $E = E^0 - \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$ | V | Foundation of all polarography |
| 5 | Drop mass flow | $m = \dfrac{4}{3} \pi \, r^3 \, \rho_{Hg} \, f$ | mg·s⁻¹ | f = drop rate, ρ_Hg = 13.6 g/cm³ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constants used in Ilkovič:** 607 µA·L·mol⁻¹·cm²·mg⁻²/³·s⁻¹/² is the value when D is in cm²/s, m in mg/s, t in s, C in mmol/L. Students often forget this — convert everything to **CGS** before plugging in!

**Real-World Utility:** Polarography is the classical method for trace determination of **Pb, Cd, Cu, Zn, Ni, Co** in environmental water, blood lead analysis, oxygen in seawater (the *Heyrovský–Ilkovič* legacy), and pharmaceutical impurity profiling.

---

## 2.5 Chromatography — Operational Logic (GC Focus)

**Gas Chromatograph Building Blocks:**
1. **Carrier Gas Supply:** N₂, He, or H₂ (ultra-high purity) at constant flow/pressure.
2. **Sample Injector:** Split / splitless, on-column, or headspace.
3. **Column:** Packed (1–3 m, 2–4 mm i.d.) or Capillary (15–60 m, 0.1–0.5 mm i.d.) — the heart of separation.
4. **Oven (Thermostatted):** Isothermal or temperature-programmed (30 → 300 °C).
5. **Detector:** TCD, FID, ECD, MS — converts eluting component into electrical signal.
6. **Recorder / Integrator:** Produces the **chromatogram** — peaks vs. time.

**Operational Steps (Why & How):**

- **Step 1 — Injection:** µL of liquid or mL of gas is injected instantly into a heated port, vaporized, and swept by carrier gas onto the column.
- **Step 2 — Partition:** Inside the column, analytes distribute themselves between the stationary liquid film (e.g., SE-30, OV-101) and the moving gas. The **distribution constant K = C_s/C_m** governs retention.
- **Step 3 — Differential Migration:** Components with low K travel fast (small retention time t_R); components with high K travel slowly.
- **Step 4 — Detection:** Each component elutes as a Gaussian peak; the area under the peak ∝ mass injected.
- **Step 5 — Identification & Quantification:** Compare t_R with standards (qualitative); use peak area/height with calibration (quantitative).

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet — Chromatography

| # | Quantity / Symbol | Equation | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Retention time | $t_R = t_M + t_S$ | s | t_M = mobile-phase hold-up time |
| 2 | Retention factor (k′) | $k' = \dfrac{t_R - t_M}{t_M} = \dfrac{K}{\beta}$ | dimensionless | β = phase ratio (V_m/V_s) |
| 3 | Distribution constant | $K = \dfrac{C_s}{C_m}$ | — | Higher K → longer retention |
| 4 | Plate height (van Deemter) | $H = A + \dfrac{B}{u} + C \, u$ | cm | u = linear velocity of carrier |
| 5 | Number of theoretical plates | $N = 16 \, \left(\dfrac{t_R}{w}\right)^2 = 5.54 \,\left(\dfrac{t_R}{w_{1/2}}\right)^2$ | dimensionless | w = peak width at base |
| 6 | Resolution | $R_s = \dfrac{\sqrt{N}}{4} \cdot \dfrac{\alpha - 1}{\alpha} \cdot \dfrac{k'}{1 + k'}$ | — | α = selectivity, k′ = retention factor |
| 7 | Capacity factor relation | $k' = K \cdot \beta$ | — | β = V_m/V_s |
| 8 | Separation factor (selectivity) | $\alpha = \dfrac{k'_2}{k'_1}$ | — | α > 1 ⇒ separable |

**Real-World Utility:** GC is the gold standard for **petrochemical fingerprinting**, **blood alcohol analysis** (headspace GC-FID), **environmental VOC monitoring**, **flavor/fragrance profiling** in F&B, and **natural gas composition** (combined with TCA-style detection).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Wheatstone Bridge Output for a TCA

We begin with a Wheatstone bridge where all four arms nominally equal R, and the measurement-cell filament changes by ΔR due to gas-induced cooling. We want the bridge output voltage ΔV as a function of ΔR.

**Step 1 — Write the node voltages.**
Let the bridge be excited by a voltage V_ex across the top–bottom diagonal. The left node (between R and R + ΔR) and the right node (between R and R) are the output terminals.

$$V_L = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R}{R + (R + \Delta R)} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R}{2R + \Delta R}$$

**Step 2 — Similarly, the right node.**
Since both arms on the right side equal R:

$$V_R = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R}{R + R} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{1}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Differential output.**
The output is the difference:

$$\Delta V = V_L - V_R = V_{ex}\left[\frac{R}{2R + \Delta R} - \frac{1}{2}\right]$$

**Step 4 — Find a common denominator.**

$$\Delta V = V_{ex}\left[\frac{2R - (2R + \Delta R)}{2(2R + \Delta R)}\right] = V_{ex}\left[\frac{-\Delta R}{2(2R + \Delta R)}\right]$$

**Step 5 — Apply the small-signal approximation.**
Because ΔR ≪ R (typically ΔR/R < 10⁻²), we can replace (2R + ΔR) ≈ 2R in the denominator:

$$\Delta V \approx V_{ex}\left[\frac{-\Delta R}{4R}\right]$$

**Step 6 — Magnitude of output.**

$$\boxed{\;\Delta V \approx -\frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R}\;}$$

The negative sign simply indicates the polarity of the output; in practice we use a differential amplifier and label the output by magnitude. This is the foundational equation the KTU examiner expects in any TCA question.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Ilkovič Equation for Diffusion Current (Polarography)

The **Ilkovič equation** relates the measured diffusion current at a Dropping Mercury Electrode to the analyte concentration. We derive it from Fick's First Law of diffusion.

**Step 1 — State Fick's First Law for radial diffusion to a spherical drop.**
For a drop of radius r growing in time, the flux J of analyte arriving at the mercury surface is:

$$J = -D \cdot \frac{dC}{dr}\bigg|_{r = r_d}$$

**Step 2 — Integrate over the drop surface.**
The total molar flow rate N (mol/s) reaching the drop is the flux times the surface area:

$$\dot{N} = 4\pi r_d^2 \cdot D \cdot \frac{dC}{dr}\bigg|_{r = r_d}$$

**Step 3 — Relate to drop growth.**
For a drop growing under gravity from a capillary with flow rate m (mg/s), the drop mass at time t is m·t, hence:

$$\tfrac{4}{3}\pi r_d^3 \, \rho_{Hg} = m \, t \quad\Rightarrow\quad r_d = \left(\frac{3\,m\,t}{4\pi\,\rho_{Hg}}\right)^{1/3}$$

**Step 4 — Ilkovič's geometric approximation.**
Ilkovič assumed the diffusion layer thickness δ ≈ r_d (i.e., diffusion extends only over a thin shell comparable to the drop radius), and integrated accordingly, eventually substituting standard constants and converting units. The result in convenient units (i_d in µA, D in cm²/s, m in mg/s, t in s, C in mmol/L) is:

$$\boxed{\;i_d = 607 \, n \, D^{1/2} \, m^{2/3} \, t^{1/6} \, C\;}$$

**Step 5 — Final current expression with Faraday's constant.**
For completeness, the explicit SI form with Faraday constant F = 96 485 C/mol is:

$$i_d = 607 \, n \, D^{1/2} \, m^{2/3} \, t^{1/6} \, C \quad \text{(µA)}$$

**Step 6 — Practical use.**
- **Quantitative analysis:** Plot i_d vs. C → slope gives 607 n D^{1/2} m^{2/3} t^{1/6}, used as a calibration constant.
- **Qualitative analysis:** E₁/₂ at the half-current point is independent of C and identifies the ion.

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Van Deemter Equation (Chromatography)

The **plate height H** of a chromatographic column controls the broadening of peaks. The Van Deemter equation decomposes H into three independent contributions.

**Step 1 — Eddy diffusion term A.**
Different path lengths in a packed column cause broadening, depending only on the packing particle size d_p:

$$A = 2 \lambda \, d_p$$

where λ is a packing irregularity factor.

**Step 2 — Longitudinal (molecular) diffusion term B/u.**
Axial diffusion along the column contributes inversely with linear velocity u:

$$\frac{B}{u} = \frac{2 \gamma \, D_m}{u}$$

where γ is a tortuosity factor and D_m is the diffusion coefficient in the mobile phase.

**Step 3 — Mass-transfer resistance term C·u.**
Finite time for analyte to equilibrate between stationary and mobile phases contributes linearly with velocity:

$$C \, u = \left(\frac{k'}{(1+k')^2}\right) \cdot \frac{d_f^2}{D_s} \cdot u + \text{similar term for mobile phase}$$

where d_f is the stationary-phase film thickness and D_s the diffusion coefficient in the stationary phase.

**Step 4 — Total plate height.**
Adding the three independent contributions:

$$\boxed{\;H = A + \frac{B}{u} + C \, u\;}$$

**Step 5 — Optimal velocity.**
Differentiating H with respect to u and setting to zero gives the optimum carrier velocity:

$$u_{opt} = \sqrt{\frac{B}{C}}$$

**Step 6 — Engineering implication.**
Modern capillary columns minimize A (small d_p, regular packing) and C (very thin stationary film), pushing u_opt down to 20–30 cm/s and producing thousands of plates per meter.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Common Exam Table)

| Parameter | Thermal Conductivity | Polarography | Chromatography (GC) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Output signal** | Resistance / Voltage | Diffusion current (µA) | Peak area / height |
| **Transducer principle** | Thermal conduction | Electrochemical reduction | Partition + detection |
| **Typical accuracy** | ±1–2 % FS | ±2–5 % | ±0.5–2 % |
| **Selectivity** | Binary only (low) | Electroactive species | Very high |
| **Detection limit** | ~100 ppm | ~10⁻⁶ M (DME) | ~ppb (with FID/ECD) |
| **Sample state** | Gas only | Liquid solution | Gas or vaporisable liquid |
| **Major equation** | $\Delta V \approx \dfrac{V_{ex}}{4}\cdot\dfrac{\Delta R}{R}$ | $i_d = 607\,n\,D^{1/2}\,m^{2/3}\,t^{1/6}\,C$ | $R_s = \dfrac{\sqrt{N}}{4}\cdot\dfrac{\alpha-1}{\alpha}\cdot\dfrac{k'}{1+k'}$ |
| **Key inventor** | Daynes (1920) | Heyrovský (1922) | Tswett (1906), James–Martin (GC, 1952) |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Thermal Conductivity Analyzer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sampleGas[Sample Gas In] --> measCell[Measurement Cell with Hot Filament]
    refGas[Reference Gas Sealed] --> refCell[Reference Cell with Hot Filament]
    pwrSupply[Constant Current Supply] --> measCell
    pwrSupply --> refCell
    measCell --> bridgeNode[Input Arm of Wheatstone Bridge]
    refCell --> bridgeNode
    bridgeNode --> amp[Differential Amplifier]
    amp --> linearizer[Linearizer and Cold Junction Compensation]
    linearizer --> display[Indicator or Recorder in percent Concentration]
    measCell --> exhaustA[Sample Gas Out]
    refCell --> exhaustB[Sealed]
    classDef cell fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,color:#000
    classDef sig fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#000
    class measCell,refCell cell
    class bridgeNode,amp,linearizer,display sig
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- Yellow nodes represent the **sensing cells** (the heart of the analyzer).
- Blue nodes represent the **signal chain** — bridge, amplifier, linearizer, display.
- The constant-current supply drives *both* filaments so their cooling rates can be directly compared.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Polarographic Setup (Classical DME)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    dcSource[Programmable DC Ramp Source 0 to minus 2 Volt] --> cellInput[Applied Between DME and SCE]
    dme[Dropping Mercury Electrode DME] --> cellInput
    sce[Saturated Calomel Electrode SCE Reference] --> cellInput
    cellInput --> cell[Electrochemical Cell with Supporting Electrolyte and Analyte]
    cell --> currentMeas[Current Measuring Resistor and Amplifier]
    currentMeas --> recorderXY[X Y Recorder]
    dcSource --> recorderXY
    recorderXY --> polarogram[Polarogram Current vs Voltage Curve]
    dme --> dropFormer[Mercury Drop Forming Every 2 to 4 seconds]
    classDef elec fill:#fde68a,stroke:#92400e,color:#000
    classDef sig fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534,color:#000
    class dme,sce,cell,dropFormer elec
    class dcSource,currentMeas,recorderXY,polarogram sig
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- Yellow = **electrochemical subsystem** (electrodes, electrolyte).
- Green = **electronic subsystem** (source, recorder).
- The X-Y recorder receives **voltage** on its X-axis (from DC ramp) and **current** (after I-to-V conversion) on its Y-axis, producing the polarogram directly.

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Gas Chromatograph Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    carrier[Carrier Gas Cylinder He or N2] --> regulator[Pressure Regulator and Flow Controller]
    regulator --> injector[Heated Sample Injector]

    injector --> oven[Thermostatted Column Oven]
    oven --> column[Capillary or Packed Column with Stationary Phase]
    column --> detectorA[Detector TCD or FID or ECD]
    detectorA --> amp[Electrometer Amplifier]
    amp --> adc[Analog to Digital Converter]
    adc --> integrator[Digital Integrator and PC]
    integrator --> chromatogram[Chromatogram Peaks vs Time]

    class integrator,chromatogram out
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- **Blue flow path** = the mobile-phase / sample trajectory.
- **Pink detection path** = the transducer + electronics.
- **Green output** = the chromatogram — the final analytical record.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, April 2024 Style]**

### **Q1. With a neat sketch, explain the working of a thermal conductivity analyzer used for binary gas analysis.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
A TCA consists of a measurement cell and a reference cell, each containing a heated filament (typically platinum). A constant current is passed through both filaments. Sample gas flows through the measurement cell while a reference gas (or sealed air) is in the reference cell. Heat loss from each filament depends on the **thermal conductivity (k)** of the surrounding gas.

If the sample contains a component with thermal conductivity different from the reference, the measurement filament's temperature (and thus resistance) changes. The two filaments form adjacent arms of a **Wheatstone bridge**, producing an unbalanced voltage ΔV proportional to the difference in k, hence to composition. The output is amplified and displayed as percent concentration. Common applications: **H₂ in N₂, CO₂ in flue gas, SO₂ in contact process gas**.

---

### **Q2. Define polarography. Why is a dropping mercury electrode preferred as the working electrode?** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
Polarography is an electroanalytical technique that records the **current–voltage curve** (polarogram) at a polarizable micro-electrode to identify and quantify electroactive species in solution.

**Reasons for preferring DME:**
1. **Renewable surface** — every drop is fresh, eliminating passivation and giving reproducible measurements.
2. **High hydrogen over-potential** on mercury allows analysis in negative potential range (−0.1 to −2.0 V vs SCE) without H₂ interference.
3. Mercury forms **amalgams** with many metals (Pb, Cd, Cu, Zn), facilitating their reduction.
4. The dropping action gives a characteristic oscillating current that aids in identifying the diffusion plateau.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU End-Semester Exam — Module 4, 14-mark pattern, model paper]**

### **Question A — Module 4, Choice Option 1**

#### **(a) Derive the Ilkovič equation for diffusion current at a DME. Explain how it is used for both qualitative and quantitative analysis.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Statement of Fick's First Law.**
For radial diffusion toward a hemispherical electrode of effective radius r_d, the flux J is:

$$J = D \cdot \frac{C - C_0}{r_d}$$

where C is bulk concentration and C₀ is surface concentration.

**Step 2 — Mass transport equation.**
The instantaneous current i(t) flowing due to reduction of n-electron species is, by Faraday's law:

$$i(t) = n \, F \, A \, J = 4\pi n F D r_d \, (C - C_0)$$

**Step 3 — Drop radius in terms of time.**
For mercury flowing at mass rate m (mg/s) and density ρ_Hg = 13.6 g/cm³:

$$r_d = \left(\frac{3 m t}{4 \pi \rho_{Hg}}\right)^{1/3}$$

**Step 4 — Average current over drop life.**
Integrate i(t) over the drop lifetime t and divide by t to obtain the **mean diffusion current**. After unit conversions and substituting standard constants (with D in cm²/s, m in mg/s, t in s, C in mmol/L), the integrated form reduces to:

$$i_d = 607 \, n \, D^{1/2} \, m^{2/3} \, t^{1/6} \, C$$

**Step 5 — Qualitative use.**
The **half-wave potential E₁/₂** (potential at i = i_d/2) is independent of concentration and is characteristic of the ion under given electrolyte/temperature conditions. Matching E₁/₂ to literature tables identifies the species.

**Step 6 — Quantitative use.**
Since i_d ∝ C, a calibration plot of i_d vs. C is linear through the origin; unknown concentrations are read from the slope:

$$C_{unknown} = \frac{i_{d,unknown}}{607 \, n \, D^{1/2} \, m^{2/3} \, t^{1/6}}$$

**Conclusion:** Ilkovič's equation is the cornerstone of quantitative polarography, with the **607 constant** being a board-favourite memory item.

---

#### **(b) With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a Gas Chromatograph. List any three detectors used in GC.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram (already shown in SECTION 4.3) — Recap:**
1. **Carrier gas** (He / N₂ / H₂) from cylinder → pressure regulator → flow controller.
2. **Sample injector** (heated) — vaporizes µL of liquid or mL of gas.
3. **Column** — packed or capillary, housed in a **thermostatted oven** for isothermal or temperature-programmed operation.
4. **Detector** — TCD / FID / ECD / MS.
5. **Amplifier + ADC + Integrator + PC** — records the **chromatogram**.

**Working Principle:**
- A small sample is injected and swept by carrier gas through the column.
- Components partition between the **stationary phase** (liquid film on column wall or packing) and **mobile phase** (carrier gas).
- Components with higher affinity for the stationary phase have longer **retention times t_R**; those with low affinity elute earlier.
- Each component elutes as a Gaussian peak; peak area is proportional to mass.

**Three Common GC Detectors:**

| Detector | Principle | Best For |
|---|---|---|
| **TCD** (Thermal Conductivity Detector) | Measures change in thermal conductivity of carrier gas when analyte elutes | Universal, good for permanent gases and H₂ |
| **FID** (Flame Ionization Detector) | Hydrocarbons burned in H₂/air flame produce ions; current measured | Organic compounds, hydrocarbons (insensitive to CO₂, H₂O) |
| **ECD** (Electron Capture Detector) | Analyte captures electrons from β-source (⁶³Ni); current drop measured | Halogenated compounds, pesticides, PCBs |

**Conclusion:** GC is the most versatile separation technique in industrial analysis, with detector selection governed by analyte chemistry.

---

### **Question B — Module 4, Choice Option 2 (Internal Choice)

#### **(a) Explain the principle and operation of a thermal conductivity analyzer with a neat block diagram. Derive the bridge output equation.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Principle:** Different gases have different thermal conductivities (k). When a heated filament is exposed to a gas, the rate of heat loss — and hence the filament temperature and resistance — depends on k. By measuring the resistance change in a Wheatstone bridge arrangement, the composition of a binary gas mixture can be determined.

**Block Diagram (already shown in SECTION 4.1) — Operation:**

- Sample gas flows through the **measurement cell**; reference gas (sealed or flowing) in the **reference cell**.
- A constant current source heats both filaments to ~150 °C above cell wall.
- Heat loss depends on k_sample and k_ref → filament temperature differs → resistance differs → Wheatstone bridge produces ΔV.
- ΔV is amplified, linearized, and displayed.

**Derivation (Recap from §3.1):**

$$\Delta V \approx -\frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R}$$

Since R = R₀[1 + α(T − T₀)], and ΔR = R₀·α·ΔT where ΔT ∝ (k_ref − k_sample), the output ΔV is directly proportional to composition.

**Conclusion:** TCAs are simple, rugged, and ideal for binary gas streams like H₂/N₂, CO₂/air, SO₂/air.

---

#### **(b) Explain the van Deemter equation. How does it help in selecting the optimum carrier gas velocity in gas chromatography?** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**The van Deemter equation** describes how the **height equivalent to a theoretical plate (H)** varies with the linear velocity (u) of the carrier gas:

$$H = A + \frac{B}{u} + C \, u$$

where:
- **A = 2 λ d_p** — eddy diffusion (depends on packing particle size).
- **B/u** — longitudinal molecular diffusion (dominates at low u).
- **C·u** — resistance to mass transfer in stationary and mobile phases (dominates at high u).

**Origin of the three terms:**
- **A** arises because different molecules take different paths through the packed column.
- **B/u** comes from Fick's law of axial diffusion along the column axis.
- **C·u** arises because finite time is needed for analyte to equilibrate between phases.

**Optimum velocity:**
Differentiating H with respect to u:

$$\frac{dH}{du} = -\frac{B}{u^2} + C = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad u_{opt} = \sqrt{\frac{B}{C}}$$

**Engineering significance:**
- Operating at u_opt gives minimum H → maximum number of plates N → narrowest peaks → best resolution.
- Below u_opt, peaks broaden due to longitudinal diffusion.
- Above u_opt, peaks broaden due to mass-transfer resistance.
- Modern capillary columns minimize A and C, allowing fast analysis at lower u_opt (20–30 cm/s).

**Conclusion:** The van Deemter equation is the design compass of any chromatographer — column length, carrier gas choice (He, H₂, N₂), and temperature program are all tuned around it.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students most-commonly lose marks in Module 4:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units in the Ilkovič equation.** The constant 607 applies ONLY when D is in cm²/s, m in mg/s, t in s, C in mmol/L, and i_d is in µA. Plugging SI values gives wrong answers. **Always convert to CGS first.**
>
> 2. **Confusing E₁/₂ with E₀ (standard reduction potential).** E₁/₂ is the *experimentally observed* half-wave potential under given matrix conditions; it is *not* equal to E⁰ unless activity coefficients and junction potentials are zero. Don't conflate them.
>
> 3. **Drawing the Wheatstone bridge with the wrong labelling.** The measurement filament and reference filament must be on **opposite arms** of the bridge, not adjacent. Otherwise common-mode drift is not rejected.
>
> 4. **Stating that TCA measures "concentration" directly.** It measures the *difference in thermal conductivity*. Composition comes via the Wassiljewa-type mixing rule — be explicit about the assumption that k_mix is approximately linear in mole fraction.
>
> 5. **Mixing up retention time (t_R) with adjusted retention time (t_R′ = t_R − t_M).** KTU frequently asks the difference; t_M is the "dead time" for non-retained components like air.
>
> 6. **Forgetting the 1/4 factor in the bridge output derivation.** Many students write ΔV = V_ex · ΔR/(2R) — the correct small-signal gain is **V_ex/4**, not V_ex/2. This costs 1–2 marks per question.
>
> 7. **Not distinguishing between A and C terms of van Deemter.** A is from packing geometry; C is from mass-transfer kinetics. Examiners *expect* the physical origin of each term to be stated.

---

## 5.4 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> **A. Thermal Conductivity Analyzer**
> - ✔ Measures **difference in k** of gases using heated filaments.
> - ✔ Filament resistance change → **Wheatstone bridge** unbalance.
> - ✔ **ΔV ≈ (V_ex/4)(ΔR/R)** — the bridge gain is V_ex/4, not V_ex/2.
> - ✔ Reference cell compensates for ambient T, P drift.
> - ✔ Best for **binary** gas streams (H₂, CO₂, SO₂, refrigerants).
> - ✔ Typical accuracy ±1–2 % FS; low selectivity limits it to binaries.

> **B. Polarography**
> - ✔ Invented by **Heyrovský (1922)** — Nobel 1959.
> - ✔ Uses **Dropping Mercury Electrode (DME)**.
> - ✔ **Ilkovič equation: i_d = 607 n D^{1/2} m^{2/3} t^{1/6} C** (mind units!).
> - ✔ **Half-wave potential E₁/₂** identifies ion (qualitative).
> - ✔ **Diffusion current i_d** proportional to concentration (quantitative).
> - ✔ Mercury gives **high H₂ over-potential** and renewable surface.

> **C. Chromatography**
> - ✔ Coined by **Tswett (1906)**; modern GC by **James & Martin (1952)**.
> - ✔ Two phases: **stationary** (fixed) + **mobile** (moving).
> - ✔ **Retention time t_R = t_M + t_S**; **Retention factor k′ = (t_R − t_M)/t_M**.
> - ✔ **van Deemter: H = A + B/u + C·u**; optimum at u_opt = √(B/C).
> - ✔ **Resolution R_s** increases with √N, (α−1)/α, and k′/(1+k′).
> - ✔ Detectors: **TCD** (universal), **FID** (organics), **ECD** (halides).

> **D. Cross-Cutting Concepts**
> - ✔ All three are **analytical instruments** but on different principles: **thermal**, **electrochemical**, **chromatographic**.
> - ✔ All three produce an **electrical output** proportional (ideally linear) to the property being measured.
> - ✔ Module-4 questions often ask for **comparison** — keep the comparison matrix (§3.4) handy.
